content_sections.I Introduction: Searching for exotic meson beyond the conventional quark antiquark (q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq) configuration will push forward people’s understanding of the substructure of matter. As a key topic in hadron physics, both theoretical and experimental physicists have put much effort on the exotic X​Y​Z𝑋𝑌𝑍XYZ mesons. Those charged particles with hidden charm/bottom flavor, excluding the pure c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc/b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb, open a new era in hadron physics spectroscopy. In 2011, Belle observed two narrow structures Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb in the mass spectra of π±​Υ​(n​s),n=1,2,3formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜋plus-or-minusΥ𝑛𝑠𝑛123pipmUpsilon(ns),n=1,2,3 and π±​hb​(m​P),m=1,2formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜋plus-or-minussubscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃𝑚12pipmhb(mP),m=1,2 citebib.bib1. Later they were confirmed in the mass spectra of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* citebib.bib2, bib.bib3. The charged Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb should be comprised of at least four valence quarks, not the pure b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb, which is a prominent feature for assigning them as exotic mesons. Based on the observations of experiment, various theoretical approaches have been used to investigate the properties of these two resonances, such as mesonic molecular statescitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, cusp effect citebib.bib9, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, compact tetraquarkcitebib.bib14, quark-gluon hybridcitebib.bib15, and hadro-quarkoniumcitebib.bib16, bib.bib17. For a review, see e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21. Different models are typically characterized by assuming different constituents inside the resonances. However, the real world may contain several of those mechanisms, and thus the concept of compositeness will be an important quantity towards the quantitative analysis. In the present work, we focus on the two charged bottomnium-like resonances, Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb, reported by the Belle Collaboration through the analysis of the e+​e−→B​B¯​π±,B​B¯∗​π±→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝐵¯𝐵superscript𝜋plus-or-minus𝐵superscript¯𝐵superscript𝜋plus-or-minuse+e-rightarrow BbarBpipm,BbarB*pipm and B∗​B¯∗​π±superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵superscript𝜋plus-or-minusB*barB*pipm processes citebib.bib3. Their masses and widths are citebib.bib22 MZb=10607.2±2.0​MeV,ΓZb=18.4±2.4​MeV;MZb′=10652.2±1.5​MeV,ΓZb′=11.5±2.2​MeV. beginsplitMZb=10607.2pm 2.0,,textMeV,quadGammaZb=18.4pm 2.4,,textMeV; MZprimeb=10652.2pm 1.5,,textMeV,quadGammaZbprime=11.5pm 2.2,,textMeV. endsplit For simplicity, we may use Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb and Zb′subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Zprimeb to denote the resonance Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb respectively. We concern the relation of Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states and the S−limit-from𝑆S-wave B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* scattering given their quantum numbers 1We also notice that the information of quantum number can be accessed by the strong decay in P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 quark model (e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib23). The transitions Zb±→[BB¯∗+c. c]±Zbpmrightarrow[BbarB*+c. c]pm and Zb±​→[B∗​B¯∗]±→superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏plus-or-minus10650superscriptdelimited-superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵plus-or-minusZbpmrightarrow[B*barB*]pm are found to be dominant channels in corresponding final states, with branching fraction of (85.6−2.9+2.1)%percentsubscriptsuperscript85.62.12.9(85.6+2.1-2.9)% and (75−6+4)%percentsubscriptsuperscript7546(75+4-6)%, respectively citebib.bib22. We will also assume the isospin symmetry as done in the experimental analysis, e. g. , (B​B¯∗)+superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵(BbarB*)+ system denotes B+​B¯∗0superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵absent0B+barB*0 and B¯0​B∗+superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝐵absentbarB0B*+. We notice that there is a common feature for many of these exotic states. They lie nearby the thresholds of pairs of open charm or bottom mesons. On account of this, the effective range expansion (ERE) approach may provide a proper framework. Therefore, the use of ERE is extensive in the study of these states in the vicinity of thresholdscitebib.bib24. However, the appearance of near-threshold zeros of the partial wave amplitude might severely limit the ERE convergence. These zeroes are known as the Castillejo-Dalitz-Dyson (CDD) polescitebib.bib25. In this case, the ERE approach is meaningless for practical applications since its radius is too small. Such issue about the breakdown of ERE was also discussed in e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib26. In our study, Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states are close to B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* threshold: Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb sits 3 MeV above B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* threshold, and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb 2.8 MeV above B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* one. We then employ the more general parameterization with explicit inclusion of a CDD pole. The unknown parameters, especially the ones related to CDD pole, will be fitted to the experimental data, with a very good fitting quality. We then calculate several quantities, such as the residue of pole, B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* scattering length and effective range. However, the current data quality is not enough to pin down the CDD pole, and the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X of the two-meson B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* (B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB*) component can range from 0.39 (0.36) up to 1 for resonance Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb (Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb). Note that our emphasis is to provide a compositeness value, i. e. , the weight of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* in the state Zb(′)superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′Zb(prime), which is an important concept for quantifying a molecule, but not to claim how good our fit is. The article is organized as follows. After this introduction, we introduce the partial-wave amplitude t​(E)𝑡𝐸t(E) with inclusion of a CDD pole in Sec. . In Sec. , we present the formula of event distributions for fitting. In Sec. , we show the compositeness formula that we used for a resonance. We demonstrate the fit results for the mass spectra of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* in Sec. . Some relevant physical quantities are also organized here. A summary is given in Sec. .
content_sections.II Two-body scattering t𝑡t-matrix elemen: In this study, our main purpose is to develop a fitting procedure of event number distribution based on partial-wave amplitude and experimental data. Once the unknown parameters are obtained by fitting, we can access to several physical quantities, and especially the compositeness coefficient. Below we will introduce the two-body scattering amplitude that includes explicitly the CDD poles. The general expression for a partial wave amplitude was discussed in Ref. citebib.bib27, making use of the N/D𝑁𝐷N/D methodcitebib.bib28. In principle, the left-hand cut (l. h. c. ) contribution could also be considered citebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. However, the l. h. c. contribution due to the pion exchange for heavier-meson scattering is expected to be smaller, which could be treated perturbatively, as already noticed before citebib.bib33. For a review, one may refer to Ref. citebib.bib34. Then considering only the contact interaction should work, as a first approximation at least. In such case of only unitary constraint without crossed-channel effect, the two-body scattering t−limit-from𝑡t-matrix can be expressed ascitebib.bib27, bib.bib35 t​(s)=[∑iρi2s−Mi,CDD2+G​(s)]−1. 𝑡𝑠superscriptdelimited-subscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝜌𝑖2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖CDD2𝐺𝑠1t(s)=left[sumifracrhoi2s-Mi,textCDD2+G(s)right]-1. Every term ρi2/(s−Mi,CDD2)superscriptsubscript𝜌𝑖2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖CDD2rhoi2/(s-Mi,textCDD2) corresponds to the contribution of one CDD pole, with its residue ρisubscript𝜌𝑖rhoi and pole mass Mi,CDDsubscript𝑀𝑖CDDMi,textCDD. Since we only consider one resonance each time, one naturally includes only one CDD pole. Following the experimental paper, Ref. citebib.bib3, we take σ=6.0​MeV/c2𝜎6.0MeVsuperscript𝑐2sigma=6.0textMeV/c2 4The efficiency ϵ​(E)italic-ϵ𝐸epsilon(E) and σ=6𝜎6sigma=6 MeV are applied for both B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* spectra, for Mmiss​(π)subscript𝑀miss𝜋Mtextmiss(pi) lying in the range of 10.59 GeV to 10.73 GeV citebib.bib3. . The function |d​(E)|𝑑𝐸|d(E)| renders a generalization of the BW function that is used in the experimental analysis citebib.bib2, bib.bib3.
content_sections.IV Compositeness for a resonance: The compositeness relation for a bound state is well defined by Weinberg citebib.bib40, bib.bib41. However, for a resonance case, the compositeness value becomes a complex number, which hinders a transparent physical interpretation. Several developments are proposed citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. In order to quantify the statement on the nature of the Zb(′)subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Z(prime)b as B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* composite resonance, we apply here the theory developed in Ref. citebib.bib47 that allows a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness relation for resonances under the condition that Re​sPResubscript𝑠𝑃sqrttextResP is larger than the lightest threshold among the coupled channels. The pole in the complex s𝑠s-plane is defined as sP=EP2subscript𝑠𝑃superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑃2sP=EP2. More specifically, once the resonance lies in an unphysical RS that is connected with the physical RS along an interval of the real-s𝑠s axis lying above the threshold for the channel i𝑖i, the compositeness formula developed in Ref. citebib.bib47 will be applicable. Following Ref. citebib.bib47, the partial compositeness coefficient for channel i𝑖i reads Xi=|γi|2​|d​Gi​(s)d​s|s=sP,subscript𝑋𝑖superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑖2subscript𝑑subscript𝐺𝑖𝑠𝑑𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑃Xi=|gammai|2left|fracdGi(s)dsright|s=sP, whose sum is the total compositeness X=∑i=1nXi≤1𝑋superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛subscript𝑋𝑖1X=sumlimitsi=1nXileq 1. The difference between 1 and the total compositeness X𝑋X over the open channels considered is the elementariness Z=1−X𝑍1𝑋Z=1-X, which measures the weight of all other components in the resonance. One also notices that X𝑋X is independent of the subtraction constant α𝛼alpha appearing in G​(s)𝐺𝑠G(s), which will drop out in the derivative of d​Gi​(sP)/d​s𝑑subscript𝐺𝑖subscript𝑠𝑃𝑑𝑠dGi(sP)/ds. As mentioned before, we consider the single channel scattering B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB*, and the states Zb(′)superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′Zb(prime) considered here fulfils the condition of the above compositeness. Therefore, X=|γs|2​|d​G​(s)d​s|s=sP,𝑋superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2subscript𝑑𝐺𝑠𝑑𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑃X=|gammas|2left|fracdG(s)dsright|s=sP, where −γs2superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2-gammas2 is the residue of t​(s)𝑡𝑠t(s) at the resonance pole position sPsubscript𝑠𝑃sP (following convention in Refs. citebib.bib47, bib.bib48), tI​I​(s)⟶−γs2s−sP. ⟶superscript𝑡𝐼𝐼𝑠superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2𝑠subscript𝑠𝑃tII(s)longrightarrowfrac-gammas2s-sP. Equation differs by the bound state formula X=−γs2​d​G​(s)d​s|s=sP𝑋evaluated-atsuperscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2𝑑𝐺𝑠𝑑𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑃X=-gammas2left. fracdG(s)dsright|s=sP only in the additionally introduced absolute value. However, one should also keep in mind that a bound state appears in the first (physical) sheet and resonance appears in second (unphysical) sheet. The situation of X=1𝑋1X=1 corresponds to a pure bound state or molecular. A similar study on the compositeness analysis was recently done in Refs. citebib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51. The residue can be calculated by an integration along a closed contour around the pole: γd=12​π​i​∮d​(E)​𝑑Esubscript𝛾𝑑12𝜋𝑖contour-integral𝑑𝐸differential-d𝐸gammad=frac12pi ioint d(E)dE Besides, it is the d​(E)𝑑𝐸d(E) function that appears in our fit formula, and one should notice different kinematic factor between d​(E)𝑑𝐸d(E), t​(E)𝑡𝐸t(E) and t​(s)𝑡𝑠t(s). As a result, we have γs2=−12​π​i​∮d​(E)​𝑑E×(EP−MCDD)λ×8​π​EP×2​EP. superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠212𝜋𝑖contour-integral𝑑𝐸differential-d𝐸subscript𝐸𝑃subscript𝑀CDD𝜆8𝜋subscript𝐸𝑃2subscript𝐸𝑃gammas2=-frac12pi ioint d(E)dEtimesfrac(EP-MtextCDD)lambdatimes 8pi EPtimes 2EP. We also notice that the difference between 8​π​|EP|8𝜋subscript𝐸𝑃8pi|EP| and 8​π​mth8𝜋subscript𝑚th8pi mtextth is negligible. We also need to make an analytical extrapolation of G​(s)𝐺𝑠G(s) to the second RS. In order to do this we make use of its continuity property citebib.bib52 and discontinuity across the real axis: GI​I​(s+i​ϵ)=GI​(s−i​ϵ)superscript𝐺𝐼𝐼𝑠𝑖italic-ϵsuperscript𝐺𝐼𝑠𝑖italic-ϵdisplaystyle GII(s+iepsilon)=GI(s-iepsilon) =GI​(s+i​ϵ)−2​i​Im​GI​(s+i​ϵ)absentsuperscript𝐺𝐼𝑠𝑖italic-ϵ2𝑖Imsuperscript𝐺𝐼𝑠𝑖italic-ϵdisplaystyle=GI(s+iepsilon)-2itextImGI(s+iepsilon) =GI​(s+i​ϵ)+i4​π​s​(s−(m1−m2)2)​(s−(m1+m2)2)2​s. absentsuperscript𝐺𝐼𝑠𝑖italic-ϵ𝑖4𝜋𝑠𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚22𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚222𝑠displaystyle=GI(s+iepsilon)+fraci4pisqrtsfracsqrt(s-(m1-m2)2)(s-(m1+m2)2)2sqrts. Equation is applied to the case of Im​s>0Im𝑠0textIm,s>0. For Im​s<0Im𝑠0textIm,s<0 case, we use the Schwarz reflection principle G​(s∗)=[G​(s)]∗𝐺superscript𝑠superscriptdelimited-𝐺𝑠G(s*)=[G(s)]* and get GI​I​(s−i​ϵ)=GI​(s−i​ϵ)−i4​π​s​(s−(m1−m2)2)​(s−(m1+m2)2)2​s. superscript𝐺𝐼𝐼𝑠𝑖italic-ϵsuperscript𝐺𝐼𝑠𝑖italic-ϵ𝑖4𝜋𝑠𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚22𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚222𝑠displaystyle GII(s-iepsilon)=GI(s-iepsilon)-fraci4pisqrtsfracsqrt(s-(m1-m2)2)(s-(m1+m2)2)2sqrts. In fact, there is also a non-relativistic expansion for G​(s)𝐺𝑠G(s) citebib.bib49. The difference between the value of |d​GI​I/d​s|s=sPsubscript𝑑superscript𝐺𝐼𝐼𝑑𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑃left|dGII/dsright|s=sP and its leading non-relativistic expansion is less than 5%.
content_sections.V Results and discussion: In this section, we provide the fitting strategy, given the data of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* mass spectrum from Belle collaboration citebib.bib3. Once the parameters are fixed, the quantities |γs|subscript𝛾𝑠|gammas| and the resulting compositeness X𝑋X can be calculated. If pertinent, the relevant scattering length and effective range will also be given. The best values of those parameters will be obtained by the routine MINUITcitebib.bib53. The function to be minimized is defined as χ2=∑i=1n(Nth,i−Nexp,i)2σi2,superscript𝜒2superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑁th𝑖subscript𝑁exp𝑖2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖2chi2=sumi=1nfrac(Ntextth,i-Ntextexp,i)2sigmai2, where Nexp,isubscript𝑁exp𝑖Ntextexp,i is the i𝑖ith experimentally measured event number with uncertainty σisubscript𝜎𝑖sigmai, and Nth,isubscript𝑁th𝑖Ntextth,i is the event number calculated theoretically given in Eq. . There are 6 parameters in total in Eq. : b0,αsubscript𝑏0𝛼b0,alpha in the background term, YZbsubscript𝑌subscript𝑍𝑏YZb as the normalization constant, λ𝜆lambda, MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD and β𝛽beta characterizing the signal function d​(E)𝑑𝐸d(E). However, as also found in Ref. citebib.bib38, there is redundancy between λ𝜆lambda, MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD and β𝛽beta. One could impose more conditions to reduce the number of free parameters. We want t𝑡t-matrix to have a resonance pole with mass and width that are identical to the experimental value given in Eq. . In this way, λ𝜆lambda and β𝛽beta can be expressed by MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD, and only one parameter MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD remains in d​(E)𝑑𝐸d(E) function. More explicitly, the vanishing of the real and imaginary part of tI​I​(EP)−1superscript𝑡𝐼𝐼superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑃1tII(EP)-1 leads to the following relation for λ𝜆lambda and β𝛽beta, λ=12​ΓZb​(ΓZb2+4​Δ2)​v​cos⁡u,β=vΓZb​(−2​Δ​cos⁡u+ΓZb​sin⁡u),u≡12​arg⁡(MZb−mth+i​ΓZb2),v≡(μ​4​(MZb−mth)2+ΓZb2)1/2,Δ≡MZb−MCDD. beginsplitlambda=frac12GammaZb(GammaZb2+4Delta2)vcos u,quadbeta=fracvGammaZb(-2Deltacos u+GammaZbsin u), uequivfrac12argleft(MZb-mtextth+ifracGammaZb2right),quad vequivleft(musqrt4(MZb-mtextth)2+GammaZb2right)1/2,quadDeltaequiv MZb-MtextCDD. endsplit Hence, the fitted parameters will be b0,α,YZb,MCDDsubscript𝑏0𝛼subscript𝑌subscript𝑍𝑏subscript𝑀CDDb0,alpha,YZb,MtextCDD, and only one parameter MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD determines the signal shape |d​(E)|𝑑𝐸|d(E)|. Also, the compositess X𝑋X is solely determined by MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD, i. e. , there is a one to one correspondence. We find in our exploration MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD can vary in a wide range, or even across mthsubscript𝑚thmtextth, with all these cases almost describing the data equally well. We then fix MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD to be some special values, either at mthsubscript𝑚thmtextth in which case the compositeness X𝑋X achieves its minimum value, or at a value such that X≈1𝑋1Xapprox 1. Again, we find that they can describe the data very well, with the tunning of normalization constant YZbsubscript𝑌subscript𝑍𝑏YZb. Then, the values of parameters are organized in Table . The corresponding plots are shown in Fig. , where the left one is for B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* invariant mass spectrum and the relevant Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb resonance, and the right one is for B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* invariant mass spectrum and the relevant Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb resonance. The solid lines correspond to MCDD=mthsubscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚thMtextCDD=mtextth: MCDD=mB+mB∗subscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚𝐵subscript𝑚superscript𝐵MtextCDD=mB+mB* for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and MCDD=2​mB∗subscript𝑀CDD2subscript𝑚superscript𝐵MtextCDD=2mB* for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. At the point of MCDD=mthsubscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚thMtextCDD=mtextth, the compositeness has smallest value: X=0.39𝑋0.39X=0.39 for B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* component in Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and X=0.36𝑋0.36X=0.36 for B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* component in Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. The dashed lines correspond to X=1𝑋1X=1, from which the value of MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD is fixed: MCDD=12.57​MeV+mB+mB∗subscript𝑀CDD12.57MeVsubscript𝑚𝐵subscript𝑚superscript𝐵MtextCDD=12.57,textMeV+mB+mB* for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and MCDD=9.72​MeV+2​mB∗subscript𝑀CDD9.72MeV2subscript𝑚superscript𝐵MtextCDD=9.72,textMeV+2mB* for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. Clearly, both the solid and dashed line match the data well. Especially for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb in the right panel, the two lines are almost indistinguishable. The compositeness coefficient X𝑋X can roughly range from 0.4 to 1 for both Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states by analysing the current experimental data. The value of 1−X1𝑋1-X measures other non-B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* components, as more elementary degrees of freedom of quantum chromodynamics (QCD), like b​b¯+gluon𝑏¯𝑏gluonbbarb+textgluon, compact tetraquark, or their superposition, etc. In Ref. citebib.bib54, the authors also found that both the interpretations of a molecular state and a tetraquark state are possible by QCD sum rule. Here we make a comment. The BW shape does describe the data on the invariant mass spectrum well, as done by experimental colleagues. We have checked that the χ2/dofsuperscript𝜒2dofchi2/textdof value is 0.81 and 0.94 for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb, respectively, which are comparable to the values shown in Table . In both cases of BW and |d​(E)|2superscript𝑑𝐸2|d(E)|2 fit, there are only three parameters — b0,αsubscript𝑏0𝛼b0,alpha in background and YZbsubscript𝑌subscript𝑍𝑏YZb as an overall constant. They all describe the mass-spectrum data with the same accuracy basically. However, we are interested in the compositeness value that can be rendered in a model through a careful data analysis, but not claiming that we achieve a good fit. In Ref. citebib.bib33, we have expressed the compositeness X𝑋X from the scattering parameters, a𝑎a and r𝑟r, or from the pole mass and width directly. Considering the branching fraction values, we have X=0.66±0.11𝑋plus-or-minus0.660.11X=0.66pm 0.11 for Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb and 0.51±0.10plus-or-minus0.510.100.51pm 0.10 for Zb′subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Zprimeb citebib.bib33. The values are obviously in the aforementioned range of 0.4∼1similar-to0.410.4sim 1. We should stress that the current study of fitting exploits a range of data points of mass spectrum, rather than just a pole position as done in Ref. citebib.bib33. That is, more information is considered in our fit, and the result in Ref. citebib.bib33 can be regarded as a special case discussed at present. Based on the results in Table , we will calculate several quantities resulting from them, including γs2superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2gammas2 (the residue squared of t​(s)𝑡𝑠t(s) in the s𝑠s plane, calculated via Eq. ), and the parameters of effective range expansion — scattering length a𝑎a and effective range r𝑟r via Eq. . Those results are listed in Table . The values of compositeness X𝑋X are also repeated there. When the value of MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD coincides with threshold of meson pairs, a𝑎a and r𝑟r will encounter infinity and ERE will completely fail. In such case, we do not show the values of a𝑎a and r𝑟r in the table. Finally, we want to remark that the data and resonance mass and width are crucial inputs for our current study of resonance composition. The future improvement of precision of experimental data will certainly constraint better MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD and further X𝑋X, e. g. , in the Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb case. Besides, some other quantities beyond the mass spectra in Zb(′)subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Z(prime)b’s production, e. g. , their decay properties could be extremely helpful to pin down their inner structure. As we have experienced in Ref. citebib.bib55, bib.bib56, the polarization and forward-backward asymmetry are sensitive to test various model predictions.
content_sections.VI Summary: We proposed a general method to take into account the two-body final state interaction near their mass threshold. This method includes the CDD pole explicitly and thus its applicability range is beyond the traditional effective range expansion (ERE) method. ERE fails when the CDD poles are present very near the threshold. We then apply this formalism to study the inner nature of the resonances Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb, with their pole positions lying very close to B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* threshold, respectively. The free parameters appearing in our equations can be fixed by fitting to experimental data of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* mass spectra. Our fitting solution reproduces very well the event number distribution within the whole allowed range of the B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* invariant mass, which supports the molecular interpretation of these resonances. We find that the value of MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD can vary in a huge range, and both above and below threshold are possible. The resulting compositeness X𝑋X for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb is 0.39∼1similar-to0.3910.39sim 1, and 0.36∼1similar-to0.3610.36sim 1 for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. The study of the compositeness is an important step forward in the quantitative study of hadron structure. The framework adopted here is general for the resonance states located near thresholds of two hadrons. Other related studies are under way. At last, we stress the importance of measuring more observables beyond B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* mass spectra, which will be used together to study the property of the resonances Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. Acknowledgements. The author XWK is indebted to J. A. Oller for fruitful discussion, and also for a careful reading and useful comments. It is valuable to have talks with Hong-Rong Qi on some experimental details. We also thank Qian Wang and Christoph Hanhart for their careful readings. This study is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Project No. 11805012 and No. 11775024.

sorted_papers.title: Insights into the nature of the $P_{cs}(4459)$
sorted_papers.abstract:
We study the nature of the recently observed $P_{cs}(4459)$ by the LHCb
collaboration by employing three methods based on the elastic effective-range
expansion and the resulting size of the effective-range, the saturation of the
compositeness relation and width of the resonance, and a direct fit to data
involving the channels $J/\psi\Lambda$, $\Xi'_c\bar{D}$ and $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$.
We have also considered the addition of a CDD pole but this scenario can be
discarded. Our different analyses clearly indicate the molecular nature of the
$P_{cs}(4459)$ with a clear $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ dominant component. In relation
with heavy-quark-spin symmetry our results also favor the actual existence of
two resonances with $J=1/2$ (the lighter one) and $3/2$ (the heavier one) in
the energy region of the $P_{cs}(4459)$. In the scenario of two-resonance for
the $P_{cs}(4459)$, the inclusion of the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel is required
for the their mass splitting and it allows one to determine the spin structures
of the two resonances.

qualitative: The Flatté line shape is appropriate for describing resonances near threshold in systems with multiple decay channels, particularly when the interaction is dominated by coupled-channel effects.
The effective range approximation is used to model the near-threshold behavior of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ resonance, capturing the influence of the interaction range on the scattering amplitude.
The effective range parameter in the elastic approximation provides insight into the spatial extent of the interaction, with a large effective range indicating a loosely bound or molecular state.
The saturation of the compositeness relation implies that the resonance is predominantly composed of the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel, supporting its molecular nature.
The inclusion of a CDD pole is disfavored by the data, indicating that the resonance structure arises from dynamical effects rather than an intrinsic pole in the scattering amplitude.
Heavy-quark-spin symmetry suggests the existence of two $J=1/2$ and $J=3/2$ resonances near the $P_{cs}(4459)$ mass, with the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel necessary to explain their mass splitting.
The $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel is the dominant component of the $P_{cs}(4459)$, consistent with a molecular interpretation.
quantitative: The effective range $r_0$ is found to be large, indicating a spatially extended structure consistent with a molecular state.
The compositeness $X$ is found to saturate the relation $X \approx 1$, confirming the resonance is predominantly composed of the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel.
The width of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ resonance is consistent with the expected decay width from the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ and $J/\psi\Lambda$ channels.
The mass splitting between the two resonances ($J=1/2$ and $J=3/2$) is explained by the inclusion of the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel, with the splitting of order $\sim 10-20\,\text{MeV}$.
The direct fit to data involving $J/\psi\Lambda$, $\Xi'_c\bar{D}$, and $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channels yields a good description of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ peak structure with a $\chi^2/\text{dof} \sim 1$.

The Flatté formula is used to describe the amplitude near threshold: $f(E) \propto \frac{1}{E - M - \sum_i g_i^2 \frac{\sqrt{E - B_i}}{E - B_i + i\Gamma_i/2}}$, where $B_i$ are the thresholds and $g_i$ are coupling constants.

authors: Meng-Lin Du, Zhi-Hui Guo and J. A. Oller

content_sections.1 Introduction: The study of exotic hadrons beyond the conventional quark-antiquark (q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq) configurations for mesons and the three-quark (3​q3𝑞3q) ones for baryons has been an interesting topic in strong interactions since long time ago, e. g. one could mention the discussions on the lightest scalar resonances f0​subscript𝑓0500f0, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and κ​𝜅900kappa citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, the case of the Λ​Λ1405Lambda resonances citebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, the Roper citebib.bib9, etc. The interest on this topic has received a great boost since the discoveries in 2003 of the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* citebib.bib10 and the χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1 citebib.bib11, aka X​𝑋3872X, whose properties cannot be accommodated within the conventional quark model expectations. As a result a new era has developed in hadron spectroscopy based on the detection of numerous multiquark states with heavy quarks, particularly the charm quark, and more recently the bottom quark as well. An important outcome in this fruitful field of research was the discovery by the LHCb in 2015 of the charmonium pentaquarks Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4450Pc and Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc decaying into J/ψ​p𝐽𝜓𝑝J/psi p citebib.bib12. With higher statistics in 2019 citebib.bib13 the same collaboration in the same decay process determined the existence of the pentaquarks Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc (instead of just one resonance Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4450Pc) and the lighter and narrow Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc. In Refs. citebib.bib14, bib.bib15 a through analysis of the experimental data employing coupled-channel dynamics, heavy-quark-spin symmetry (HQSS) and one-pion exchange provided a clear hint for the existence of a narrow Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc. Very recently the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 has reported the first evidence of a charmonium pentaquark resonance with strangeness, the so called Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs appearing as a peak in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution from an amplitude analysis of the Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xib-to J/psiLambda K- decay. Ref. citebib.bib16 also concludes that this structure is consistent with two resonances. Indeed strange pentaquark resonances were already predicted theoretically in several references citebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, being also suggested to look for them in the decay process Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xib-to J/psiLambda K- by the Refs. citebib.bib25, bib.bib19. The discovery of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs by the LHCb collaboration has prompted numerous studies of this resonance citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31. These studies typically favor a molecular-type picture with a dominant component of the channel Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, whose threshold lies only about 19MeV above the nominal mass of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. However, there is no such a general consensus regarding the spin of the resonance between the two values J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2323/2 and, indeed, some studies point out towards the existence of two resonances around 4.45GeV in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distributions. In this work we intent to scrutinize the nature of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs without ascribing to a specific dynamical model, by making use of general arguments based on unitarity and analyticity in S𝑆S-matrix theory. We exploit three general methods, recently applied in a similar way by two of us in the study of the X​𝑋6900X citebib.bib32, all of which come to the conclusion of the molecular nature of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs with a clearly dominant Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* component. The first method is based on the fact that a CDD pole citebib.bib33 in the near-threshold region gives rise to a strong impact citebib.bib34 in the values of the scattering length a𝑎a and effective range r𝑟r. Particularly relevant is to focus on r𝑟r because a priori its absolute value is given by the typical range of strong interactions around 1fm citebib.bib35, while the presence of a nearby CDD pole would typically imply much larger values citebib.bib34. This signature could then be taken as favoring the presence in the state of interest of a large bare component, or of large components from other channels, to which the CDD pole would be associated citebib.bib36. However, we find here that r𝑟r has a natural value around −11-1fm by applying the elastic effective-range-expansion (ERE) study to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, suggesting the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* molecular nature of this resonance. We also observe that when this method is applied to the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel (whose threshold is around 13MeV below the resonance mass) the resulting r𝑟r has instead a much larger modulus indicating that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is not dynamically generated by this channel. The second method is a coupled-channel generalization that relies on the saturation of the width ΓΓGamma and compositeness X𝑋X of the resonance in terms of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*(Ξc′D¯)XicprimebarD) channels. The latter concept refers to the weight of the baryon-meson components in the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. For its calculation we follow Ref. citebib.bib37 that provides a probabilistic interpretation for the compositeness of a resonance. The optimal way to calculate the compositeness is still under debate citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib41, bib.bib41. By following this method we have at our disposal two equations, though the outcome depends on the value assumed for the total compositeness X𝑋X, while ΓΓGamma is taken from the LHCb collaboration Ref. citebib.bib16. Nonetheless, given a value of X𝑋X the method provides us with the partial decay widths and the partial compositeness coefficients for each channel. The third method allows one to also include coupled channels and avoid having to take for granted a value for X𝑋X. We proceed with a direct fit to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution around the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance energy region explicitly taking into account the three coupled channels J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, together with constraints from HQSS. The lightest channel accounts in an effective way of those channels whose thresholds are relatively far from the resonance mass, in particular this is the case for the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda which is degenerate to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda in the heavy-quark limit. Our analysis also favors the presence of two resonances (with J=12𝐽12J=frac12 and J=32𝐽32J=frac32 respectively) in the peak of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution associated to the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. Since the S𝑆S-wave Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD only couples to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* in J=12𝐽12J=frac12, it could affect the dynamics of this channel and leave significant impact on the line shape. As a result, it leads to the mass split of the two resonances and allows one to determine their spins. We stress that the HQSS breaking of the potential, which is of order 𝒪​(ΛQCDmc)𝒪subscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑚𝑐O(fracLambdatextQCDmc), cannot account for the mass splitting of the two resonances, cf. Sec. . In other words, the inclusion of the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel provides an extra mechanism which is responsible for the mass splitting of the two resonances while keeping the HQSS constraints on the interacting potential. In addition, we have also allowed the presence of a CDD pole in the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel but no acceptable fits result. We find that the methods 1 and 3 agree in the basic fact that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is mainly a Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* molecular resonance, while the methods 2 and 3 provide also very compatible values for the couplings of the resonance to the different channels when the same value for X𝑋X is used in the second method. The contents of the manuscript are structured as follows. After this introduction we describe and apply the methods 1, 2 and 3 to the study the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs in the Secs. , and , respectively. In the latter section we also compare between several types of fits with different number of channels and partial waves involved, as well as with different degree of imposition of the HQSS constraints. The conclusions are gathered in Sec. .
content_sections.2 Effective range expansion for the elas: First we rely on the ERE method to perform an exploratory study of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs near the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD thresholds. The elastic S𝑆S-wave ERE formula reads T​(E)=1−1a+12​r​k2−i​k,𝑇𝐸11𝑎12𝑟superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘displaystyle T(E)=frac1-frac1a+frac12r,k2-i,k,, where a𝑎a and r𝑟r are the scattering length and the effective range in order, and the non-relativistic relation between the three-momentum k𝑘k and the energy E𝐸E in the center of mass (CM) frame is k=2​μm​(E−mth),𝑘2subscript𝜇𝑚𝐸subscript𝑚thdisplaystyle k=sqrt2mum(E-mrm th),, being μm=m1​m2/(m1+m2)subscript𝜇𝑚subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2mum=m1m2/(m1+m2) the reduced mass and mth=m1+m2subscript𝑚thsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2mrm th=m1+m2 the threshold of the two states in the scattering. Above the threshold, the unitarity relation Im​T​(E)−1=−k,(E>mth),Im𝑇superscript𝐸1𝑘𝐸subscript𝑚thrm Im,T(E)-1=-k,,quad(E>mrm th),, is automatically satisfied by the amplitude in Eq. . There are two caveats when applying the ERE formalism. First the left hand cuts originated from the particle exchanges in the crossed channels are neglected. This assumption is consistent with the pionless effective filed theory description, which turns out to be able to explain the various pentaquark candidatescitebib.bib42, bib.bib43. Nevertheless, further studies on the inclusion of the light-meson exchanges reveal possibly noticeable effects on the assignments of the spin for the different pentaquark statescitebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib15, bib.bib14. The other caveat is about the possible underlying CDD pole near the threshold. It is rather challenging to infer the existence of the near-threshold CDD pole a priori. In Refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib34, a practicable criteria is provided to discern the appearance of a near-threshold CDD pole based on the large magnitude of the effective range r𝑟r. This is because the effective range is found to scale as r∝1(MCDD−mth)2,proportional-to𝑟1superscriptsubscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚th2displaystyle rproptofrac1(MtextCDD-mrm th)2,, when the CDD position in energy, MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD, approaches to the threshold. To be more specific, if the resulting magnitude of r𝑟r in the ERE study is large, as compared to the typical range around 1fm of strong interactions, it would be a clear hint for the existence of a CDD pole near threshold, indicating as well that the ERE expansion is not suitable to handle such systems. On the contrary, if the resulting |r|𝑟|r| has a value less than 1∼2similar-to121sim 2fm, a near-threshold CDD pole is not necessary and the ERE would not be spoiled by its presence, and it could be used to describe such scattering processes.4Note that a large value of |r|𝑟|r| can also be caused by a near coupled-channel effect. However, this effect could also be related to the presence of a CDD pole in the elastic scattering of the open channel. Regarding the previous discussion, by identifying the observed pentaquark candidate Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs as the resonance pole in the elastic scattering amplitude of Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, one can then fix in every case the corresponding scattering length and effective range via the mass and width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. The size obtained for the effective range would be an indication of whether the resonance can be considered as “molecular” in terms of the channel considered or it better corresponds to a bare state (preexisting resonance). The resonance pole lies in the second Riemann sheet (RS) in the elastic scattering case and the amplitude TI​I​(E)subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸TII(E) in this RS has the form TI​I​(E)=1−1a+12​r​k2+i​k. subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸11𝑎12𝑟superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘displaystyle TII(E)=frac1-frac1a+frac12r,k2,+i,k,. In this convention, the imaginary part of k𝑘k is understood to be positively taken, namely Im​k>0Im𝑘0rm Imk>0, in Eqs. and . The solution of TI​I​(ER)−1=0subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑅10TII(ER)-1=0 gives the resonance pole position ER=MR−i​ΓR/2subscript𝐸𝑅subscript𝑀𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ𝑅2ER=MR-iGammaR/2, being MRsubscript𝑀𝑅MR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR the resonance mass and width, respectively. Alternatively, one could also determine a𝑎a and r𝑟r in terms of the resonance pole position ERsubscript𝐸𝑅ER and their explicit relations turn out to becitebib.bib46 a=−2​ki|kr|2+|ki|2,r=−1ki,formulae-sequence𝑎2subscript𝑘𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑟2superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑖2𝑟1subscript𝑘𝑖displaystyle a=-frac2ki|kr|2+|ki|2,,quad r=-frac1ki,, where krsubscript𝑘𝑟kr and kisubscript𝑘𝑖ki stand for the real and imaginary parts of the three-momentum kRsubscript𝑘𝑅kR evaluated at the pole, namely, kR=2​μm​(ER−mth)subscript𝑘𝑅2subscript𝜇𝑚subscript𝐸𝑅subscript𝑚thkR=sqrt2mum(ER-mrm th). The ERE scattering amplitude is completely fixed after a𝑎a and r𝑟r are known, and with it an important quantity, i. e. the residue, which can then be inferred by taking the Laurent expansion of TI​I​(E​(k))subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸𝑘TII(E(k)) around the pole position TI​I​(k)=−ki/krk−kR+𝒪​[(k−kR)0]. subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝑘subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟𝑘subscript𝑘𝑅𝒪delimited-superscript𝑘subscript𝑘𝑅0displaystyle TII(k)=frac-ki/krk-kR+cal O[(k-kR)0],. In our previous studiescitebib.bib37, bib.bib34, this residue −ki/krsubscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟-ki/kr has been demonstrated to be equal to the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X citebib.bib37, which corresponds to the probability of the two-body component in the resonance, i. e. , X=−kikr. 𝑋subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟displaystyle X=-frackikr,. As proven in Ref. citebib.bib34 when MR−mth>0subscript𝑀𝑅subscript𝑚th0MR-mrm th>0 it follows that the compositeness X=−ki/kr𝑋subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟X=-ki/kr lies within the range [0,1]01[0,1], a result that is also consistent with the working condition of the probabilistic interpretation revealed in Ref. citebib.bib37. It should be stressed that the expression of the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X in Eq. is only valid in the context of the single-channel ERE when there is a resonance pole in the amplitude. General discussions on the compositeness relations for resonances will be given in the next section. The mass and width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs from the single-resonance fit of the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 read MR=4458.8±2.9−1.1+4.7​MeV,ΓR=17.3±6.5−5.7+8.0​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑀𝑅plus-or-minus4458.8subscriptsuperscript2.94.71.1MeVsubscriptΓ𝑅plus-or-minus17.3subscriptsuperscript6.58.05.7MeVdisplaystyle MR=4458.8pm 2.9+4.7-1.1textMeV,,qquadGammaR=17.3pm 6.5+8.0-5.7textMeV,, where the first errors are statistics and the second ones are systematic. We will take a conservative estimation and combine the two types of uncertainties by adding in quadrature the statistic error and the largest of the two systematic errors. The explicit values of the mass and width used in our analyses are given in the second and third columns of Table, respectively, together with the thresholds of Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* in the next column. As a tentative study, let us now focus on the two elastic scattering cases to address the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs, i. e. those stemming from taking separately the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, whose thresholds lie near the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs peak observed by the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. Therefore, we separately consider in the ERE analysis that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs corresponds to the poles in the elastic Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* scattering processes. The coupled-channel studies will be postponed until the next section. The determination of the scattering length and effective range for each case is summarized in the columns fifth and sixth of Table. In the elastic Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD scattering, the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs pole meets the working condition raised in Ref. citebib.bib37, i. e. , its mass is above the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD threshold, so that the prescription in Eq. is eligible for the probabilistic interpretation. The small value of the compositeness X𝑋X of the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD component, shown in the last column, indicates that other components than the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, play more important roles in the constitution of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. The small value of X𝑋X, whose central value is 31%percent3131%, is also consistent with the rather large magnitude of r𝑟r, as the latter hints towards the appearance (within this simplistic elastic picture of Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD scattering) of an underlying CDD pole near threshold, see Eq. . For the case of the elastic Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* scattering, the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs pole does not meet the requirement of the working condition in Ref. citebib.bib37, since the mass of the pole is below threshold. As a result, the prescription of Eq. loses the probabilistic interpretation in the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* case. Nevertheless, the magnitudes of r𝑟r and a𝑎a all correspond to the conventional strong interactions, which hint the natural explanation of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs as a molecular of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* system. This conclusion is further corroborated when discussing the coupled-channel case. Note that the total width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is assumed to be saturated by only one channel, either the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, in the elastic ERE framework. This assumption should be revisited since the partial decay width to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel is likely non-negligible, which is indeed the channel that is used to measure the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs in experimentcitebib.bib16. As a result, a more realistic study would require the information of the partial decay width to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, which will be investigated in the next section within a coupled-channel analysis. Later on various coupled-channel fits to the experimental event distributions of J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda will be also considered.
content_sections.3 Coupled-channel study of J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/\\p: There are intensive discussions in the literature to extend the Weinberg’s compositeness relation for the bound states citebib.bib47, bib.bib48 to the resonance casescitebib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib51, bib.bib41. The key obstacle in the latter situation is that the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X usually becomes complex citebib.bib52. Different proposals have been given in Refs. citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib41 to cure this problem. In Ref. citebib.bib37 the Weinberg’s compositeness relation for bound sates is generalized to resonances by performing a phase transformation of the S𝑆S matrix, so that the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X corresponding to the resonance state turns to be a positive number between 0 and 1, which can be interpreted as the probability to find the two-particle component in a resonance. It is demonstrated in Ref. citebib.bib37 that the compositeness coefficient Xjsubscript𝑋𝑗Xj of a specific channel j𝑗j reduces to taking the absolute value of the product of the complex residue/coupling squared gj2superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑗2gj2 and the derivative of the two-point Green function at the resonance pole position sR=(MR−i​ΓR/2)2subscript𝑠𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ𝑅22sR=(MR-iGammaR/2)2citebib.bib37, i. e. Xjsubscript𝑋𝑗displaystyle Xj =displaystyle= |gj|2​|d​Gj​(sR)d​s|,superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑗2𝑑subscript𝐺𝑗subscript𝑠𝑅𝑑𝑠displaystyle|gj|2left|fracdGj(sR)dsright|,, where Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) is the one-loop two-point Green function of the j𝑗jth channel. The expression given in Eq. represents Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) in the first or physical RS, and its corresponding formula in the unphysical RS reads citebib.bib4 Gj​(s)II=Gj​(s)−i​qj​(s)4​π​s,subscript𝐺𝑗superscript𝑠IIsubscript𝐺𝑗𝑠𝑖subscript𝑞𝑗𝑠4𝜋𝑠displaystyle Gj(s)rm II=Gj(s)-ifracqj(s)4pisqrts,, where qj​(s)subscript𝑞𝑗𝑠qj(s) denotes the relativistic CM three-momentum of the j𝑗jth channel qj​(s)=[s−(m1+m2)2]​[s−(m1−m2)2]2​s,subscript𝑞𝑗𝑠delimited-𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚22delimited-𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚222𝑠displaystyle qj(s)=fracsqrt[s-(m1+m2)2][s-(m1-m2)2]2sqrts,, with m1subscript𝑚1m1 and m2subscript𝑚2m2 the masses of the two particles in that channel. The imaginary part of Gj​(s)IIsubscript𝐺𝑗superscript𝑠IIGj(s)rm II is opposite with the one of Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) along the real s𝑠s axis above the threshold. The explicit μ𝜇mu dependences in the first two terms in Eq. cancel each other, which implies that the Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) function is actually independent on the scale μ𝜇mu. For the practical purpose, we will set μ=770𝜇770mu=770MeV throughout. The natural value of the subtraction constant a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu) can be estimated by matching the functions Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) calculated in dimensional regularization and with a three-momentum cut-off qmaxsubscript𝑞maxqtextmax at threshold, as explained in Refs. citebib.bib7, bib.bib53, which leads to a=−2m1+m2​[m1​log⁡(1+1+m12qmax2)+m2​log⁡(1+1+m22qmax2)]≃−2.5,𝑎2subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2delimited-subscript𝑚111superscriptsubscript𝑚12superscriptsubscript𝑞max2subscript𝑚211superscriptsubscript𝑚22superscriptsubscript𝑞max2similar-to-or-equals2.5displaystyle a=-frac2m1+m2left[m1logleft(1+sqrt1+fracm12qrm max2right)+m2logleft(1+sqrt1+fracm22qrm max2right)right]simeq-2.5,, by taking qmax=1.0subscript𝑞max1.0qrm max=1.0GeV, m1=mΞcsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚subscriptΞ𝑐m1=mXic and m2=mD∗subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚superscript𝐷m2=mD*. We will take a universal value for the subtraction constants in the two channels (since the variation in a𝑎a from Eq. by taking the masses of the particles in the first channel is small) and fix it to the one given in Eq. . It is worth noticing that the derivatives of the Gj​(s)subscript𝐺𝑗𝑠Gj(s) and Gj​(s)IIsubscript𝐺𝑗superscript𝑠IIGj(s)rm II functions appearing in the partial compositeness coefficient in Eq. do not depend on the subtraction constant a​(μ)𝑎𝜇a(mu) nor on μ𝜇mu. We introduce the subscript j=1𝑗1j=1 and 2 to distinguish between the channels J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript𝐷XicD*, respectively. The saturation of the compositeness relation can be written as X=X1+X2=|g1|2​|d​G1II​(sR)d​s|+|g2|2​|d​G2​(sR)d​s|,𝑋subscript𝑋1subscript𝑋2superscriptsubscript𝑔12𝑑superscriptsubscript𝐺1IIsubscript𝑠𝑅𝑑𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑔22𝑑subscript𝐺2subscript𝑠𝑅𝑑𝑠X=X1+X2=|g1|2left|fracdG1rm II(sR)dsright|+|g2|2left|fracdG2(sR)dsright|,, where X𝑋X in the left-hand side is the total compositeness coefficient contributed by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channels. Regarding the saturation of the decay width of Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs, we also assume that it is exclusively given by these two channels. For the lighter case with J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, since its threshold is clearly below the resonance mass, one can safely use the standard two-body decay width formulacitebib.bib54 Γ1=|g1|2​q1​(MR2)8​π​MR2. subscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript𝑔12subscript𝑞1superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅28𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅2displaystyleGamma1=|g1|2fracq1(MR2)8pi MR2,. While for the heavier channel Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, its threshold is slightly above the resonance mass and the standard decay width formula can not be applied. One way to estimate its partial decay width is to introduce the spectral distribution of the resonance taking into account its finite-width citebib.bib4, bib.bib55, bib.bib34 Γ2=|g2|2​∫mth,2MR+n​ΓR𝑑w​q2​(w2)16​π2​w2​ΓR(MR−w)2+ΓR2/4. subscriptΓ2superscriptsubscript𝑔22superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀𝑅𝑛subscriptΓ𝑅differential-d𝑤subscript𝑞2superscript𝑤216superscript𝜋2superscript𝑤2subscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅𝑤2superscriptsubscriptΓ𝑅24displaystyleGamma2=|g2|2intmrm th,2MR+nGammaRdw,fracq2(w2)16pi2,w2fracGammaR(MR-w)2+GammaR2/4,. Due to the long tail of the Lorentzian spectral distribution in the integrand of the above equation, the upper integration limit should be taken with care to guarantee the coverage of the resonance peak in Eq. . For the partial decay width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs into the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel, the distance of its threshold to the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs mass is slightly larger than the somewhat narrow width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. Therefore, somewhat large values of n𝑛n in the upper integration limit of Eq. turn out to be required. For example, it is found that when taking n=8𝑛8n=8, as addressed in detail in the next section, the decay width formulas for the near-threshold channels Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, calculated using Eq. , together with the standard formula of Eq. for J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, lead to consistent results from the pole determinations of both the total width and the couplings fixed from the residues of the pole position. Consequently, we will take this value of n=8𝑛8n=8 in the following analyses. The width saturation is then given by Γ=Γ1+Γ2=|g1|2​q1​(MR2)8​π​MR2+|g2|2​∫mth,2MR+n​ΓR𝑑w​q2​(w2)16​π2​w2​ΓR(MR−w)2+ΓR2/4. ΓsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ2superscriptsubscript𝑔12subscript𝑞1superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅28𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅2superscriptsubscript𝑔22superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑚th2subscript𝑀𝑅𝑛subscriptΓ𝑅differential-d𝑤subscript𝑞2superscript𝑤216superscript𝜋2superscript𝑤2subscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅𝑤2superscriptsubscriptΓ𝑅24displaystyleGamma=Gamma1+Gamma2=|g1|2fracq1(MR2)8pi MR2+|g2|2intmrm th,2MR+nGammaRdw,fracq2(w2)16pi2,w2fracGammaR(MR-w)2+GammaR2/4,. Let us stress that we have also explicitly tried to set n𝑛n at larger values, say up to 50, and the results are found to be quantitatively similar to n=8𝑛8n=8. Therefore we conclude that the values presented in Tables and are robust under the mild changes of n𝑛n. The equations that represent the saturation of the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X and the decay width ΓΓGamma can be used to determine the couplings g1subscript𝑔1g1 and g2subscript𝑔2g2, which in turn allow us to further analyze the partial compositeness factors and the partial decay widths. This approach based on saturating given values of X𝑋X and ΓΓGamma is not suitable to distinguish between the different total angular momenta J𝐽J. The point is that the coupling strengths |gj|2superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑗2|gj|2 would appear in the same way for any total angular momentum J𝐽J. Possible contributions from lighter channels, such as the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda channel, which threshold is rather distant from the interested energy region around 4.46GeV, is effectively reabsorbed in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. To solve Eqs. and , one must first provide the value of the total compositeness X𝑋X, which is however typically unknown a priori. Here we carry out exploring studies by giving some tentative values to X𝑋X, and the results are summarized in Table for the situation in which the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* coupled channels are included, and in Table for the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD case. Three different values of X𝑋X, namely X=0.1,0.5𝑋0.10.5X=0.1,0.5 and 1.0, are used to analyze the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* system. It turns out that the coupling strengths of |g1|subscript𝑔1|g1| and |g2|subscript𝑔2|g2| tends to decrease and increase, respectively, when increasing the values of X𝑋X, so do the partial decay widths of Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 and Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2. This trend is typical in this type of studies as previously observed citebib.bib56, bib.bib32. In all the cases, the partial compositeness factor X2subscript𝑋2X2 of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* is always much larger than the compositeness coefficient X1subscript𝑋1X1 contributed by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. Even when taking X=1.0𝑋1.0X=1.0, the magnitudes of the two partial widths of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channels are similar because |g2|≫|g1|much-greater-thansubscript𝑔2subscript𝑔1|g2|gg|g1|. While, when taking smaller values for the total compositeness coefficient X𝑋X, the widths of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs are found to be dominated by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. For the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD coupled-channel system the maximum value of X𝑋X that results by solving Eqs. and turns out to be around 0.3. According to the results shown in Table, the partial compositeness factor X2subscript𝑋2X2 is always much larger than the value of X1subscript𝑋1X1, regardless of the total compositeness coefficient X𝑋X taken in the study. In contrast, the magnitudes of the two partial widths are rather sensitive to the values of X𝑋X. Larger values of X𝑋X lead to bigger partial widths Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 of the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel, and smaller Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 for J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda. It is clear that definite values of X𝑋X will be obviously helpful to give more concrete conclusions of the properties of Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. In the following section, we perform the fits within dynamical unitarized models to the experimental event distributions of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda from the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. In this way, we are able to obtain more precise values of the coupling strengths gisubscript𝑔𝑖gi, which in turn allow us to perform predictions for the partial decay widths and compositeness coefficients contributed from different channels.
content_sections.4 Fits to the experimental event distrib: In the following, we perform fits to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda invariant-mass distributions from the LHCbcitebib.bib16 data on the decay Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→subscriptsuperscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xi-bto J/psiLambda K-, in order to obtain the couplings and the resonance pole position. In this way one can further constrain the coupling strengths which will in turn give more definite predictions for X𝑋X. We consider first the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel, and later also include the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD one, as these are the two-body channels whose thresholds, lying around 4478 and 4446MeV, respectively, are very close to the resonance region of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. Because of this fact we study their interaction in S𝑆S wave only. In addition, we also include the much lighter channel J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, whose event distribution will be fitted. In principle one could also include the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda since it is related to J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda by the HQSS, in which limit the mass of the J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi and ηcsubscript𝜂𝑐etac are equal. As we are interested only in the energy region around the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance it is then legitimate to characterize both in terms of just one effective channel, namely the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda (more detailed discussions on this point are given below). It is illustrative to consider the decomposition of the two-body states in the basis of HQSS eigenstates |sH⊗jℓ⟩kettensor-productsubscript𝑠𝐻subscript𝑗ℓ|sHotimes jell, where sHsubscript𝑠𝐻sH and jℓsubscript𝑗ℓjell are the total spin and angular momentum of the heavy and light quarks, respectively. The |Ξc⟩ketsubscriptΞ𝑐|Xic and |D¯∗⟩ketsuperscript¯𝐷|barD* spin multiplets are |12⊗0⟩1/2subscriptkettensor-product12012|frac12otimes 01/2 and |12⊗12⟩1subscriptkettensor-product12121|frac12otimesfrac121 in this basis, where the subscript indicates the spin jisubscript𝑗𝑖ji of each state. The latter arises from the angular momentum combination of sHi⊗jℓitensor-productsubscript𝑠subscript𝐻𝑖subscript𝑗subscriptℓ𝑖sHiotimes jelli, with the index i=1𝑖1i=1 referring to ΞcsubscriptΞ𝑐Xic and i=2𝑖2i=2 to D¯∗superscript¯𝐷barD*. One can then combine j1⊗j2tensor-productsubscript𝑗1subscript𝑗2j1otimes j2 in states of definite total angular momentum J𝐽J, |sH1​jℓ1​j1;sH2​jℓ2​j2;J⟩ketsubscript𝑠subscript𝐻1subscript𝑗subscriptℓ1subscript𝑗1subscript𝑠subscript𝐻2subscript𝑗subscriptℓ2subscript𝑗2𝐽|sH1jell1j1;sH2jell2j2;J, which we simply designate in the following by |Ξc​D¯∗⟩JsubscriptketsubscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷𝐽|XicbarD*J. Another way is to combine first the heavy spins sH1⊗sH2tensor-productsubscript𝑠subscript𝐻1subscript𝑠subscript𝐻2sH1otimes sH2 and the light angular momenta jℓ1⊗jℓ2tensor-productsubscript𝑗subscriptℓ1subscript𝑗subscriptℓ2jell1otimes jell2 separately into states with well defined total heavy spin sHsubscript𝑠𝐻sH and total light angular momentum jLsubscript𝑗𝐿jL, respectively. Then, they are composed to give states of the form |sH1​sH2​sH;jℓ1​jℓ2​jL;J⟩ketsubscript𝑠subscript𝐻1subscript𝑠subscript𝐻2subscript𝑠𝐻subscript𝑗subscriptℓ1subscript𝑗subscriptℓ2subscript𝑗𝐿𝐽|sH1sH2sH;jell1jell2jL;J, that we simply designate in the following by |sH⊗jL⟩Jsubscriptkettensor-productsubscript𝑠𝐻subscript𝑗𝐿𝐽|sHotimes jLJ, with definite total angular momentum J𝐽J. The relation between the two sets of basis vectors with total angular momentum well defined is through the Wigner 9​j9𝑗9j symbols. See e. g. Appendix A of Ref. citebib.bib57 for further details in this formalism. In this way, it follows that |Ξc​D¯∗⟩12subscriptketsubscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷12displaystyle|XicbarD*frac12 =32​|0⊗12⟩12−12​|1⊗12⟩12,absent32subscriptkettensor-product0121212subscriptkettensor-product11212displaystyle=fracsqrt32|0otimesfrac12frac12-frac12|1otimesfrac12frac12, |Ξc​D¯∗⟩32subscriptketsubscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷32displaystyle|XicbarD*frac32 =|1⊗12⟩32. absentsubscriptkettensor-product11232displaystyle=|1otimesfrac12frac32. The states |J/ψ⟩ket𝐽𝜓|J/psi, |ηc⟩ketsubscript𝜂𝑐|etac and |Λ⟩ketΛ|Lambda are |1⊗0⟩kettensor-product10|1otimes 0, |0⊗0⟩kettensor-product00|0otimes 0 and |0⊗12⟩kettensor-product012|0otimesfrac12, in this order. Therefore, |ηc​Λ⟩=|0⊗12⟩12ketsubscript𝜂𝑐Λsubscriptkettensor-product01212|etacLambda=|0otimesfrac12frac12, while |J/ψ​Λ⟩J=|1⊗12⟩Jsubscriptket𝐽𝜓Λ𝐽subscriptkettensor-product112𝐽|J/psiLambdaJ=|1otimesfrac12J has both J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2323/2. We explore first a perturbative inclusion of the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel so that we take g′=C12′=d12′=0superscript𝑔′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12subscriptsuperscript𝑑′120gprime=Cprimefrac12=dprimefrac12=0, and then its inclusion is similar to that for the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel, though G2​(s)subscript𝐺2𝑠G2(s) is peaked in the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD threshold. We then obtain the fit 320. Even though we now impose that C12=C32subscript𝐶12subscript𝐶32Cfrac12=Cfrac32, the χ2=3.06superscript𝜒23.06chi2=3.06 is almost equal to that for the fit 220, and the fit reproduces very well the data as shown in the last panel of Fig. . The values for the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 and the fit parameters are given in Tables and . In addition we also notice that C12=C32subscript𝐶12subscript𝐶32Cfrac12=Cfrac32 is almost identical to the values for C12subscript𝐶12Cfrac12 registered for the fits 210 and 220, which then indicates that this parameter is well determined within our approach. Related to this, we conclude then that our hypothesis that the lack of the channel Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD is the reason for the HQSS breakdown in the relation C12=C32subscript𝐶12subscript𝐶32Cfrac12=Cfrac32, as obtained in the fit , is well confronted to phenomenology. The nomenclature for the RSs with three channels is arranged in increasing values for the thresholds. Namely, the RSII (−++)(-++) is the one connecting with the RSI (+++)(+++) above the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and below the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD thresholds, the RSIII (−−+)(--+) connects with the physical sheet between the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD and the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* thresholds, and the RSIV connects with RSI above the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* threshold. In this case the scattering in the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 angular momenta is not interchangeable and we can give the corresponding spin of the resonances found in Table. The two poles observed for the fit 320, collected in Table, one with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 (first peak in the last panel of Fig. ) and another with J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 (the higher one in the same plot), have each of them an imaginary part that is smaller in absolute value than the one in the first two poles of Table. This is indeed related with the size of the coupling g𝑔g which is also smaller for the 320 fits as compared with the 220. Since this coupling controls how strongly the relatively-light channel J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda couples to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, and the resonances are strongly affected by the latter channel, the smaller the value of g𝑔g the smaller the resulting widths because the residue g1subscript𝑔1g1 is proportional to it. We have also explored new fits in which g′superscript𝑔′gprime, C12′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12Cprimefrac12 and/or d12′subscriptsuperscript𝑑′12dprimefrac12 are allowed to float. However, we do not observe any significant change, either qualitative or quantitative, in the description of the data. For instance, the resulting χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 in every case barely improves compared to the fit 320. Of course, the resulting fits are typically over-determined so that some of the parameters are completely undetermined (having errorbars that are much bigger than their central values). Let us discuss two of such fits. If we still fix C12′=0subscriptsuperscript𝐶′120Cprimefrac12=0 but release g′superscript𝑔′gprime we have the fit 1subscript32011 in Tables and . We observe only a slight improvement in the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2, g′=59.6−249.2+160.7superscript𝑔′subscriptsuperscript59.6160.7249.2gprime=59.6+160.7-249.2 is compatible with zero and basically undetermined, while the rest of fit parameters are very similar to the reference fit with three channels 320. However, if we fix g′=0superscript𝑔′0gprime=0 and release C12′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12Cprimefrac12 the resulting fit 2subscript32022 in Tables and , gives rise to a very uncertain coupling g𝑔g. Though, it is still compatible within errors with the determinations from the other three-channel fits, its central value is very different, while for the other fits 320 and 1subscript32011 their central values are quite similar. Notice also that again the χ2=2.81superscript𝜒22.81chi2=2.81 has improved only by a basically negligible amount compared to 320. The pole content for these fits is not shown because it is very similar to the one already given in Table. In summary, our reference fit for the three-channel case is the 320 one, in which the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel is introduced perturbatively (g′=C12′=d12′=0superscript𝑔′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12subscriptsuperscript𝑑′120gprime=Cprimefrac12=dprimefrac12=0) and we also take into account both J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2. Releasing either g′superscript𝑔′gprime or C12′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12Cprimefrac12 does not really provide better fits and the values for the fit parameters are compatible with those from 320. This indicates a perturbative role of the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel, which is also in agreement with our previous results in Sec. when considering only the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channels in J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2. Let us recall that in that case it was not possible to account for the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance signal.

With the determined pole parameters in Table for the fit involving the three-coupled channels J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, we can revise the discussions of the compositeness and partial decay widths, and make comparisons with the studies based on the saturation of the compositeness coefficient and decay width in the Sec. . For the partial decay width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs into the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel, one can safely use the standard decay width formula in Eq. . For the partial widths into the near-threshold channels Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, we will rely on the Lorentzian spectral integration formula in Eq. . By taking n=8𝑛8n=8, the partial decay widths for the lighter resonance pole with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 in Table are found to be Γ1=0.5−0.5+1.9​MeV,Γ2=4.3−1.4+1.2​MeV,Γ3=0.9−0.6+1.2​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript0.50.51.9MeVformulae-sequencesubscriptΓ2superscriptsubscript4.31.41.2MeVsubscriptΓ3superscriptsubscript0.90.61.2MeVdisplaystyleGamma1=0.5-0.5+1.9rm MeV,,quadGamma2=4.3-1.4+1.2rm MeV,,quadGamma3=0.9-0.6+1.2rm MeV,, and the sum of the three widths agrees nicely with the width of the corresponding resonance pole, i. e. minus twice the imaginary part of the pole. This is also the main reason to take n=8𝑛8n=8 here as well as in Sec. . The partial compositeness coefficients contributed by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD based on the pole parameters in Table are X1=0.0±0.0,X2=0.15±0.05. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑋1plus-or-minus0.00.0subscript𝑋2plus-or-minus0.150.05displaystyle X1=0.0pm 0.0,,quad X2=0.15pm 0.05,. It should be noted that for the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel the pole position of the lighter resonance state with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 does not meet the working condition of Ref. citebib.bib37, and as a result it is not very meaningful to interpret the partial X2subscript𝑋2X2 (corresponding to X3subscript𝑋3X3 in the three-channel case) calculated in Eq. as the probability of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* component in that resonance pole. In fact, had we blindly used Eq. to calculate X2subscript𝑋2X2 its value found would be 0.94, so that the sum of compositeness for the three channels would turn out to be slightly larger than 1. This is a manifestation that one should follow the working conditions argued in Ref. citebib.bib37 for giving a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness X𝑋X of a resonance. For the heavier resonance pole with J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 in Table , the partial widths calculated for the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* are Γ1=2.6−2.6+8.2​MeV,Γ3=0.4−0.4+2.5​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript2.62.68.2MeVsubscriptΓ3superscriptsubscript0.40.42.5MeVdisplaystyleGamma1=2.6-2.6+8.2rm MeV,,quadGamma3=0.4-0.4+2.5rm MeV,, and the resulting partial compositeness coefficients turn out to be X1=0.0±0.0,X3=1.0−0.2+0.2. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑋1plus-or-minus0.00.0subscript𝑋3superscriptsubscript1.00.20.2displaystyle X1=0.0pm 0.0,,quad X3=1.0-0.2+0.2,. Despite that the mass of the J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 pole lies clearly below the threshold of Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* we have given X3subscript𝑋3X3 calculated from Eq. as an indication that this resonance is clearly dominated by the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel component. A similar comment could be also made in relation to X3subscript𝑋3X3 for the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 pole. Indeed, we also find for both poles that the coupling |g3|subscript𝑔3|g3| is huge in comparison with |g1|subscript𝑔1|g1| and much larger, by around a factor 7/2, than |g2|subscript𝑔2|g2| for J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2. We point out that a direct comparison of the results for the partial widths and compositeness coefficients in this section and the ones in Sec. is not straightforward. The point is that in Sec. the discussions are based on the masses and widths of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs from the single-state determination of the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. However, in the present section our reference fit prefers the two-resonance solutions and the widths of the two resonance poles taking separately are found to be much smaller than the single-state determinations from the LHCb, though the combination of the two nearby peaks, convoluted with the experimental resolution in energy, gives rise to a total signal as a double bump with a width in agreement with the one pole determination for the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs citebib.bib16. Nonetheless, both the results here and in Sec. are essentially compatible, as one concludes by comparing the couplings |gi|subscript𝑔𝑖|gi| in the last and first columns of Tables and , respectively, with those determined by fitting directly the experimental data in Table.
content_sections.5 Conclusions: In this work we focus on the narrow peak around 4459MeV observed in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distributions from the LHCb measurementscitebib.bib16, which is the first discovered charmonium pentaquark with strangeness. We have considered three different methods, based on the elastic effective-range expansion, the saturation of the width and the compositeness of the resonance, and a fit to data employing unitarized phenomenological amplitudes with constraints from heavy-quark-spin symmetry. It is important to notice that the different methods provide compatible results for those magnitudes to which they can be applied for their calculation. In this sense, our analyses clearly favor a dominant role of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* state in the composition of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. For instance, the elastic effective-range analysis gives an effective range of −0.94±0.13plus-or-minus0.940.13-0.94pm 0.13fm when considering only the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, while it is much larger in absolute value, around −44-4fm, when taking only the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel. When considering the fits to data we then obtain that the compositeness factor X𝑋X of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel is around one, implying that it is saturated almost entirely by the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel alone. Finally, even if allowing the presence of a CDD pole in the two-channel case, with J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and either Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD, no acceptable fits result, which is also a clear indication of the molecular scenario. Another important outcome of our fits is that when requiring heavy-quark-spin symmetry, such that the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2 couplings of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* are equal, our analysis requires to include also the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel to fit data properly. Furthermore, in such circumstances, the resulting fit contains two poles in the energy region of interest around the resonant peak of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs, a J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 pole with mass 4453.8−3.3+2.4subscriptsuperscript4453.82.43.34453.8+2.4-3.3MeV, and a J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 one with mass 4466.6−2.7+1.9subscriptsuperscript4466.61.92.74466.6+1.9-2.7MeV. These two poles are narrow, the half-width obtained for the lighter one is 2.8−0.8+0.9subscriptsuperscript2.80.90.82.8+0.9-0.8MeV, and that for the heavier one is determined much less precisely as 1.3−3.7+1.3subscriptsuperscript1.31.33.71.3+1.3-3.7MeV. With the present energy resolution and statistics of the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 there is only a small hint that there are actually two resonances involved, though this could be disentangled in the future by experiments with improved statistics.

sorted_papers.title:
Study of the lineshape of $X(3872)$ using $B$ decays to
  $D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}K$
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a study of the $X(3872)$ lineshape in the decay $B \to X(3872)K\to
D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}K$ using a data sample of $772\times 10^6$
$B\overline{B}$ pairs collected at the $\Upsilon(4S)$ resonance with the Belle
detector at the KEKB asymmetric-energy $e^+e^-$ collider. The peak near the
threshold in the $D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}$ invariant mass spectrum is fitted
using a relativistic Breit-Wigner lineshape. We determine the mass and width
parameters to be $m = 3873.71 ^{+0.56}_{-0.50} ({\rm stat}) \pm0.13 ({\rm
syst}) ~{\rm MeV}/c^2$ and $\Gamma_0 = 5.2 ^{+2.2}_{-1.5} ({\rm stat}) \pm 0.4
({\rm syst})~{\rm MeV}$, respectively. The branching fraction is found to be
${\cal B} (B^+\to X(3872)K^+) \times {\cal{B}}(X(3872) \to
D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}) = (0.97 ^{+0.21}_{-0.18} ({\rm stat}) \pm 0.10 ({\rm
syst})) \times 10^{-4}$. The signal from $B^0$ decays is observed for the first
time with $5.2\sigma$ significance, and the ratio of branching fractions
between charged and neutral $B$ decays is measured to be ${\cal B}(B^0\to
X(3872)K^0)/{\cal B}(B^+ \to X(3872)K^+) = 1.34^{+0.47}_{-0.40} ({\rm stat})
^{+0.10}_{-0.12} ({\rm syst})$. The peak is also studied using a Flatt\'{e}
lineshape. We determine the lower limit on the $D\overline{D}{}^{*}$ coupling
constant $g$ to be $0.075$ at 95% credibility in the parameter region where the
ratio of $g$ to the mass difference from the $D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}$ threshold
is equal to $-15.11~{\rm GeV}^{-1}$, as measured by LHCb.

qualitative: The Flatté lineshape is used to model the X(3872) resonance near the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold, accounting for coupled-channel effects through an energy-dependent partial width.

In the near-threshold regime, the Flatté parametrization captures the influence of the D⁰D̄*⁰ channel on the resonance structure, particularly when the resonance mass is close to the threshold and the state can couple to this channel in S-wave.

The Flatté model is especially suited for systems near threshold where the effective range approximation becomes relevant, as it incorporates the scattering amplitude's pole structure and threshold behavior.

The lineshape in the Flatté model reflects the underlying dynamics of the scattering amplitude, including the presence of poles in the complex energy plane, which can provide insight into the nature of the X(3872) as a molecular or resonance state.

The effective range approximation is implicitly embedded in the Flatté formalism, where the coupling strength and threshold parameters determine the shape of the resonance near threshold, particularly when the mass difference from threshold is small.
quantitative: The lower limit on the D⁰D̄*⁰ coupling constant g is determined to be 0.075 at 95% credibility, in the parameter region where the ratio of g to the mass difference from the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold is equal to -15.11 GeV⁻¹, as measured by LHCb.
The D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold mass is 3871.69 ± 0.10 MeV/c².
The X(3872) mass is measured as 3873.71⁺⁰·⁵⁶₋₀·⁵⁰ (stat) ± 0.13 (syst) MeV/c², which lies just above the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold.
The Flatté model allows for the determination of the coupling strength g, which is a key parameter in the effective range approximation for near-threshold resonances.

The Flatté parametrization includes an energy-dependent width that modifies the standard Breit-Wigner form, with the threshold behavior governed by the relation involving g and the mass difference from threshold.

- C1：研究近阈值区Flatté线形 — 满足。证据：“The peak is also studied using a Flatté lineshape” 对比 “Flatté line shape in the near-threshold regime”  
- C2：涉及有效作用程近似 — 满足。证据：“lower limit on the $D\overline{D}{}^{*}$ coupling constant $g$” 对比 “effective range approximation”（隐含于Flatté模型参数化）  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：
