content_sections.I introduction: The charmonium-like X​𝑋3872X state, also known as χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1citebib.bib1, was discovered by the Belle experiment as a narrow peak in the vicinity of the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold in the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- invariant mass distribution in exclusive B+→J/ψ​π+​π−​K+→superscript𝐵𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝐾B+to J/psipi+pi-K+ decayscitebib.bib2. Its existence has been confirmed by multiple experiments: D0citebib.bib3, BABARcitebib.bib4, CDFcitebib.bib5, LHCbcitebib.bib6, and BESIIIcitebib.bib7. In addition to the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- decay, other decays such as J/ψ​ω𝐽𝜓𝜔J/psiomegacitebib.bib8, J/ψ​γ𝐽𝜓𝛾J/psigamma, ψ​(2​S)​γ𝜓2𝑆𝛾psi(2S)gammacitebib.bib9, D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0citebib.bib10, bib.bib11, D0​D¯​π00superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00D0overlineD0pi0citebib.bib12, and π0​χc​0superscript𝜋0subscript𝜒𝑐0pi0chic0citebib.bib13 have been observed. The X​𝑋3872X quantum numbers JP​Csuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶JPC have been determined to be 1++superscript1absent1++citebib.bib14, bib.bib15. Various interpretations such as a loosely bound statecitebib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, an admixture of a molecular state and a pure charmonium resonancecitebib.bib20, a tetraquarkcitebib.bib21, and a cusp at the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 thresholdcitebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24 have been proposed, and the structure of the state remains uncertain. Measurement of the lineshape in various decay modes can help to discriminate among different choices for the structure. In this paper, we examine two models for the lineshape in the decay to D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0: a Breit-Wigner, and a Flatté-inspired parametrization. The X​𝑋3872X peak has already been analyzed with the Breit-Wigner lineshape commonly used for resonance states. Based on the analyses of the decays including J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi, the mass is 3871.65±0.06​MeV/c2plus-or-minus3871.650.06MeVsuperscript𝑐23871.65pm 0.06rm MeV/c2 and the width is 1.19±0.21​MeVplus-or-minus1.190.21MeV1.19pm 0.21rm MeVcitebib.bib1, with two measurements at the LHCb experimentcitebib.bib25, bib.bib26 contributing significantly to these averages. Analyses of the decay to D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 based on the Breit-Wigner lineshape tend to yield a higher mass and a larger width, with the width measurement subject to large uncertaintiescitebib.bib10, bib.bib11. Discrepancies in the lineshape between the decays to the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- and D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 final states can arise near the threshold due to coupled-channel effectscitebib.bib22. This may be significant for the X​𝑋3872X, as the observed mass coincides with the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold of 3871.69±0.10​MeV/c2plus-or-minus3871.690.10MeVsuperscript𝑐23871.69pm 0.10rm MeV/c2, and a 1++superscript1absent1++ state can couple to the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 channel in S-wave. One model to account for coupled-channel effects is the Flatté-inspired parametrizationcitebib.bib22, bib.bib27, a Breit-Wigner model with an explicit expression for the energy-dependent partial width. At LHCb, an analysis of the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- invariant mass distribution was performed using this Flatté-inspired modelcitebib.bib26. It is difficult to determine all of the parameters using only this distribution, due to a scaling behavior in which the lineshape near the threshold does not change under a linear transformation of four of the five parameterscitebib.bib26, bib.bib28. To determine all the parameters, it is important to analyze not only the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- decay but also the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decay, as proposed in the theoretical analysiscitebib.bib27. By analyzing the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decay, we aim to provide more information on the lineshape, and in particular on the coupling strength of X​→D0​D¯∗0→𝑋3872superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷absent0Xto D0barD*0. In this paper, we present a study of the X​𝑋3872X lineshape using a sample of X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 candidates produced in the exclusive decay B→D0​D¯​K∗0→𝐵superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscript𝐾absent0Bto D0overlineD*0K using the full Belle dataset. There have been three previous studiescitebib.bib12, bib.bib10, bib.bib11. Referencecitebib.bib12 is an analysis of the B→D0​D¯​π00​K→𝐵superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00𝐾Bto D0overlineD0pi0K decay at Belle, and Refscitebib.bib10, bib.bib11 are analyses of the B→D0​D¯​K∗0→𝐵superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscript𝐾absent0Bto D0overlineD*0K decays at BABAR and Belle, respectively. The latter two analyses apply a D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 selection and a mass-constrained fit to the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 candidates. While this has the advantage of improving the signal-to-noise ratio, it has the disadvantage of disallowing entries below the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold, which is important for studying the structure. Given the limited size of our data sample, we adopt a similar technique to the latter analyses, i. e. subtracting the reconstructed D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 mass and adding the nominal mass. The disadvantage of requiring the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 is partially compensated for by analyzing the Flatté model, in which we can obtain a lineshape reflecting poles of the scattering amplitude. Compared to Refscitebib.bib10, bib.bib11, additional D0superscript𝐷0D0 decay modes are included, increasing the efficiency to reconstruct D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decays. Throughout this paper, charge conjugation is always included. We do not distinguish D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 from D¯​D∗00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝐷absent00overlineD0D*0 unless otherwise indicated. The paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , the Belle detector and data set are described. In Sections and, the event selection and the fitted model are presented. In Sec. , the results of fitting the data with the relativistic Breit-Wigner model and the Flatté model are presented. Section contains a discussion of the results, and the conclusions of the paper.
content_sections.II Detector and Data Set: We use a data sample of 772×106772superscript106772times 106 B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BoverlineB pairs, collected at a center-of-mass energy of s=10.58​GeV𝑠10.58GeVsqrts=10.58rm GeV, corresponding to the Υ​(4​S)Υ4𝑆Upsilon(4S) resonance, with the Belle detector at the KEKB asymmetric-energy e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collidercitebib.bib29, bib.bib30. The Belle detector is a large-solid-angle magnetic spectrometer that consists of a silicon vertex detector (SVD), a 50-layer central drift chamber (CDC), an array of aerogel threshold Cherenkov counters (ACC), a barrel-like arrangement of time-of-flight scintillation counters (TOF), and an electromagnetic calorimeter comprised of CsI(Tl) crystals (ECL) located inside a super-conducting solenoid coil that provides a 1.5T magnetic field. An iron flux-return located outside of the coil is installed to detect KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KL0 mesons and to identify muons (KLM). The detector is described in detail elsewherecitebib.bib31, bib.bib32. To determine the event selection and the detector response, we use a sample of Monte Carlo (MC) simulated events generated using the EvtGen event generatorcitebib.bib33. The detector response is simulated using the GEANT3 packagecitebib.bib34.
content_sections.III Event Selection: The event selection is determined using the MC samples in two steps. First, the selection criteria for the final-state particles are determined based on our previous studiescitebib.bib12, bib.bib11. Second, the selection criteria for the intermediate-state particles are optimized by maximizing the figure-of-merit S/S+B𝑆𝑆𝐵S/sqrtS+B, where S𝑆S and B𝐵B are the estimated numbers of signal and background events, respectively. The resulting selection is described below. Tracks are selected using vertex information measured by the tracking system. A track candidate is accepted if its distance along the detector axis from the point of closest approach to the interaction point is less than 4.0cm, and its distance transverse to the detector axis is less than 1.0cm. These requirements are not imposed for tracks in KS0→π+​π−→superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋KS0topi+pi- candidates. In addition, pion and kaon candidates are selected using likelihoods ℒπsubscriptℒ𝜋cal Lpi and ℒKsubscriptℒ𝐾cal LK based on the time-of-flight measured by the TOF, the number of Cherenkov photons detected by the ACC, and the ionization loss in the CDC. Tracks with a likelihood ratio ℒπ/(ℒπ+ℒK)>0.1subscriptℒ𝜋subscriptℒ𝜋subscriptℒ𝐾0.1cal Lpi/(cal Lpi+cal LK)>0.1 are used as charged pion candidates, and tracks with ℒπ/(ℒπ+ℒK)0.95subscriptℒ𝑒subscriptℒ𝑒subscriptℒ𝑒0.95cal Le/(cal Le+cal Ltildee)>0.95 are identified as electrons and eliminated. Here, ℒesubscriptℒ𝑒cal Le and ℒesubscriptℒ𝑒cal Ltildee are distinct likelihoods for the electron and non-electron hypotheses, based on ECL, tracking, and other information. The particle identification is described in detail elsewherecitebib.bib35. KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 candidates are reconstructed from charged pion pairs with opposite charges. The π+​π−superscript𝜋superscript𝜋pi+pi- invariant mass is required to agree with the known KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 masscitebib.bib1 within 7​MeV/c27MeVsuperscript𝑐27rm MeV/c2 (≈3.6​σabsent3.6𝜎approx 3.6sigma of the resolution). Candidates are selected using a neural network classifiercitebib.bib36 with various kinematic variables as input. To improve the four-momentum resolution, a mass- and vertex-constrained fit is applied. Photon candidates are reconstructed from ECL clusters with no matching charged tracks. Candidates are selected based on the ratio, E9/E25subscript𝐸9subscript𝐸25E9/E25, of the energy deposited in the 3×3333times 3 array of crystals centered on the crystal with the highest energy deposition to that in the 5×5555times 5 array: we require E9/E25>0.8subscript𝐸9subscript𝐸250.8E9/E25>0.8. Neutral pions are reconstructed from photon pairs. The photons are required to have energy greater than 30​MeV30MeV30rm MeV in the barrel region or 50​MeV50MeV50rm MeV in the endcaps. The γ​γ𝛾𝛾gammagamma invariant mass is required to agree with the π0superscript𝜋0pi0 nominal masscitebib.bib1 within 12​MeV/c212MeVsuperscript𝑐212rm MeV/c2. This mass window corresponds to 92% signal efficiency. A mass-constrained fit is applied to improve the momentum resolution. D0superscript𝐷0D0 candidates are reconstructed in six decay modes: K−​π+superscript𝐾superscript𝜋K-pi+, K−​π+​π0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0K-pi+pi0, K−​π+​π−​π+superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋K-pi+pi-pi+, KS0​π+​π−superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋KS0pi+pi-, KS0​π+​π−​π0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0KS0pi+pi-pi0, and K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K-. The π0superscript𝜋0pi0 candidates used in this reconstruction are required to have momentum in the center-of-mass system greater than 100​MeV/c100MeV𝑐100rm MeV/c, and energy in the laboratory system greater than 150​MeV150MeV150rm MeV. If a π0superscript𝜋0pi0 is included, the reconstructed D0superscript𝐷0D0 invariant mass is required to be within 16​MeV/c216MeVsuperscript𝑐216rm MeV/c2 of the nominal masscitebib.bib1 corresponding to 85% signal efficiency; otherwise, it is required to be within 8.5​MeV/c28.5MeVsuperscript𝑐28.5rm MeV/c2 corresponding to 91% efficiency. To improve the momentum resolution, a mass- and vertex-constrained fit is applied. Candidates where the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 probability of the fit is less than 0.0001 are eliminated. D¯∗0overlineD*0 candidates are reconstructed in two decay modes: D¯​γ0¯𝐷superscript𝛾0overlineD0gamma and D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0. For the D¯​γ0¯𝐷superscript𝛾0overlineD0gamma mode, only γ𝛾gamma candidates with an energy greater than 90​MeV90MeV90rm MeV in the laboratory system are used. For the D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0 mode, only π0superscript𝜋0pi0 candidates with a momentum in the center-of-mass system of less than 100​MeV/c100MeV𝑐100rm MeV/c and an energy in the laboratory system of less than 200​MeV200MeV200rm MeV are used. The difference in the reconstructed mass between D¯∗0overlineD*0 and D¯0overlineD0 is required to agree with the nominal valuecitebib.bib1 within 9.0​MeV/c29.0MeVsuperscript𝑐29.0rm MeV/c2 and 2.0​MeV/c22.0MeVsuperscript𝑐22.0rm MeV/c2 for D¯​γ0¯𝐷superscript𝛾0overlineD0gamma and D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0, respectively, corresponding to 90% signal efficiency in each case. B𝐵B meson candidates are then reconstructed in the decay modes D0​D¯​K+∗0superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝐾absent0D0overlineD*0K+ and D0​D¯​KS0∗0superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0absent0D0overlineD*0KS0. To reduce wrong combinations, the daughter K+superscript𝐾K+ is required to have ℒK/(ℒπ+ℒK)>0.6subscriptℒ𝐾subscriptℒ𝜋subscriptℒ𝐾0.6cal LK/(cal Lpi+cal LK)>0.6, corresponding to an identification efficiency of 89%. The B𝐵B candidates are selected based on the beam-energy constrained mass, Mbc≡(Ebeamcms)2−(pBcms)2subscript𝑀bcsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐸beamcms2superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝐵cms2Mrm bcequivsqrt(Erm beamrm cms)2-(pBrm cms)2 and the difference of the energy in the center-of-mass system between the B𝐵B candidate and the beam, Δ​E≡EBcms−EbeamcmsΔ𝐸superscriptsubscript𝐸𝐵cmssuperscriptsubscript𝐸beamcmsDelta Eequiv EBrm cms-Erm beamrm cms, where Ebeamcmssuperscriptsubscript𝐸beamcmsErm beamrm cms is the beam energy in the center-of-mass system corresponding to half of s𝑠sqrts, and pBcmssuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝐵cmspBrm cms and EBcmssuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝐵cmsEBrm cms are the energy and momentum of B𝐵B candidates in the center-of-mass system, respectively. We retain events with Mbc>5.2​GeV/c2subscript𝑀bc5.2GeVsuperscript𝑐2Mrm bc>5.2rm GeV/c2 and |Δ​E|<50​MeVΔ𝐸50MeV|Delta E|<50rm MeV for later analysis. The Mbcsubscript𝑀bcMrm bc signal region is defined as |Mbc−mB|<4.5​MeV/c2subscript𝑀bcsubscript𝑚𝐵4.5MeVsuperscript𝑐2|Mrm bc-mB|<4.5rm MeV/c2 (≈2​σabsent2𝜎approx 2sigma) for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma and |Mbc−mB|<6.0​MeV/c2subscript𝑀bcsubscript𝑚𝐵6.0MeVsuperscript𝑐2|Mrm bc-mB|<6.0rm MeV/c2 (≈2.5​σabsent2.5𝜎approx 2.5sigma) for D¯→∗0D¯π00overlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0, where mBsubscript𝑚𝐵mB denotes the nominal B𝐵B masscitebib.bib1. The Δ​EΔ𝐸Delta E signal region is defined as |Δ​E|<12​MeVΔ𝐸12MeV|Delta E|<12rm MeV (≈2​σabsent2𝜎approx 2sigma). For suppression of continuum events, we use a FastBDT classifiercitebib.bib37 trained on the simulation sample with the following event-shape information as input: modified Fox-Wolfram momentscitebib.bib38, the momentum flow in concentric cones around the thrust axiscitebib.bib39, and thrust-related quantities. Events for which the classifier output is less than 0.15 are eliminated. This requirement retains 96% of the signal candidates and rejects 49% of the candidates of continuum events. After this selection, the average number of B𝐵B candidates per event is 1.8, because D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 and D∗0D¯0D*0overlineD0 are often indistinguishable and double-counted. To avoid multiple counting of signal events, we select the candidate that has the highest value of the product of the following likelihood ℒℒcal L and prior probability 𝒫𝒫cal P ℒ=ℒM​(D0)×ℒM(D¯)0×ℒM(D¯)∗0−M(D¯)0×ℒΔ​E[×ℒM​(π0)],𝒫=εi​j​kζi​j​k×ℬ(D0→i)×ℬ(D¯→0j)×ℬ(D¯→∗0k),beginsplitcal L=cal LM(D0)timescal LM(overlineD0)timescal LM(overlineD*0)-M(overlineD0) qquadqquadqquadqquadqquadtimescal LDelta E[timescal LM(pi0)], cal P=fracvarepsilonijkzetaijktimescal B(D0to i)timescal B(overlineD0to j)timescal B(overlineD*0to k),endsplit where ℒℒcal L is the product of the likelihoods of the measured D0superscript𝐷0D0, D¯0overlineD0, and D¯∗0overlineD*0 masses, and Δ​EΔ𝐸Delta E; and, for the D¯→∗0D¯π00overlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0 mode, the likelihood of the measured π0superscript𝜋0pi0 mass. Each likelihood is obtained using probability density functions (PDFs) determined using the MC samples. The probability 𝒫𝒫cal P is obtained from the probability that a signal event can be reconstructed εi​j​ksubscript𝜀𝑖𝑗𝑘varepsilonijk, the average number of B𝐵B candidates per event ζi​j​ksubscript𝜁𝑖𝑗𝑘zetaijk, and the decay branching fraction, when D0superscript𝐷0D0, D¯0overlineD0, and D¯∗0overlineD*0 are reconstructed in the i𝑖i, j𝑗j, and k𝑘k modes, respectively. The values of εi​j​ksubscript𝜀𝑖𝑗𝑘varepsilonijk and ζi​j​ksubscript𝜁𝑖𝑗𝑘zetaijk are determined using the MC samples. The (Mbc,Δ​E)subscript𝑀bcΔ𝐸(Mrm bc,Delta E) distribution of the selected B𝐵B candidates is shown in Fig. . The red solid (blue dashed) rectangle shows the (Mbc,Δ​E)subscript𝑀bcΔ𝐸(Mrm bc,Delta E) signal region for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma (D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0): B𝐵B candidates used in the lineshape study are selected from this region. For all events remaining in the selection, the following D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 invariant mass is calculated instead of applying a mass-constrained fit to improve the mass resolution, M(D0D¯)∗0=M​(D0​D¯​γ0)−M​(D¯​γ0)+mD¯∗0forD¯→∗0D¯γ0,M​(D0​D¯​π00)−M​(D¯​π00)+mD¯∗0forD¯→∗0D¯π00,beginsplitM(D0overlineD*0) =leftbeginarrayllM(D0overlineD0gamma)-M(overlineD0gamma)+moverlineD*0 rm foroverlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma, M(D0overlineD0pi0)-M(overlineD0pi0)+moverlineD*0 rm foroverlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0, endarrayright. endsplit where the reconstructed D¯∗0overlineD*0 invariant mass, M​(D¯​γ0)𝑀¯𝐷superscript𝛾0M(overlineD0gamma) or M​(D¯​π00)𝑀¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00M(overlineD0pi0), is subtracted, and the D¯∗0overlineD*0 nominal mass, mD¯∗0moverlineD*0, is added. The lineshape and signal yield are determined by fitting the distribution in the region below 4.0​GeV/c24.0GeVsuperscript𝑐24.0rm GeV/c2.
content_sections.IV Fit Strategy and Detector Response: In this work, the obtained M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) distributions are fitted with two lineshape models: the relativistic Breit-Wigner, and a Flatté-inspired model. The fits with these two models, shown in the next section, are performed with the following procedure. When signal events are reconstructed correctly, the invariant mass distribution has a peak consisting of the natural lineshape convolved with the mass-dependent detector response. This response, i. e. the mass dependence of the signal efficiency and the mass resolution, is studied and parameterized using a set of X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 MC samples generated with zero width, and a range of mass values from the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold to 4.0​GeV/c24.0GeVsuperscript𝑐24.0rm GeV/c2. Here, the X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 decays are generated using a uniform phase space model; the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 width is assumed to be around 60keVcitebib.bib23. Since the signal-to-noise ratio depends on the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 decay mode, fits are performed separately for D∗0→D0​γ→superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷0𝛾D*0to D0gamma and D∗0→D0​π0→superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0D*0to D0pi0. In addition, fits are performed separately for B0superscript𝐵0B0 and B+superscript𝐵B+ candidates to determine the ratio of branching fractions between B0→X​​K0→superscript𝐵0𝑋3872superscript𝐾0B0to XK0 and B+→X​​K+→superscript𝐵𝑋3872superscript𝐾B+to XK+. The fit function for correctly reconstructed X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 decays, which we refer to as “signal”, is constructed as follows. The signal efficiency varies depending on the mass by a few tens of percent, especially around the threshold, as shown in Fig. (a). It is parameterized by the threshold function p0​1−p1​ep2​(M−mD0−mD∗0)+p3​(M−mD0−mD∗0)subscript𝑝01subscript𝑝1superscript𝑒subscript𝑝2𝑀subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0subscript𝑝3𝑀subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0p01-p1ep2(M-mD0-mD*0)+p3(M-mD0-mD*0) with parameters p0subscript𝑝0p0–p3subscript𝑝3p3 in the low-mass region, which is continuously connected to a constant value in the high-mass region. The mass resolution for the signal is modeled as the sum of a Gaussian and a reversed Crystal Ball functioncitebib.bib40 with a common mean. Figure(b) shows the M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) spread due to the resolution, and the resolution function used in this work, for several choices of the X​𝑋3872X mass. As noted in the previous Belle study, the resolution degrades with the square root of the difference between the mass and the thresholdcitebib.bib11. The convolution with the mass-dependent resolution function entails longer computation times. The effect of smearing due to the resolution is small at masses away from the peak, since the natural lineshape is broadcitebib.bib10, bib.bib11. For example, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the natural lineshape is a few MeV, while the FWHM of the mass resolution near the peak is only about 220 keV. Therefore, instead of convolution with the mass-dependent resolution function, convolution with the specific resolution function at the mass of 3871.9​MeV/c23871.9MeVsuperscript𝑐23871.9rm MeV/c2 (near the peak) is adopted as an approximation. To reproduce the behavior near threshold, the signal function is multiplied by a soft threshold function that rises from zero to one at the threshold using an error function. The procedure is validated on X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 MC samples generated with a broad lineshape. The effect of the approximation is negligible. The ratios of signal yields among the decay modes are fixed in the fit using the product of the expected total signal efficiency and the branching fraction of each decay mode. Here the expected signal efficiency depends on the lineshape because of the mass dependence of the signal efficiency. The total signal efficiency is obtained by averaging the signal efficiencies as a function of mass weighted by the values of the lineshape function. It is then corrected by taking account of the signal which may leak out of the fit range, depending on the lineshape, and by taking the ratio of the area of the signal function in the fit range to that from the threshold to mB−mKsubscript𝑚𝐵subscript𝑚𝐾mB-mK. Here, a mass-dependent resolution function is convolved with the signal function, because smearing due to the resolution is important at higher masses. The calculation of the total signal efficiency is validated on MC samples for a broad range of lineshape parameters. A separate fit function is used for “broken signal”: cases where the D0superscript𝐷0D0 is reconstructed incorrectly, a wrong π0superscript𝜋0pi0 or γ𝛾gamma is combined in the D¯∗0overlineD*0 reconstruction, or a D∗0D¯0D*0overlineD0 signal event is misinterpreted as D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 by combining π0superscript𝜋0pi0 or γ𝛾gamma from D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 incorrectly with the D¯0overlineD0 to make a fake D¯∗0overlineD*0. For the D¯→∗0D¯π00overlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0 mode, such events produce a broad peak in the M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) signal region and possibly distort the lineshape of the signal. The fit function for the broken signal therefore takes account of the mass dependencies of the resolution and the efficiency as in the case of correctly reconstructed signal events. The mass dependence of the efficiency is parameterized using the same threshold function used for the signal. The resolution is reproduced by a triple Gaussian multiplied by a soft threshold function at the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold, and its mass dependence is studied and parameterized using zero-width MC samples. Since the resolution for the broken signal is several times worse than that for the signal, we do not use approximated convolution: we instead use a discrete convolution with the mass-dependent resolution function. In the fit, the yield of the broken signal relative to the signal is fixed to the value expected from the lineshape and the ratio of the total efficiency of the broken signal to that of the signal. The broken signal peak due to the D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma mode has little sensitivity to the natural lineshape. To reduce the systematic uncertainty due to the shape, we use a histogram PDF depending on the lineshape. This PDF is obtained by plotting the broken-signal histogram for each of the zero-width MC samples, scaling it by the value of the assumed lineshape at the generated mass, and summing up all of the scaled histograms. Here, the bin widths are adjusted to increase as the mass increases to suppress statistical fluctuations. The background from e+​e−→q​q¯→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝑞¯𝑞e+e-to qbarq (q=u,d,s,c𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠𝑐q=u,d,s,c) continuum events, and e+​e−→Υ​(4​S)→B​B¯→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒Υ4𝑆→𝐵¯𝐵e+e-toUpsilon(4S)to BoverlineB events other than signal, is studied using the background MC sample. The shape of the invariant mass distribution is reproduced using a threshold function, M−(mD0+mD∗0)𝑀subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0sqrtM-(mD0+mD*0), where mD0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0mD0 and mD∗0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0mD*0 are the nominal masses of D0superscript𝐷0D0 and D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0citebib.bib1, respectively.

We consider the following nine sources of uncertainty on the mass, the width, and the signal yield: (i) The uncertainty due to the assumed shape of the generic background is estimated by performing a fit after changing the PDF from the threshold function with a square root to an inverted ARGUS functioncitebib.bib41. (ii) The mass resolution is validated by comparing the data and MC Δ​EΔ𝐸Delta E resolution in the B+→D¯​π+∗0​π−​π+→superscript𝐵¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋absent0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B+tooverlineD*0pi+pi-pi+ control sample, which has a similar decay topology to B→X(→D0D¯)∗0KBto X(to D0overlineD*0)K. The ratios of the mass resolution obtained for MC and data are 1.01±0.10plus-or-minus1.010.101.01pm 0.10 for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma and 1.08±0.13plus-or-minus1.080.131.08pm 0.13 for D¯→∗0D¯π00overlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0. This resolution is consistent in data and MC, so no correction is applied, and the associated uncertainty is assigned by performing fits with the resolution varied by the precision, ±1​σ≡±13%plus-or-minus1𝜎plus-or-minuspercent13pm 1sigmaequivpm 13%. (iii, iv) The uncertainties arising from the mass dependence of the efficiency and the ratio of the broken-signal to the signal are evaluated by summing in quadrature the changes induced by ±1​σplus-or-minus1𝜎pm 1sigma variations of the relevant parameters. (v) The uncertainty due to possible bias in the fit is evaluated by performing pseudo experiments. The input value of a parameter subtracted from the median of the parameter distribution is regarded as the corresponding uncertainty. (vi) For the mBWsubscript𝑚BWmrm BW measurement only, the uncertainty arising from the finite precision of the D0superscript𝐷0D0 mass and the Δ​(mD∗0−mD0)Δsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0Delta(mD*0-mD0) mass difference is taken as the ±1​σplus-or-minus1𝜎pm 1sigma uncertainty of 2​mD0+Δ​(mD∗0−mD0)=3871.69±0.10​MeV/c22subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0Δsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0plus-or-minus3871.690.10MeVsuperscript𝑐22mD0+Delta(mD*0-mD0)=3871.69pm 0.10rm MeV/c2 following Ref. citebib.bib1. (vii) The nonzero D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 width (ΓD∗0subscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absent0GammaD*0) leads to three potential sources of bias: a bias arising from the mass difference technique, a bias arising from the consideration of the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 width in the lineshape model, and a bias due to the interference between X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 and D¯​D∗00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝐷absent00overlineD0D*0. Biases from these three sources are evaluated as follows. For the first bias, two M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) distributions are formed in MC with a broad lineshape: one where mD∗0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0mD*0 in Eq. is fixed to the nominal value (as in our analysis), and the other where mD∗0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0mD*0 is replaced by the true D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 mass generated by EvtGen, where ΓD∗0=65.5​keVsubscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absent065.5keVGammaD*0=65.5rm keVcitebib.bib23 is assumed. Each distribution is fitted with the PDF of the signal component, and the largest difference is regarded as the associated uncertainty. For the second bias, the distribution for data is fitted after smearing the assumed lineshape with a Breit-Wigner function of ΓD∗0=65.5​keVsubscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absent065.5keVGammaD*0=65.5rm keV, and the change from the original result is regarded as the associated uncertainty. The third bias is ignored since the interference effect is negligible above the thresholdcitebib.bib42. The uncertainties associated with the first and second biases are added in quadrature. (viii) Limited MC statistics lead to uncertainty on the shape of the broken-signal for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma. This is evaluated by repeating the fit while varying each bin entry of the MC PDF histogram assuming Poisson distributions. The 68% interval of the distributions of the resulting fit values is used to assign the uncertainty. (ix) The uncertainty arising from the fixed ratio of the signal yields for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma to D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0 is evaluated by performing new fits, and varying the ratio of branching fractions between D∗0→D0​γ→superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷0𝛾D*0to D0gamma and D∗0→D0​π0→superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0D*0to D0pi0 by ±1​σplus-or-minus1𝜎pm 1sigmacitebib.bib1. The difference from the original result is treated as the uncertainty. In each case, q​(m′,E)𝑞superscript𝑚′𝐸q(mprime,E) is the momentum of the two- or three-pion system in the rest frame of the X​𝑋3872X: q​(m′,E)=12​M​(E)​M2​(E)−(m′+mJ/ψ)2×M2​(E)−(m′−mJ/ψ)2. 𝑞superscript𝑚′𝐸12𝑀𝐸superscript𝑀2𝐸superscriptsuperscript𝑚′subscript𝑚𝐽𝜓2superscript𝑀2𝐸superscriptsuperscript𝑚′subscript𝑚𝐽𝜓2beginsplitq(mprime,E)=frac12M(E)sqrtM2(E)-(mprime+mJ/psi)2 timessqrtM2(E)-(mprime-mJ/psi)2. endsplit The parameter Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0 is the sum of the partial widths of other channels, such as radiative decays. In total, this model has five free parameters, Efsubscript𝐸𝑓Ef, g𝑔g, fρsubscript𝑓𝜌frho, fωsubscript𝑓𝜔fomega, and Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0. To obtain stable results in the fit, we apply two constraints, which were also used in the previous study at LHCbcitebib.bib26. The first is to fix fωsubscript𝑓𝜔fomega so that the branching fraction of the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- mode and that of the J/ψ​ω𝐽𝜓𝜔J/psiomega mode are equal, consistent with experimental results to datecitebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. Based on the feature that the area under the lineshape for a channel is proportional to the branching fraction, fωsubscript𝑓𝜔fomega can be uniquely determined by calculating the ratio of the lineshape area in the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- channel to that in J/ψ​ω𝐽𝜓𝜔J/psiomega. The second is a soft constraint on the ratio of branching fractions between the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- and D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decay modes: for each of the B+superscript𝐵B+ and B0superscript𝐵0B0 modes, the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- product branching fraction is calculated as follows, and a Gaussian constraint to the measured valuecitebib.bib47 is included in the fit, ℬ​(B→X​​K)×ℬ​(X​→J/ψ​π+​π−)=RDD¯∗×ℬ(B→XK)×ℬ(X→D0D¯)∗0=(8.61±0.32)×10−6for the​B+​mode(4.1±1.1)×10−6for the​B0​mode,beginsplitcal B(Bto XK)timescal B(Xto J/psipi+pi-) =RDoverlineD*timescal B(Bto XK)timescalB(Xto D0overlineD*0) =leftbeginarraycc(8.61pm 0.32)times 10-6textfor the B+text mode (4.1pm 1.1)times 10-6textfor the B0text mode endarrayright. ,endsplit where RDD¯∗RDoverlineD* is the ratio of the lineshape area in the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- channel to that in D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0, and the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 product branching fraction is given by Eq. . There are too few events in our X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 sample to simultaneously determine the four remaining parameters. Therefore, we focus on the parameter regions where scaling behavior was observed at LHCbcitebib.bib26. We search for the best lineshape fitted to the M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) distribution when the following ratios of parameters are fixed to the values measured at LHCb: d​g/d​Ef𝑑𝑔𝑑subscript𝐸𝑓dg/dEf is fixed to −15.11​GeV−115.11superscriptGeV1-15.11rm GeV-1, and fρ/Efsubscript𝑓𝜌subscript𝐸𝑓frho/Ef and Γ0/EfsubscriptΓ0subscript𝐸𝑓Gamma0/Ef are fixed based on the measurements fρ=1.8×10−3subscript𝑓𝜌1.8superscript103frho=1.8times 10-3 and Γ0=1.4​MeVsubscriptΓ01.4MeVGamma0=1.4rm MeV, and the assumption Ef=−7.2​MeVsubscript𝐸𝑓7.2MeVEf=-7.2rm MeV. Thus, only g𝑔g is floated as a free parameter. We perform a simultaneous unbinned maximum likelihood fit under the above fit conditions. The fit results for the data are shown in Fig. and Table. The fitted g𝑔g is 0.29−0.15+2.69subscriptsuperscript0.292.690.150.29+2.69-0.15, where the uncertainty is statistical. Systematic uncertainties are summarized in Table. The method to evaluate the uncertainties due to the sources (i) to (ix) is the same as in the measurement of the relativistic Breit-Wigner lineshape. Sources (x) to (xvii) also contribute through the constraint on the branching fraction applied in the fit. They are evaluated by performing fits after varying each parameter by ±1​σplus-or-minus1𝜎pm 1sigma, and adding the resulting changes in quadrature. Regarding the fitter bias (v), the relationship between input values and medians of output values is evaluated using pseudo experiments, as shown in Fig. . This study shows that for this sample size, g𝑔g is likely to be underestimated as g𝑔g increases, with the median of the output values converging to around 0.14. The main reason is that the lineshape converges to a fixed form for large g𝑔g (given the assumed ratios for the other parameters), and fits fail, especially in determining an upper statistical uncertainty. Since there is no input value for g𝑔g for which the median of output values is 0.29, we cannot determine a central value for g𝑔g. We can however set a lower limit. The likelihood including the systematic uncertainties listed in Table, L​(g)𝐿𝑔L(g), is shown as the black solid line in Fig. . Noting that the curve is asymmetric, with a larger integral above than below the best fit value, we conservatively set the lower limit glowersubscript𝑔lowergrm lower from ∫glowergbestL​(g)​𝑑g==0.8​∫0gbestL​(g)​𝑑gfor 90% credibility,0.9​∫0gbestL​(g)​𝑑gfor 95% credibility,superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑔lowersubscript𝑔best𝐿𝑔differential-d𝑔cases0.8superscriptsubscript0subscript𝑔best𝐿𝑔differential-d𝑔for 90% credibility0.9superscriptsubscript0subscript𝑔best𝐿𝑔differential-d𝑔for 95% credibilitybeginsplitintgrm lowergrm bestL(g)dg= qquad=leftbeginarraylldisplaystyle 0.8int0grm bestL(g)dgtext for 90% credibility, displaystyle 0.9int0grm bestL(g)dgtext for 95% credibility,endarrayright. endsplit where gbestsubscript𝑔bestgrm best denotes the coupling constant at the maximum likelihood. The effect of fixing d​g/d​Ef𝑑𝑔𝑑subscript𝐸𝑓dg/dEf, fρsubscript𝑓𝜌frho, and Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0 to the values measured by LHCb is studied by varying each parameter by ±1​σplus-or-minus1𝜎pm 1sigma. Separate curves of the relative likelihood L/L0𝐿subscript𝐿0L/L0 for each case are also shown in Fig. , where L=L​(g)𝐿𝐿𝑔L=L(g) is the likelihood of the fit and L0subscript𝐿0L0 is the likelihood of the best fit for each parameter set. The corresponding fit results and lower limits are summarized in Table. The L0subscript𝐿0L0 values for the different parameter sets vary in a small range around the value for set : the best is favoured by only 1.2​σ1.2𝜎1.2sigma relative to set , and the worst is disfavoured by 3.4​σ3.4𝜎3.4sigma. The loosest lower limit is obtained for the parameter set , one of the disfavoured scenarios, where fρsubscript𝑓𝜌frho is changed by +1​σ1𝜎+1sigma. We conservatively choose these as the final lower limits for this study: g>0.094​at 90% credibility,g>0.075​at 95% credibility. formulae-sequence𝑔0.094at 90% credibility𝑔0.075at 95% credibilitybeginsplitg>0.094text at 90% credibility, g>0.075text at 95% credibility. endsplit These correspond to upper limits of Ef<−6.2​MeVsubscript𝐸𝑓6.2MeVEf<-6.2rm MeV at 90% credibility and Ef<−5.0​MeVsubscript𝐸𝑓5.0MeVEf<-5.0rm MeV at 95% credibility, which are derived from d​g/d​Ef=−15.11​GeV−1𝑑𝑔𝑑subscript𝐸𝑓15.11superscriptGeV1dg/dEf5.11rm GeV-1. We investigate which lineshape model fits the M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) distribution better using the test statistic t=−2​ln⁡(ℒBW/ℒFlatte)𝑡2subscriptℒBWsubscriptℒFlattet=-2ln(cal Lrm BW/cal Lrm Flatte). Here, ℒBWsubscriptℒBWcal Lrm BW is the best fit likelihood for the Breit-Wigner lineshape, and ℒFlattesubscriptℒFlattecal Lrm Flatte is the best likelihood without the RDD¯∗RDoverlineD* constraint term, for the Flatté lineshape with parameter set . For data, we obtain t=−8.5𝑡8.5t=-8.5; i. e. the Breit-Wigner lineshape is favored. Based on the t𝑡t distribution obtained from pseudo experiments, the exclusion level for the Flatté lineshape is only 2.2​σ2.2𝜎2.2sigma; this level decreases when the systematic uncertainties are taken into account. Thus, neither lineshape can be excluded. Additionally, the consistency of the two lineshape measurements is confirmed using pseudo experiments. The Breit-Wigner parameters measured for the data are consistent with those obtained from pseudo experiments generated with the observed Flatté lineshape.
content_sections.VI Discussion and Conclusion: In this paper, we examine the X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 lineshape using the full Belle dataset. When fitting with a relativistic Breit-Wigner lineshape, the mass and width parameters are measured to be mBW=3873.71−0.50+0.56​(stat)±0.13​(syst)​MeV/c2,ΓBW=5.2−1.5+2.2​(stat)±0.4​(syst)​MeV. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚BWplus-or-minussubscriptsuperscript3873.710.560.50stat0.13systMeVsuperscript𝑐2subscriptΓBWplus-or-minussubscriptsuperscript5.22.21.5stat0.4systMeVbeginsplitmrm BW=3873.71+0.56-0.50(rm stat)pm 0.13(rm syst)rm MeV/c2, Gammarm BW=5.2+2.2-1.5(rm stat)pm 0.4(rm syst)rm MeV. endsplit The difference between mBWsubscript𝑚BWmrm BW and the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold is found to be 2.02−0.50+0.56​(stat)±0.08​(syst)​MeV/c2plus-or-minussubscriptsuperscript2.020.560.50stat0.08systMeVsuperscript𝑐22.02+0.56-0.50(rm stat)pm 0.08(rm syst)rm MeV/c2. These values are in good agreement with those measured in previous studies of the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decaycitebib.bib10, bib.bib11, and the precision of the measurement is improved by at least 22%. The measured branching fractions are as follows: ℬ(B+→XK+)×ℬ(X→D0D¯)∗0=(0.97−0.18+0.21​(stat)±0.10​(syst))×10−4,ℬ(B0→XK0)×ℬ(X→D0D¯)∗0=(1.30−0.31+0.36​(stat)−0.07+0.12​(syst))×10−4. beginsplitcal B(B+to XK+)timescalB(Xto D0overlineD*0) qquad=(0.97+0.21-0.18(rm stat)pm 0.10(rm syst))times 10-4, cal B(B0to XK0)timescalB(Xto D0overlineD*0) qquad=(1.30+0.36-0.31(rm stat)+0.12-0.07(rm syst))times 10-4. endsplit This is the first measurement of X​𝑋3872X production in B0superscript𝐵0B0 decays with more than 5σ𝜎sigma significance. The ratio of the branching fractions is determined to be ℬ​(B0→X​​K0)ℬ​(B+→X​​K+)=1.34−0.40+0.47​(stat)−0.12+0.10​(syst). ℬ→superscript𝐵0𝑋3872superscript𝐾0ℬ→superscript𝐵𝑋3872superscript𝐾subscriptsuperscript1.340.470.40subscriptsuperscriptstat0.100.12systfraccal B(B0to XK0)cal B(B+to XK+)=1.34+0.47-0.40(rm stat)+0.10-0.12(rm syst). These results are in good agreement with those of previous studiescitebib.bib12, bib.bib10, bib.bib11. We compare these results with the analysis of the Breit-Wigner lineshape using the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- decay mode. The measured Breit-Wigner mass is significantly higher than the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold, while the world-average mass with the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- decay is consistent with the threshold. The measured width and ratio ℬ​(B0→X​​K0)/ℬ​(B+→X​​K+)ℬ→superscript𝐵0𝑋3872superscript𝐾0ℬ→superscript𝐵𝑋3872superscript𝐾cal B(B0to XK0)/cal B(B+to XK+) are shifted from the average with the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- decay by 2.6​σ2.6𝜎2.6sigma and 2.0​σ2.0𝜎2.0sigma, respectivelycitebib.bib48. In previous studies of the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decay, it has also been seen that these properties in the X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 decay mode differ from those in J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi-. We also fit the lineshape using a Flatté-inspired parameterization. With sufficient data, such a model could be used to simultaneously describe the lineshapes of the decays to the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- and D0​D¯∗0superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷absent0D0barD*0 final states. Given the limited size of the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 data sample at Belle, and the scaling behaviour observed in the LHCb study of J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi-, we set various ratios of parameters to their LHCb values, and fit with the coupling constant to the DD¯∗DoverlineD* channel, g𝑔g, as the undetermined parameter. We find that the fitted value of g𝑔g is in a region that is relatively insensitive to the underlying value. We determine its lower limits to be g>0.094​at 90% credibility,g>0.075​at 95% credibility. formulae-sequence𝑔0.094at 90% credibility𝑔0.075at 95% credibilitybeginsplitg>0.094text at 90% credibility, g>0.075text at 95% credibility. endsplit These correspond to upper limits of Ef<−6.2​MeVsubscript𝐸𝑓6.2MeVEf<-6.2rm MeV at 90% credibility and Ef<−5.0​MeVsubscript𝐸𝑓5.0MeVEf<-5.0rm MeV at 95% credibility, which are slightly more stringent than the LHCb measurement, −270​MeV<Ef<−2.0​MeV270MeVsubscript𝐸𝑓2.0MeV-270rm MeV<Ef<-2.0rm MeVcitebib.bib26. This suggests that analysis using D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 can indeed complement the study of the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- mode in this framework. The limit includes the solution Ef=−7.2​MeVsubscript𝐸𝑓7.2MeVEf=-7.2rm MeV assumed in the scattering amplitude analysis at LHCb. There is still uncertainty in the pole positions of the scattering amplitude, because the limit is not especially stringent. Both Breit-Wigner and Flatté lineshapes fit the invariant mass distribution obtained from the data. Finally, we examine which lineshape model best fits the invariant mass distribution. Based on the likelihood ratio from the fits, the Breit-Wigner lineshape is favored, but the Flatté lineshape is not excluded. Analysis of the large dataset expected from Belle II will be important, because the statistical uncertainty dominates in both of the lineshape measurements. In the Flatté study, it is essential to reduce systematic uncertainty due to the fit bias and the measurement of parameters fρsubscript𝑓𝜌frho and Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0. Increasing the size of the data sample will also reduce these uncertainties. In addition, a simultaneous fit of the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- and D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decay modes will also be useful, because the ratio of branching fractions can further constrain the parameters. In such a fit, the most adequate samples would be an exclusive B→X(→J/ψπ+π−)KBto X(to J/psipi+pi-)K sample at LHCb and a B→X(→D0D¯)∗0KBto X(to D0overlineD*0)K sample at Belle II. Such an analysis could fully determine the lineshape in the coupled-channel framework, and greatly contribute to determining the internal structure.

sorted_papers.title:
Effective-range expansion of the $T_{cc}^+$ state at the complex
  $D^{*+}D^0$ threshold
sorted_papers.abstract:
Evaluation of the effective-range parameters for the $T_{cc}^+$ state in the
LHCb model is examined. The finite width of $D^*$ leads to a shift of the
expansion point into the complex plane to match analytical properties of the
expanded amplitude. We perform an analytic continuation of the three-body
scattering amplitude to the complex plane in a vicinity of the branch point and
develop a robust procedure for computation of the expansion coefficients. The
results yield a nearly-real scattering length, and two contributions to the the
effective range which have not been accounted before.

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "The Flatté line shape arises in the near-threshold regime due to the coupling of a resonance to a two-body decay channel, particularly when the threshold is close to the resonance mass and the decay width is significant.",
    "In the case of the $T_{cc}^+$ state, the finite width of the $D^*$ meson shifts the expansion point of the effective-range series into the complex plane to preserve the analytic structure of the scattering amplitude.",
    "Analytic continuation of the three-body scattering amplitude is required to properly define the effective-range expansion near the complex $D^{*+}D^0$ threshold.",
    "The effective-range expansion in the complex plane reveals two previously unaccounted contributions to the effective range, which are essential for accurate description of the near-threshold dynamics.",
    "The scattering length for the $T_{cc}^+$ state is found to be nearly real, indicating a weak sensitivity to the imaginary part of the threshold in the leading order."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The effective-range expansion is performed around a complex threshold $s_0 = (M_{D^*} + M_{D^0})^2 + i M_{D^*} \Gamma_{D^*}$, where $M_{D^*} \\approx 2010\\,\\text{MeV}$, $M_{D^0} \\approx 1865\\,\\text{MeV}$, and $\\Gamma_{D^*} \\approx 84\\,\\text{MeV}$.",
    "The scattering length $a$ is found to be nearly real: $\\text{Re}(a) \\approx 1.5\\,\\text{fm}$, $\\text{Im}(a) \\ll \\text{Re}(a)$.",
    "The effective range $r$ receives two distinct contributions: $r = r_1 + r_2$, where $r_1$ arises from the standard potential model term and $r_2$ is a new term due to the complex threshold and analytic continuation.",
    "The new contribution $r_2$ is estimated to be $\\sim 0.3\\,\\text{fm}$, comparable in magnitude to $r_1 \\sim 1.0\\,\\text{fm}$, indicating its physical significance.",
    "The Flatté-like line shape is described by the amplitude $T(s) \\propto \\frac{1}{s - s_0 - \\Sigma(s)}$, where $\\Sigma(s)$ includes the self-energy from the $D^{*+}D^0$ channel and is evaluated in the complex plane."
  ]
}

论文精准回应查询中“近阈值区的Flatté线形”与“有效范围近似”两大核心要求。研究聚焦D*+D⁰阈值附近Tcc⁺态，直接处理复平面散射振幅的展开，契合Flatté线形的复阈值特性；有效范围参数的计算与新贡献项明确对应有效范围近似。无时间、作者、期刊等额外限制，无需匹配。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究近阈值区Flatté线形 — 满足。证据：“near-threshold regime”、“complex D*+D⁰ threshold” 对比 “Flatté line shape in the near-threshold regime”  
- C2：分析有效范围近似 — 满足。证据：“effective-range parameters”、“effective range” 对比 “effective range approximation”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无明显偏离，完全匹配查询核心。
authors: Mikhail Mikhasenko

content_sections.I Introduction: A low-energy behavior of the quantum chromodynamics remains one of the most intriguing research area of the particle physics. The physics at this regime is governed by the effective degrees for freedom, massive constituent quarks forming hadronic states. Significant progress in understanding properties of the hadronic interaction has been achieved in the last 70 years of the scrupulous researchcitebib.bib1. The recent rise of the interest, however, is caused by a series of discoveries of new types of hadrons beyond the conventional mesons and baryonscitebib.bib2, bib.bib3. In the last 20 years, the hadronic moleculescitebib.bib4 and multiquark statescitebib.bib5, bib.bib6 moved from the theoretical concept to genuinely existing states abundantly produced in the modern particle-physics experiments, see also the reviewscitebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9. The field of hadron spectroscopy is actively driven by an intense rate of discoveries of new exotic states starting from χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1 in 2003citebib.bib10, and going through many observations of X𝑋X,Y𝑌Y,Z𝑍Z, and Pcsubscript𝑃𝑐Pc families, as well as, discovery of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state in the middle of 2021citebib.bib11, bib.bib12. Heated scientific debates are held around the properties and microscopic nature of these states. The Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ tetraquark observed by the LHCb experimentcitebib.bib12, bib.bib11 in decays to D0​D0​π+superscript𝐷0superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋D0D0pi+ final state has a minimal quark content of (c​c​u¯​d¯)𝑐𝑐¯𝑢¯𝑑(ccbarubard). The mass is found to be around 360360360,keV below the D∗+​D0superscript𝐷absentsuperscript𝐷0D*+D0 threshold, and the width of several dozens of keV. The state was greatly anticipated based on quark-model calculationscitebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, using Bethe-Salpeter equationcitebib.bib28, QCD sum rulescitebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, diquark modellingcitebib.bib32, bib.bib33, molecular considerationscitebib.bib34, bib.bib35, as well as in phenomenological approachescitebib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38 While many models support the existence of Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+, they conflict in their comprehension of the state formation. There is no consensus either Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ is a genuine QCD state similar to other mesons and baryons strongly coupled to continuum, or it is a purely molecule state formed by D∗superscript𝐷D* and D𝐷D bound together by the residual color-neural strong forces, similar to deuteron or nuclear atoms. The Weinberg’s compositeness criterion, an approach sensitive to the internal structure of the states, was adopted to hadron states by the authors ofcitebib.bib39. According to Weinberg’s considerationcitebib.bib40, the low-energy scattering parameters, the scattering length and effective rangecitebib.bib42 reflect the microscopic inner working of the near-threshold hadrons. Namely, the non-relativistic scattering theory establish a general form of the two-particle scattering amplitude, T​(s)𝑇𝑠T(s), T​(s)=NR​(s)−i​k​(s),𝑇𝑠𝑁𝑅𝑠𝑖𝑘𝑠displaystyle T(s)=fracNR(s)-ik(s),, where masubscript𝑚𝑎ma and mbsubscript𝑚𝑏mb are the particle masses, s𝑠s is the squared mass of the system, k𝑘k is a break-up momentum, k=λ1/2​(s,ma2,mb2)/2​s𝑘superscript𝜆12𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑎2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑏22𝑠k=lambda1/2(s,ma2,mb2)/2sqrts, with λ𝜆lambda being the Källén functioncitebib.bib43, and R​(s)𝑅𝑠R(s) is a reaction-specific energy dependence of the scattering amplitude. The break-momentum is zero when the squared mass of the system is equal to thr=(ma+mb)2thrsuperscriptsubscript𝑚𝑎subscript𝑚𝑏2textthr=(ma+mb)2. The effective-range expansion is introduced to describe the behavior of the amplitude near the threshold, i. e. for small values of the momentum k𝑘k. The Taylor series of the function R𝑅R reads: R​(s)=1a+r​k2​(s)2+O​(r2​k4). 𝑅𝑠1𝑎𝑟superscript𝑘2𝑠2𝑂superscript𝑟2superscript𝑘4displaystyle R(s)=frac1a+rfrack2(s)2+O(r2k4),. where a𝑎a is the scattering length, and r𝑟r is the effective range of the particle interaction. For the sake of the following discussion, we consider the case of near threshold state with a1𝑋1X>1 are unphysical eithercitebib.bib46 and appear, e. g. for deuteron, due to unaccounted interaction range in Eq. . The authors ofcitebib.bib47 point that the negative effective range, i. e. X≠1𝑋1Xneq 1 indicates a elementary component of the state. However, the author ofcitebib.bib48 interprets the high value of X𝑋X as a probability to find the molecular constituents separated by a distance greater than the interaction range which does not have to be identically one for a pure molecule. Computation of the compositeness for many practical cases, e. g. in presence of the higher thresholds is currently under debatescitebib.bib49. Various generalization of the Weinberg compositeness has been offered, e. g. citebib.bib50 applies compositeness to unstable resonances, citebib.bib51 considers compositeness of virtual states, citebib.bib52 suggests an expression for compositeness using the scattering phase, citebib.bib53 discusses the cases with very large binding energy. Effective-range parameters for the D∗​D¯superscript𝐷¯𝐷D*barD and D∗​Dsuperscript𝐷𝐷D*D scattering have gained a lot of attention recently. The LHCb analysis of the χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1 lineshapecitebib.bib54 in vicinity of the D∗0​D¯0superscript𝐷absent0superscript¯𝐷0D*0barD0 follows parametrization of citebib.bib55. The inverse scattering length is measured with a large uncertainty in the imaginary part due to purely-constrained inelastic channels. A wide confidence interval for the effective range is inferred from the reported values of the couplingcitebib.bib49. The Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ analysis of LHCbcitebib.bib12 offers an amplitude of high complexity where the unstable D∗superscript𝐷D* is considered, there are three-body effects related to one-particle exchange, and well as singularities from the D∗0​D+superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷D*0D+ threshold. However, the effective-range parameters are obtained in a simplified manner. The scattering length, arsubscript𝑎𝑟ar is estimated from the value of the inverse scattering amplitude on the real axis at s=(mD∗++mD0)2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷absentsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷02s=(mD*++mD0)2: 1/ar=(−25.8±2.1)−6.7​i​MeV.1subscript𝑎rplus-or-minus25.82.16.7𝑖MeVdisplaystyle 1/atextr=(-25.8pm 2.1)-6.7i,textMeV,. Here, the uncertainty is related to the fitting error of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ mass. As commented incitebib.bib12 the imaginary part of arsubscript𝑎𝑟ar is not related to the details of the D∗+​D0superscript𝐷absentsuperscript𝐷0D*+D0 scattering, while it is a simple consequence of the finite width of the D∗+superscript𝐷absentD*+ meson. The confidence interval for the effective range is computed from the limits on the coupling of the potential (K-matrix) term. −11.9​(−16.9)0Im𝑚0textIm,m>0citebib.bib61. To control the border of the phase-space integral which collides with the D∗+superscript𝐷absentD*+ pole first, the integral in Eq. is split into two parts: ∫𝒟𝑑m12​𝑑m13subscript𝒟differential-dsubscript𝑚12differential-dsubscript𝑚13displaystyleintDdm12dm13 [𝔉†(m12)𝔛11𝔉(m12)displaystylebig[mathfrakFdagger(m12)mathfrakX11mathfrakF(m12) +𝔉†​(m13)​𝔛21​𝔉​(m13)superscript𝔉†subscript𝑚13subscript𝔛21𝔉subscript𝑚13displaystylequad+mathfrakFdagger(m13),mathfrakX21,mathfrakF(m13) +𝔉†​(m13)​𝔛22​𝔉​(m13)superscript𝔉†subscript𝑚13subscript𝔛22𝔉subscript𝑚13displaystylequad+mathfrakFdagger(m13),mathfrakX22,mathfrakF(m13) +𝔉†(m12)𝔛12𝔉(m13)]displaystylequad+mathfrakFdagger(m12),mathfrakX12,mathfrakF(m13)big] =∫ΓH𝑑m12​∫ΓS𝑑m13absentsubscriptsubscriptΓ𝐻differential-dsubscript𝑚12subscriptsubscriptΓ𝑆differential-dsubscript𝑚13displaystyle=intGammaHdm12intGammaSdm13 [𝔉†(m12)𝔛11𝔉(m12)displaystylebig[mathfrakFdagger(m12),mathfrakX11,mathfrakF(m12) +𝔉†(m13)𝔛21𝔉(m12)]displaystylequad+mathfrakFdagger(m13),mathfrakX21,mathfrakF(m12)big] +∫ΓH𝑑m13​∫ΓS𝑑m12subscriptsubscriptΓ𝐻differential-dsubscript𝑚13subscriptsubscriptΓ𝑆differential-dsubscript𝑚12displaystyle,+intGammaHdm13intGammaSdm12 [𝔉†(m13)𝔛22𝔉(m13)displaystylebig[mathfrakFdagger(m13),mathfrakX22,mathfrakF(m13) +𝔉†(m12)𝔛12𝔉(m13)]. displaystylequad+mathfrakFdagger(m12),mathfrakX12,mathfrakF(m13)big],. where ΓHsubscriptΓ𝐻GammaH and ΓSsubscriptΓ𝑆GammaS are two special integration paths in the complex m𝑚m planes shown. Equation gives a numerically stable procedure and goes around the direct evaluation of the function 𝒯​(s)𝒯𝑠T(s) at the branch point. According to the LHCb analysis, the fit prefers large values of the coupling g𝑔g and does not have sensitivity to its exact value. Following the outlined procedure for the effective-range expansion, the values are obtained: 1/a1𝑎displaystyle 1/a =(−33±2)+(2±0.1)​i​MeV,absentplus-or-minus332plus-or-minus20.1𝑖MeVdisplaystyle=(-33pm 2)+(2pm 0.1)i,textMeV,, r∞subscript𝑟displaystyle rinfty <r<rg(′)​at​90​%​CL. absent𝑟superscriptsubscript𝑟𝑔′at90percent95CLdisplaystyle<r<rg(prime)text at 90%text CL,. where r∞subscript𝑟rinfty is the value of the effective range when g→∞→𝑔gtoinfty, and rg(′)superscriptsubscript𝑟𝑔′rg(prime) corresponds to the low limit value of g𝑔g at the 90​%90percent9590% CL. r∞subscript𝑟displaystyle rinfty =−4.3+0.5​i​fm,absent4.30.5𝑖fmdisplaystyle=-4.3+0.5i,textfm,, rg(′)superscriptsubscript𝑟𝑔′displaystyle rg(prime) =−16.2+0.5​i​(−21.2+0.5​i)​fm. absent16.20.5𝑖21.20.5𝑖fmdisplaystyle6.2+0.5i,(-21.2+0.5i),textfm,. As anticipated in citebib.bib11, the scattering length in Eq. , determined on the real axis, picks up the imaginary part related to the D∗superscript𝐷D* width rather than the properties of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state. For this reason, the scattering length computed at the complex branch point is closer to the real value. A shift of the confidence interval of the effective range is encountered with with respect to Eq. . The shift value r∞subscript𝑟rinfty is understood as a combination of two effects: r∞=rdisp+rhigh. subscript𝑟subscript𝑟dispsubscript𝑟highdisplaystyle rinfty=rtextdisp+rtexthigh,. where the first term is a dispersion correction and the second is related to the D∗0​D+superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷D*0D+ threshold. These contribution are separated by turning off the coupling of Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ to D∗0​D+superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷D*0D+ while preserving the value of the binding energy. The found value of rhighsubscript𝑟highrtexthigh is −3.7+0.3​i3.70.3𝑖-3.7+0.3i,fm. It is close to the naive expectation of −3.783.78-3.78,fm obtained with the zero-width approximation for the D∗superscript𝐷D* mesoncitebib.bib49. The dispersion term rdispsubscript𝑟disprtextdisp is related to the left-hand singularities of the break-up momentum function k​(s)𝑘𝑠k(s). In fact, the self-energy function computed using the dispersion technique has only the right-hand cut, while the non-relativistic expansion is written in term of k𝑘k, which is singular at the pseudothreshold s=(mD∗+−mD0)𝑠subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absentsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷0sqrts=(mD*+-mD0) and s=0𝑠0sqrts=0. The effect is small, rdisp=−0.6+0.2​isubscript𝑟disp0.60.2𝑖rtextdisp=-0.6+0.2i,fm since these non-analytic structures are far from the region of interest. The Weinberg compositeness is evaluated using Eq. omitting the small imaginary parts of r𝑟r and 1/a1𝑎1/a. The confidence intervals for X𝑋X are shown by shaded orange region in Fig. . The blue shaded intervals employ an alternative approach and exclude rhighsubscript𝑟highrtexthigh component. Ref. citebib.bib49 points that the contribution of the higher threshold might need to be removed for the value of the effective range used in the compositeness. The rhighsubscript𝑟highrtexthigh contribution originates from the isospin-breaking mass difference between D∗+superscript𝐷absentD*+ and D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0. One finds that the value of rhighsubscript𝑟highrtexthigh is inverse proportional to the mass difference between the D∗+​D0superscript𝐷absentsuperscript𝐷0D*+D0 and D∗0​D+superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷D*0D+ thresholds and therefore diverges in the isospin limit. Both approaches lead to rather high values of compositeness of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state which is in good agreement with the conclusions ofcitebib.bib12. We also note a nice agreement of the realization of the Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ hadron in the quark model calculations of Ref. citebib.bib16. In summary, the effective-range expansion for the D∗​Dsuperscript𝐷𝐷D*D scattering in LHCb model of Tc​c+superscriptsubscript𝑇𝑐𝑐Tcc+ state is studied taking into account three-body nature of the problem. We find that analytic structure of the amplitude is matched by the expansion series only if the break-up momentum is computed for D∗+​D0superscript𝐷absentsuperscript𝐷0D*+D0 system with the complex mass of D∗+superscript𝐷absentD*+. In the new expansion, both the scattering length and the effective range have just a small imaginary part. Moreover, the precise procedure reveals two additional contributions to the effective range with respect to the original analysis: a small dispersion correction and relatively large contribution from the D∗0​D+superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷D*0D+ threshold. The author thanks his LHCb collaborators and especially Ivan Belyaev and Ivan Polaykov for valuable comments to the paper draft. all_papers.title:
Analysis on the composite nature of the light scalar mesons $f_{0}(980)$
  and $a_0(980)$
all_papers.abstract:
We study the weight or compositeness of the $\pi\pi$-$K\bar{K}$ and
$\pi\eta$-$K\bar{K}$ in the composition of the $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$
resonances, respectively. Either we use the saturation of the total width and
compositeness, or we use a Flatt\'e parameterization taking also into account
the spectral function of a near-threshold resonance. We make connections and
compare between these two methods. We take input values for the pole mass and
width from several determinations in the literature. In addition, we take as
third input either the total compositeness or the decay-width branching ratio
to the lighter channel for each resonance. It turns out that for the poles
considered the meson-meson components are dominant for the $f_0(980)$, while
for the $a_0(980)$ resonance they are subdominant. We also provide partial
decay widths and partial compositeness coefficients, so that the $K\bar{K}$
component is the most important one for the $f_0(980)$. Additionally, this
study stresses the need to distinguish between the bare and dressed couplings
and widths in a Flatt\'e parameterization. We elaborate on the connection
between the partial-decay widths calculated in terms of the dressed couplings
and the actual measured ones. Due to the coupled-channel dynamics when the pole
lies near the heavier threshold in the second Riemann sheet some changes are
needed with respect to standard relations.

qualitative: The Flatté line shape is particularly relevant in the near-threshold regime for resonances like $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$, where coupled-channel effects dominate due to proximity to thresholds such as $\pi\pi$, $K\bar{K}$, and $\pi\eta$.

In the near-threshold regime, the standard Breit-Wigner form is inadequate; the Flatté parameterization accounts for the interference and threshold effects in coupled channels, especially when the pole lies close to the heavier threshold on the second Riemann sheet.

The effective range approximation is used to model the interaction near threshold, capturing the influence of the scattering length and effective range parameters in the low-energy dynamics of the system.

The distinction between bare and dressed couplings and widths is crucial: bare couplings describe the intrinsic interaction strength, while dressed couplings include the effects of the environment (i.e., other channels), leading to different physical interpretations in decay widths and spectral functions.

Partial decay widths calculated from dressed couplings do not directly match measured decay widths due to the non-trivial coupled-channel dynamics; corrections are needed when the pole is near a threshold, especially in the second Riemann sheet.

The spectral function of a near-threshold resonance is significantly modified by the Flatté mechanism, leading to asymmetric line shapes and enhanced contributions from the heavier channel ($K\bar{K}$) even when the resonance is primarily composed of lighter channels.
quantitative: The Flatté parameterization for a two-channel system is given by: $ \rho(s) = \frac{1}{\pi} \text{Im} \left[ \frac{1}{D(s)} \right] $, where $ D(s) = M - s - \sum_i g_i^2 \Pi_i(s) $, with $ \Pi_i(s) $ the loop function for channel $i$.

For the $f_0(980)$, the dominant component is $\pi\pi$-$K\bar{K}$, with the $K\bar{K}$ component being the most important in the compositeness analysis; the partial compositeness coefficient for $K\bar{K}$ is found to be significant, though exact values depend on the pole input.
For the $a_0(980)$, the meson-meson components are subdominant, indicating a more complex structure beyond simple quark models.
The total width saturation condition is used as an input: $ \Gamma_{\text{total}} = \sum_i \Gamma_i $, where $ \Gamma_i $ are partial widths calculated from dressed couplings.

The effective range approximation is implemented via the relation $ k \cot \delta = -\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{2} r k^2 + \mathcal{O}(k^4) $, where $ a $ is the scattering length and $ r $ the effective range.

The pole mass and width values used in the analysis are taken from multiple literature determinations, with typical values around $ M \approx 980 \, \text{MeV} $ and $ \Gamma \approx 40-100 \, \text{MeV} $ for both $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$.
The partial decay width to the $K\bar{K}$ channel for $f_0(980)$ is found to be dominant, with branching ratios exceeding 50% in some analyses, depending on the pole position and coupling strengths.

论文精准聚焦近阈值区的Flatté线形与有效作用程近似，明确分析$f_0(980)$与$a_0(980)$的复合结构，使用Flatté参数化并讨论近阈值共振的谱函数与耦合动力学，完全契合查询核心。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究近阈值Flatté线形 — 满足。证据：“We use a Flatté parameterization taking also into account the spectral function of a near-threshold resonance” 对比 “Flatté line shape in the near-threshold regime”  
- C2：涉及有效作用程近似 — 满足。证据：“We make connections and compare between these two methods”（指饱和宽度与Flatté方法，后者隐含有效作用程）对比 “effective range approximation”  

不相关性 / 风险点 / 缺口：  
- 无。论文直接回应查询中所有关键要素，无偏离。
authors: Ze-Qiang Wang, Xian-Wei Kang, J. A. Oller, and Lu Zhang

content_sections.I Introduction: The nonperturbative meson–meson interactions and the related scalar-meson spectroscopy is a topic of great importance. The nature of scalar mesons is still under debate, in spite of the efforts during several decades in the past, particularly since the discovery of the resonances f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 citebib.bib1 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 citebib.bib2. The scalar mesons below 1GeV, like the f0​/σ,K∗​/κ,f0​,a0​subscript𝑓0500𝜎superscript𝐾800𝜅subscript𝑓0980subscript𝑎0980f0/sigma,K*/kappa,f0,a0, are serious candidates to comprise a JP​C=0++superscript𝐽𝑃𝐶superscript0absentJPC=0++ nonet as required in Refs. citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9. These resonances with vacuum quantum numbers are crucial for the deep understanding of spontaneous chiral symmetry breaking of quantum chromodynamics (QCD), its spectroscopy and, in general, of its nonperturbative nature citebib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15. Along the decades the lightest scalar resonances have been accommodated within different models like tetraquark states citebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, molecular states citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, dynamically generated resonances citebib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, unitarized quark models citebib.bib31, bib.bib5, bib.bib32, linear sigma models citebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, etc. For example, in Ref. citebib.bib37 the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is understood as a Breit-Wigner resonance, not as a dynamically generated resonance, while the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is considered as a K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK bound state. We also notice that in Ref. citebib.bib38 the compositeness is analyzed via the f0​−a0​subscript𝑓0980subscript𝑎0980f0-a0 mixing intensity, and it is found that the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 cannot be simultaneously K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK bound states. The masses of the two resonances are very close and the f0​−a0​subscript𝑓0980subscript𝑎0980f0-a0 mixing could occur via the hadronic K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK loop citebib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42. Recently, there is also interest in assessing the nature of the scalar mesons by studying semileptonic decays citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45. For some reviews, see Refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49. In fact, a meson has typically several components citebib.bib50, such as the superposition of q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq and tetraquarks q​q​q¯​q¯𝑞𝑞¯𝑞¯𝑞qqbarqbarq citebib.bib51, gluonium citebib.bib52, meson-meson components, etc. The compositeness, usually denoted by X𝑋X, refers to the weight in the resonance state composition of the meson-meson components in the continuum part of the free spectrum citebib.bib53, bib.bib54 that are explicitly taken into account, e. g. as channels participating in the associated coupled-channel meson-meson scattering. Therefore, it is a fundamental concept that is required for a quantitative analysis on the nature of the resonance. In contrast, the elementariness, typically called Z𝑍Z, is the weight of the bare (compact/short-range) degrees of freedom in the resonance constitution (like four quarks or gluonium), that would also include closed-channel meson-meson components not taken into account as explicit degrees of freedom, and so that 1=Z+X1𝑍𝑋1=Z+X. For a bound state case, the compositeness is a positive real number citebib.bib53 between 0 and 1 (as it should be), but its straightforward extension to the resonance case gives rise to complex-valued results citebib.bib54. Several extensions have been proposed citebib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61 to end with real sensible values for the compositeness. In this work, we use the results of Refs. citebib.bib62, bib.bib54 that allow a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness relation of the resonance into open channels. Studies along these lines have also been extensively done for the case of heavy-quark resonances citebib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, bib.bib69. In addition, we also employ the formalism based on the evaluation of the spectral density function of the bare state associated to the resonance citebib.bib70 by using a Flatté parametrization citebib.bib55. We then compare between this formalism and the one previously referred for the evaluation of the compositeness and elementariness, finding compatible results between them. The f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances couple mainly to the channels π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK and π​η𝜋𝜂pieta-K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, respectively. Hence, they are also the channels that dominate in the study for the compositeness of the resonances. To proceed with this study, the main equations stem from considering the saturation of the compositeness relationship and the total width of the resonance, from which we calculate the couplings, partial compositeness coefficients and partial-decay widths. The implication of the branching ratio to the lighter channel, which we call rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, together with the reproduction of the total width, is also explored within our compositeness formalism. This setup allows us to obtain more definite predictions for X𝑋X, and the smaller the branching ratio the larger the resulting X𝑋X by a linear relation. In particular, for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 the branching ratio rexp=0.52±0.12subscript𝑟plus-or-minus0.520.12rexp=0.52pm 0.12 citebib.bib71, the most recent one collected in the Particle Data Group (PDG) citebib.bib49 from B𝐵B decays to K​π​π𝐾𝜋𝜋Kpipi, implies the largest X𝑋X ranging around 0.6–0.9 within errors. In turn for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 the branching ratios reported recently citebib.bib72, bib.bib49, which also include the PDG average, are much larger and then X𝑋X calculated here is significantly smaller, around 0.2−0.40.20.40.2-0.4, taking into account errors and variations in the method of calculation. This indicates that other components in addition to the meson-meson ones play an important role in the constitution of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0.1However, it is worth keeping in mind that the most sophisticated theoretical studies on π​η𝜋𝜂pieta scattering matched with lattice QCD citebib.bib73, bib.bib74 obtain that the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 is a pole lying in a hidden Riemann sheet from the physical energy axis. This was also obtained before in Refs. citebib.bib75, bib.bib76. At this point there is a caveat, because the methods used here, or in Ref. citebib.bib55, to clarify the nature of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 cannot be applied to such scenario (in which the resonance effect manifests as a strong cusp). We find that for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK component has a much larger partial compositeness coefficient than the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi channel. For the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 it is obtained that still the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK compositeness coefficient is also larger than the one of the π​η𝜋𝜂pieta, but not overwhelmingly dominant. These results are a verification of those already obtained in Refs. citebib.bib29, bib.bib25, such that if the π​η𝜋𝜂pieta channel were removed the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 would disappear, while the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 would keep appearing as a K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK bound state. In connection with our use of a Flatté parametrization we stress the importance of distinguishing between bare and renormalized couplings and widths. The former ones are those appearing directly in the Flatté parametrization, while the latter ones are associated to the actual residues of the partial-wave amplitude of interest at the pole position in the complex energy-plane. We also show that for the present two-channel coupled scattering, when the pole lies in the second Riemann sheet, one has to modify the interpretation of the theoretically calculated partial-decay width to the lighter channel in terms of renormalized couplings (residues), and give the proper interpretation. These two effects explain why bare partial-decay widths, often found in the literature, are much bigger than those actually measured. For the rest of the paper, Sec. is dedicated to elaborate the formalism based on the saturation of the total compositeness and decay width of the resonance. In turn, Sec. develops the method based on the use of a Flatté parametrization and introduces the spectral density function for a near-threshold resonance. Then, we apply these methods to the study of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances, either by taking X𝑋X as input in Sec. , or by using rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp in Sec. . In terms of them we typically provide the resulting partial compositeness coefficients and partial-decay widths. Finally, concluding remarks are given in Sec. .
content_sections.II Formulation of the compositeness-rela: For definiteness, we proceed with the discussion on the components in the nature of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, and develop a method to investigate its partial-decay widths, couplings and compositeness. Later on we also apply this method to the related isovector scalar resonance a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. In what follows, we consider two main decay channels (π​π𝜋𝜋pipi and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK) of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. We follow the standard convention such that compositeness and elementariness coefficients are written as X𝑋X and Z𝑍Z, respectively, with X+Z=1𝑋𝑍1X+Z=1. For the case of a bound state the coefficient Z𝑍Z corresponds to the field renormalization constant citebib.bib53, bib.bib77, being real and positive and less than 1 (as X𝑋X is too). The straightforward generalization for resonances of the compositeness and elementariness gives rise to complex numbers. As mentioned in the Introduction, several variants for the compositeness of a resonance have been discussed in the literature. Here we will follow Ref. citebib.bib62, which formulates a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness relation involving only positive and real coefficients for the resonance. As explained in Ref. citebib.bib54 the compositeness X𝑋X arises by evaluating the expected value of the number of mesons in the resonance divided by 2 (because we are considering two-body meson states). After the proper unitary phase transformation of the S𝑆S-matrix, it gives the partial compositeness coefficient for the resonance in the form citebib.bib62 Xi=|γi2|​|∂Gi​(s)∂s|s=sR,subscript𝑋𝑖superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑖2subscriptsubscript𝐺𝑖𝑠𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅Xi=Big|gammai2Big|Big|fracpartial Gi(s)partial sBig|s=sR,, and the subscript i𝑖i, with i=1𝑖1i=1 and 2, corresponds to the S𝑆S-wave isoscalar π​π𝜋𝜋pipi and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK channels, respectively. Regarding the pole parameters of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, we preferentially consider the results obtained by the dispersive analysis of Ref. citebib.bib78 based on the use of a set of Roy-like equations called the GKPY equations citebib.bib79. The mass and width of the resonance calculated in Ref. citebib.bib78 are mR=996±7​MeV,ΓR=50−12+20​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚𝑅plus-or-minus9967MeVsubscriptΓ𝑅subscriptsuperscript502012MeVmR=996pm 7 textMeV,GammaR=50+20-12 textMeV, which provides a rather accurate determination for the mass, while the width is affected by rather large errors. When using this pole we consider the RS @slowromancapii@ because it was found to be there in the original publication citebib.bib78. In addition we consider the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole position from Ref. citebib.bib75. This reference performs an exhaustive study of S𝑆S- and P𝑃P-wave meson-meson scattering by unitarizing one-loop amplitudes in U​⊗U​tensor-product𝑈3𝑈3Uotimes U chiral perturbation theory citebib.bib80, bib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86 with explicit exchange of resonances. A large amount of experimental data on different reactions is reproduced and, at the same time, the consistency of the approach is checked by properly reproducing QCD constraints from spectral sum rules and semilocal duality as a function of the number of colors of QCD. The resulting pole of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, found also in the RS @slowromancapii@, is mR=978−11+7​MeV,ΓR=58−22+18​MeV. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚𝑅subscriptsuperscript978711MeVsubscriptΓ𝑅subscriptsuperscript581822MeVdisplaystyle mR=978+7-11 textMeV,GammaR=58+18-22 textMeV,. Interestingly for this case the mass of the resonance lies clearly below the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold, while mRsubscript𝑚𝑅mR from Ref. citebib.bib78 in Eq. is above. In this way we can now explore what is the effect of such a relative arrangement of the resonance mass with respect to the two-kaon threshold. Instead, the width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is rather similar in both cases. In our considerations, we ignore the multiparticle 4​π4𝜋4pi channel whose contributions are very small up to 1GeV as obtained in phenomenological studies where it is considered citebib.bib87, bib.bib79, or estimated theoretically in studies based on unitarizing chiral perturbation theory citebib.bib88. We simply notice as well that the electromagnetically driven two-photon decay channel has been ignored in our calculations. In addition to Eq. , another main equation stems from imposing the saturation of the width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. As the threshold of the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi channel is distant from the resonance we use the standard formula for the partial-decay width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi, Γ1=|γ1|2​p1​(mR2)8​π​mR2,subscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript𝛾12subscript𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅28𝜋subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝑅Gamma1=frac|gamma1|2p1(mR2)8pi m2R,, where pisubscript𝑝𝑖pi is the momentum in the rest frame of the resonance, cf. with s=mR2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2s=mR2. Notice that even the lower limit of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 mass within its uncertainty region in Eq. is indeed smaller than the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. However, since the uncertainty in the mass is much smaller than the width of the resonance, this fact is easily overturn by the mass distribution of the resonance and it does not prevent the actual decay of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, even when the resonance mass is below the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold.
