For our consideration, we restrict the upper integration limit in the resonance region to mR+2​ΓRsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscriptΓ𝑅mR+2GammaR (which comprises the resonance signal as it can be seen in Fig. , introduced in Sec. within the context of a Flatté parametrization).3We want to stress here that the use of a Flatté parametrization, cf. Sec. , does not make use of any specific expression for Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 as a function of γ2subscript𝛾2gamma2, like Eq. . However, this equation has such a clear physical insight that the results are compatible with those from a Flatté parametrization, as it will be shown in Secs. and when discussing results. Another aspect to take into account is the RS in which the pole lies because the sign of the momentum of the kaons in the center of mass reference frame has opposite signs in the RSs @slowromancapii@ and @slowromancapiii@ at the pole position. Notice that for the latter RS the kaon momentum has the standard sign in the lower half of the complex energy-plane, corresponding to a mR−i​Γ1/2−i​Γ2/2subscript𝑚𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ12𝑖subscriptΓ22mR-iGamma1/2-iGamma2/2, while for the former one has instead mR−i​Γ1/2+i​Γ2/2subscript𝑚𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ12𝑖subscriptΓ22mR-iGamma1/2+iGamma2/2. Because this change of sign in the kaon momentum the saturation of the resonance width obtained from the pole position varies, such that Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 standardly adds to Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 when the pole lies in the RS @slowromancapiii@, but Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 subtracts from Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 when the pole lies in the RS @slowromancapii@. These points are further elaborated when considering a Flatté parametrization for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances in Sec. . Combining Eq. and Eq. allows us to solve |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1| and |γ2|subscript𝛾2|gamma2| in terms of the total compositeness and width. Then we can obtain the partial-decay width ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai and individual compositeness coefficient Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi for each channel.4The fact that the width of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 is substantially larger than the difference between its mass and the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold allows to apply in a reasonable way the standard formula Eq. for the partial compositeness X2subscript𝑋2X2, even if the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole lies in the 2nd (3rd) RS above (below) the nearby K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. The mass distribution of the resonance smooths the sharp condition, alluded after Eq. , on the relative position between the resonance mass and the threshold of K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK when their distance is much smaller than the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 width. We consider several choices for X𝑋X in Eq. , typically from 0.2 up to 0.8 in steps of 0.2. The results following one way or the other are organized in Secs. and , respectively. They are also applied in analogous way to the isovector scalar a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 involving the scattering channels π​η𝜋𝜂pieta and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, with rexp=Γ​(a0​→π​η)/[Γ​(a0​→π​η)+Γ​(a0​→K​K¯)]subscript𝑟expΓ→subscript𝑎0980𝜋𝜂delimited-Γ→subscript𝑎0980𝜋𝜂Γ→subscript𝑎0980𝐾¯𝐾rrm exp=Gamma(a0rightarrowpieta)/[Gamma(a0rightarrowpieta)+Gamma(a0rightarrow KbarK)] then.
content_sections.III Flatté parametrization and the spect: One disadvantage of the approach followed in Sec. is the necessity to assume a value of n𝑛n for the upper limit of integration in Eq. for evaluating the partial-decay width of the resonance into K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, that is, Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2. This can be overcome by using a Flatté parametrization citebib.bib92, without increasing the number of the input parameters needed to calculate the couplings |γi|subscript𝛾𝑖|gammai|, partial-decay widths ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai and partial compositeness coefficients Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi. Since the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 lie very close to the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold a Flatté parametrization is then especially suitable citebib.bib55.5Let us notice that the method of Sec. , based on the saturation of the total width and compositeness of the resonance, can also be applied to resonances not necessarily lying near a main threshold, like wider or heavier ones. As discussed in Refs. citebib.bib93, bib.bib65, there is another limitation in the use of a Flatté parametrization as it assumes that the corresponding K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK partial-wave amplitude has no zero in the near-threshold region. We assume that this is the case and proceed with the rather intuitive picture offered by a Flatté parametrization of dressing a bare resonance propagator, 1/D​(E)1𝐷𝐸1/D(E), by the self energy due to the intermediate channels 1 and 2, D​(E)𝐷𝐸displaystyle D(E) =E−Ef+i​Γ12+i2​g2​mK​E. absent𝐸subscript𝐸𝑓𝑖subscriptΓ12𝑖2subscript𝑔2subscript𝑚𝐾𝐸displaystyle=E-Ef+ifracwidetildeGamma12+fraci2g2sqrtmKE. Here E𝐸E is the total center of mass energy measured with respect to the two-kaon threshold, E≡s−2​mK𝐸𝑠2subscript𝑚𝐾Eequivsqrts-2mK, Efsubscript𝐸𝑓Ef is the bare mass of the resonance plus the contributions at around the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold from the real parts (which are taken as constants) of the meson-meson loops contributing to the resonance self energy. In addition, gisubscript𝑔𝑖gi is the bare coupling squared of the resonance to the ithsubscript𝑖thirm th channel, such that the bare width Γ1subscriptΓ1widetildeGamma1 to channel 1 is written in terms of g1subscript𝑔1g1 as Γ1subscriptΓ1displaystylewidetildeGamma1 =p1​(mR)​g18​π​mR2. absentsubscript𝑝1subscript𝑚𝑅subscript𝑔18𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2displaystyle=fracp1(mR)g18pi mR2. The pole position in the variable E𝐸E is called ER=MR−i​ΓR/2subscript𝐸𝑅subscript𝑀𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ𝑅2ER=MR-iGammaR/2, with MRsubscript𝑀𝑅MR the mass of the resonance with respect to 2​mK2subscript𝑚𝐾2mK, MR=mR−2​mKsubscript𝑀𝑅subscript𝑚𝑅2subscript𝑚𝐾MR=mR-2mK. This more detailed discussion based on the use of the Flatté parametrization extends the same topic already discussed in Sec. for the interpretation of the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai’s and their connection with the experimental decay widths for the case of a pole lying in the RS @slowromancapii@. It is worth stressing that when X𝑋X is taken as input the procedure explained above, Eqs. -, allows to calculate Γ1/ΓRsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅Gamma1/GammaR without relying on its connection to rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, and the resulting values, which we discuss below, are perfectly compatible with the picture just explained. An interesting outcome of our study is that by combining outputs from different sources we are able to constrain further the knowledge on the nature of the resonances f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 than by just relying on one reference. This is clear by considering the huge uncertainty in the decay branching ratios Γ1/ΓRsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅Gamma1/GammaR deduced in Eq. from Refs. citebib.bib78 and citebib.bib75. However, later on we will use these same references for the mass and width and then take the branching ratio from other studies so that one can then conclude a tighter information on the nature for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. Related to this, we will be able to deduce values for the dressed coupling squared |γ2|2superscriptsubscript𝛾22|gamma2|2 which cannot be provided by the formalism of Refs. citebib.bib78, bib.bib75. Similar remarks also hold for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. Finally, let us now show the relationship between the sign σ𝜎sigma and the RS in which the pole lies. For that, we isolate ERsubscript𝐸𝑅sqrtER from Eq. , which can then be written as ERsubscript𝐸𝑅displaystylesqrtER =2​ig2​mK​(MR−Ef)+ΓR−Γ1g2​mK. absent2𝑖subscript𝑔2subscript𝑚𝐾subscript𝑀𝑅subscript𝐸𝑓subscriptΓ𝑅subscriptΓ1subscript𝑔2subscript𝑚𝐾displaystyle=frac2ig2sqrtmK(MR-Ef)+fracGammaR-widetildeGamma1g2sqrtmK. This equation tells us that if MR−Ef>0subscript𝑀𝑅subscript𝐸𝑓0MR-Ef>0 the pole lies in the RS @slowromancapii@, since then ℑ⁡ER>0subscript𝐸𝑅0ImsqrtER>0 and, conversely, if MR−Ef0subscript𝑀𝑅subscript𝐸𝑓0MR-Ef>0 and the pole lies in the RS @slowromancapii@ for σ=+1𝜎1sigma=+1. We give the results obtained with the present formalism based on the use of the Flatté parametrization, distinguishing between when X𝑋X or rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp are taken as inputs in Secs. and , respectively.

Here we use the spectral density function ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) of a near-threshold resonance, in our case either f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 or a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, as a way to calculate the compositeness of the meson-meson states in these resonances. We follow the formalism of Ref. citebib.bib55 to which we refer for further details. There the spectral density function ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) is introduced, and it provides the probability distribution function in energy for finding a bare elementary state in the continuum citebib.bib70. As a result, its integration around the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold comprising the resonance signal, which we call WRsubscript𝑊𝑅WR, is the probability for finding the bare state. The use of Eq. allows to determine |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1|, which is certainly larger than the “effective” one deduced by fulfilling Eq. with an intermediate plus sign. However, the values for X2subscript𝑋2X2, Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 and the physical partial-decay width to channel 1 almost do not change, which are typically the most important pieces of information to infer about the nature of the resonances. Now, if rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp is taken as input the equations that one has to fulfill are Eq. and 2−rexp=|γ1|2​p1​(mR2)8​π​mR2​ΓR,2subscript𝑟expsuperscriptsubscript𝛾12subscript𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅28𝜋subscriptsuperscript𝑚2𝑅subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle 2-rrm exp=|gamma1|2fracp1(mR2)8pi m2RGammaR, which are apparently very different to the “standard” Eq. and rexp=Γ1ΓR,subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle rrm exp=fracGamma1GammaR, already written for a pole in the RS @slowromancapiii@ in Sec. . However, they basically provide again the same results for Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2, X2subscript𝑋2X2, and the physical partial-decay width to channel 1, while the use of Eqs. and provides the already mentioned “effective” value for |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1|. In order to see it, let us divide Eq. by ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR and write the following equations equivalent to Eqs. and , 11displaystyle 1 =Γ1ΓR−Γ2ΓR,absentsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅subscriptΓ2subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle=fracGamma1GammaR-fracGamma2GammaR, 2−rexp2subscript𝑟expdisplaystyle 2-rrm exp =Γ1ΓR. absentsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle=fracGamma1GammaR. Subtracting the first line from the second one we then have 1−rexp1subscript𝑟expdisplaystyle 1-rrm exp =Γ2ΓR,absentsubscriptΓ2subscriptΓ𝑅displaystyle=fracGamma2GammaR, which fixes |γ2|subscript𝛾2|gamma2| as if Eq. were used. Afterwards, Eq. is employed to calculate |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1|, while the use of Eq. would provide the so-called “effective” value for |γ1|subscript𝛾1|gamma1|, which would be smaller than the one obtained from Eq. .
content_sections.IV Results and discussions using the tot: Here we apply the formalism derived in Secs. and to study the nature of the resonances f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. Subsequently, the former method based on the saturation of ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR and X𝑋X is denoted by S, while the latter one based on the use of a Flatté parametrization is called F𝐹F. For each resonance pole we first apply the method S and then F.

Assuming given values for the total compositeness X𝑋X of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, varying it from 0.20.20.2 to 1.01.01.0 in steps of 0.2, we obtain the couplings, partial-decay widths, and compositeness coefficients by solving Eq. and Eq. . We indicate that it is not possible to find solutions for the parameters to reproduce X=0𝑋0X=0, because then the couplings would be zero which is in contradiction of having a finite width. As already mentioned, we consider the RS @slowromancapii@ where sRsubscript𝑠𝑅sR of Eq. lies citebib.bib78 and the results calculated are shown in Table. One can observe from this table that the compositeness coefficient X2subscript𝑋2X2 is always much larger than X1subscript𝑋1X1, which means that the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK channel plays a much more important role than the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi one in the structure of f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. Related to this point, the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 couples much more strongly to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK than to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi, in agreement with the fact that f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 sits very close to the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. We also observe from Table that as the total compositeness X𝑋X increases the physical partial-decay width to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi (here, for a RSII pole, Γπ​π=Γ1−2​Γ2subscriptΓ𝜋𝜋subscriptΓ12subscriptΓ2Gammapipi=Gamma1-2Gamma2) decreases, while that to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK becomes larger. Similarly, this fact has also been found in other heavy-quark resonances with open and near-threshold channels citebib.bib69, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, bib.bib94. In these regards, it follows from Table that ΓK​K¯>Γπ​πsubscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾subscriptΓ𝜋𝜋GammaKbarK>Gammapipi for X≳0.6greater-than-or-equivalent-to𝑋0.6Xgtrsim 0.6,7This can be easily seen because ΓK​K¯>25subscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾25GammaKbarK>25MeV. but as X𝑋X decreases Γπ​πsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜋Gammapipi becomes larger than ΓK​K¯subscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾GammaKbarK. For estimating the errors associated to the input values of the resonance pole mass and width we proceed in this work similarly as done in Ref. citebib.bib64. Then, we discretize the inputs, mRsubscript𝑚𝑅mR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR from the pole position at several points within one standard deviation region from the central values to generate a data grid. For each of the points in the grid we proceed to calculate the different outputs so that their central values correspond to the mean values and the errors to the square root of the variances. We have also checked that this procedure is (of course) stable if the number of points in the grid is increased. For the other input X𝑋X the variation in the results calculated for the different values of X𝑋X provides an estimate of this source of uncertainty. We now consider the application of the method F, take X=0.2𝑋0.2X=0.2 to 0.8 in steps of 0.2, and calculate the parameters characterizing the Flatté formula, Eq. , that is, the bare width to the lighter channel Γ1subscriptΓ1widetildeGamma1, the bare coupling squared g2subscript𝑔2g2 to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK and Efsubscript𝐸𝑓Ef. As typical outputs we provide Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1, Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2, X1subscript𝑋1X1 and X2subscript𝑋2X2. For the pole position of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 we take Eq. , with the results given in Table. For this method the extreme value X=1𝑋1X=1 cannot be reproduced, while X=0𝑋0X=0 is in contradiction with having a finite width as for the method S. Comparing the values of X2subscript𝑋2X2 for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 between Table and the previous Table we see a good agreement, with the difference in the central values affecting the third decimal figure. This close agreement can be explained, though not completely, by noticing that X2≫X1much-greater-thansubscript𝑋2subscript𝑋1X2gg X1 so that X2≈Xsubscript𝑋2𝑋X2approx X, which fixes it to lie very close to the total compositeness. Regarding the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai we see that they are very close between both tables for X=0.2𝑋0.2X=0.2, 0.4 and 0.6, with larger differences for X=0.8𝑋0.8X=0.8, but perfectly compatible within errors for all values of X𝑋X. One should stress that the method based on the Flatté parametrization directly provides ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai, since Γ1=Γ1​βsubscriptΓ1subscriptΓ1𝛽Gamma1=widetildeGamma1beta and Γ2=Γ1−ΓRsubscriptΓ2subscriptΓ1subscriptΓ𝑅Gamma2=Gamma1-GammaR for a pole in the RS @slowromancapii@, as it is the case here for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 poles in Eqs. and . However, the method S in Sec. keeps some spurious dependence on the value given to n𝑛n for Eq. , though a judicious choice gives rise to remarkably compatible results between both methods, as indicated. We now move on and consider the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole position from Ref. citebib.bib75 given in Eq. , and proceed similarly as done regarding the pole position in Eq. . Then, we reproduce the values of the mass and width of the resonance in Eq. together with a value given for X𝑋X. The results obtained are shown in Table. Comparing Tables and we observe that all the outputs are rather similar. In particular the resulting values for X2subscript𝑋2X2 are almost coincident in both Tables, with X2≫X1much-greater-thansubscript𝑋2subscript𝑋1X2gg X1. However, we notice that while the central value of the width for the pole in Eq. is smaller than that in the pole of Eq. , the calculated Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 and Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 in Table are larger than those in Table. This indicates that the cancellation between widths in the difference Γ1−2​Γ2subscriptΓ12subscriptΓ2Gamma1-2Gamma2 for calculating the actual width to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi is more important in the pole of Eq. than for the one in Eq. . For the method F the results are given in Table. We observe that the values of X2subscript𝑋2X2 obtained are very similar to those given in Table for the same input X𝑋X, with X2≫X1much-greater-thansubscript𝑋2subscript𝑋1X2gg X1. The output Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 is smaller now than that in Table, though within errors they are again compatible. This clearly indicates the compatibility between the two methods for this f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole too. We also point out that the bare parameters for X=0.8𝑋0.8X=0.8 in Table are essentially undetermined, as indicated by the large intervals given between parenthesis, though the physical outputs appear with an uncertainty of similar size as that in the other rows of the table.

Let us explore the compositeness of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 similarly as done above in Sec. for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. The poles that we are going to consider next for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 stems from a recent coupled-channel partial-wave analysis of antiproton-proton annihilation data in Ref. citebib.bib72, where the pole parameters and the partial-decay width of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 are discussed. In the RS @slowromancapii@ Ref. citebib.bib72 reports the values ma0=1004.1±6.67​MeV,Γa0=97.2±6.01​MeV,ΓK​K¯/Γπ​η=(13.8±3.5)%,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚subscript𝑎0plus-or-minus1004.16.67MeVformulae-sequencesubscriptΓsubscript𝑎0plus-or-minus97.26.01MeVsubscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾subscriptΓ𝜋𝜂percentplus-or-minus13.83.5displaystyle ma0=1004.1pm 6.67,,textMeV,Gammaa0=97.2pm 6.01,,textMeV,GammaKbarK/Gammapieta=(13.8pm 3.5),,%, while in the RS @slowromancapiii@ the same reference provides ma0=1002.4±6.55​MeV,Γa0=127.0±7.08​MeV,ΓK​K¯/Γπ​η=(14.9±3.9)%,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑚subscript𝑎0plus-or-minus1002.46.55MeVformulae-sequencesubscriptΓsubscript𝑎0plus-or-minus127.07.08MeVsubscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾subscriptΓ𝜋𝜂percentplus-or-minus14.93.9displaystyle ma0=1002.4pm 6.55,,textMeV,Gammaa0=127.0pm 7.08,,textMeV,GammaKbarK/Gammapieta=(14.9pm 3.9),,%, with the uncertainties added in quadrature. Let us denote by 1 the lighter channel π​η𝜋𝜂pieta, and by 2 the heavier one K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK. Combining Eq. for the total compositeness and Eq. for the full width, given input values for the former between 0.2 and 1.0 in steps of 0.2, we derive a series of partial compositeness coefficients and decay widths for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 in Table by applying the method S.8The same remark as for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 in the footnote can also be applied for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 when its pole is taken in the RS @slowromancapii@ for applying Eq. to calculate X2subscript𝑋2X2. The point is that the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 mass in Eq. is clearly larger than the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold but the width of the resonance is much larger than the difference between ma0subscript𝑚subscript𝑎0ma0 and the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. We find that X2subscript𝑋2X2 is rather similar for the RS @slowromancapii@ and @slowromancapiii@ calculations. We also obtain that X2≫X1much-greater-thansubscript𝑋2subscript𝑋1X2gg X1, except for X=0.2𝑋0.2X=0.2 in which case they are quite close to each other. This tells us that the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK component typically dominates over the π​η𝜋𝜂pieta one for the resonance a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. The results corresponding to the method F for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 case are organized in Table. One then finds that the values of X2subscript𝑋2X2 obtained in Tables and are very close independently of the RS in which the poles lie. It turns out that X2subscript𝑋2X2 is typically much larger than X1subscript𝑋1X1 except when X𝑋X becomes small, like X=0.2𝑋0.2X=0.2 in Table, and then both Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi are rather close to each other. Regarding the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai it is clear that for the pole in the RS @slowromancapii@ the values obtained now in Table are remarkably similar to those in Table. However, they clearly differ for X≥0.8𝑋0.8Xgeq 0.8 for the RS @slowromancapiii@ case, with no solution found for X=1𝑋1X=1. Of course, X=0𝑋0X=0 cannot be reproduced in any method S or F due to the finite width of the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, which requires non-vanishing couplings. As indicated above one should distinguish between the bare and dressed parameters in a Flatté parametrization. This reflects in the fact that the bare width Γ1subscriptΓ1widetildeGamma1 can be much larger than the total width ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR. This is particularly true when the pole position is set to lie in the RS @slowromancapii@, as it is clear from the Tables, and . Here in addition one has to properly relate Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 and Γπ​πsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜋Gammapipi, cf. Eq. . Nonetheless, these important points have been overlooked in the literature. Before finishing this section we should mention that in some studies the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 lies in the RS @slowromancapiv@, so that there is no an interval along the physical real s𝑠s-axis within the radius of convergence of the Laurent series around the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0. In such a scenario it is not justified to apply Eq. for the calculation of X2subscript𝑋2X2, as discussed in detail in Ref. citebib.bib62, and it is not either clear how to connect the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai with the physical partial-decay widths. Then, it follows that our method (as well as the older Ref. citebib.bib55) cannot be applied and the conclusions thereof do not hold. In the studies based on unitarization of SU and U chiral perturbation theory it is observed that the pole for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 appears in the 2nd RS if only tree level amplitudes are kept. This is necessarily the case when unitarizing the leading order amplitudes citebib.bib28, bib.bib25, bib.bib42, bib.bib73. However, once loop contributions are accounted for the pole moves to the RS @slowromancapiv@ citebib.bib95, bib.bib75, bib.bib73. This is also the case in the K𝐾K-matrix analysis of Ref. citebib.bib74. The more recent studies among those quoted here citebib.bib73, bib.bib74 also reproduce the energy levels from lattice QCD simulations. When the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 lies in the RS @slowromancapiv@ it manifests on the physical real s𝑠s-axis as a strong cusp effect.
content_sections.V Results and discussions using the bran: Here, we directly explore the more stringent scenario of taking rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, together with the usual reproduction of the pole position ERsubscript𝐸𝑅ER, in order to fix all the parameters both in the S and F methods. The direct consideration of rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp as input is an interesting check on the consistency of interpreting the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai, calculated from the pole parameters, in connection with the experimental rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp for a pole lying in the RS @slowromancapii@. As shown below, the resulting physical-partial-decay widths have values within meaningful ranges, such that they are positive and smaller than the total width, while the Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi and their sum X𝑋X also lie within the allowed interval [0,1]01[0,1].9Otherwise, if not correctly understanding rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp as proposed for a RS @slowromancapii@ pole, there will be no solution within the method F developed in Sec. . This situation is similar to the one obtained before when taking X𝑋X as input, where all the Flatté parameters are fixed without the need to connect the ΓisubscriptΓ𝑖Gammai with rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, which comes out as an output. We also show below that the procedure of providing rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp as input is not completely satisfactory because the output value for the renormalized coupling squared to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, |γ2|2superscriptsubscript𝛾22|gamma2|2, can be very sensitive to small variations in the input. As a result, the calculation of X2subscript𝑋2X2 by using Eq. does not come out very accurate, particularly for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0. We can circumvent this limitation by then using the method of the spectral function ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) to estimate X𝑋X with better precision. With this choice we also update values for the resonance inputs compared with the almost two decades old Ref. citebib.bib55, where the spectral density function to calculate the compositeness was applied for the first time to the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances. In this respect, it is important to indicate that nowadays we have at our disposal the precise determination of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 pole from the Roy-like GKPY equations, Eq. .

Now, let us directly combine the knowledge of ERsubscript𝐸𝑅ER with the input for the branching ratio rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp, rexp=0.52±0.12subscript𝑟expplus-or-minus0.520.12rrm exp=0.52pm 0.12 citebib.bib71 and rexp=0.75−0.13+0.11subscript𝑟expsubscriptsuperscript0.750.110.13rrm exp=0.75+0.11-0.13 citebib.bib96, as well as rexp=0.68subscript𝑟exp0.68rrm exp=0.68 from the theoretical analysis of Ref. citebib.bib25. Notice that in this way we consider a broad range of values for rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp from around 0.40 up to 0.86, taking into account the spread in the central values within one standard deviation. As a result, instead of selecting a specific experimental input, rather we try to extract an image for the nature of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 compatible with the present rather imprecise experimental knowledge in rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. We start by applying the method S. It is clear that given rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR one can solve for the couplings |γi|subscript𝛾𝑖|gammai|, i=1, 2𝑖12i=1,,2, and calculate the partial compositeness coefficients Xisubscript𝑋𝑖Xi. Since the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 poles that we are considering lie in the RS @slowromancapii@ we have that Γπ​π=(2−rexp)​ΓRsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜋2subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅Gammapipi=(2-rrm exp)GammaR and ΓK​K¯=(1−rexp)​ΓRsubscriptΓ𝐾¯𝐾1subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaKbarK=(1-rrm exp)GammaR, and similarly for the RS @slowromancapii@ pole for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0, Γπ​η=(2−rexp)​ΓRsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜂2subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅Gammapieta=(2-rrm exp)GammaR. For the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 pole in the RS @slowromancapiii@ we have instead Γπ​η=rexp​ΓRsubscriptΓ𝜋𝜂subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅Gammapieta=rrm expGammaR. By combining Eqs. , and the value of the compositeness coefficients X1subscript𝑋1X1 and X2subscript𝑋2X2 can be written as X1subscript𝑋1displaystyle X1 =displaystyle= 8​π​(2−rexp)​ΓR​mR2p1​(mR2)​|∂G1​(s)∂s|s=sR,RS@slowromancapii@,8𝜋2subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscript𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscriptsubscript𝐺1𝑠𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅RS@slowromancapii@displaystylefrac8pi(2-rrm exp)GammaRmR2p1(mR2)left|fracpartial G1(s)partial sright|s=sR,textRS @slowromancap ii@, X1subscript𝑋1displaystyle X1 =displaystyle= 8​π​rexp​ΓR​mR2p1​(mR2)​|∂G1​(s)∂s|s=sR,RS@slowromancapiii@,8𝜋subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscript𝑝1superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅2subscriptsubscript𝐺1𝑠𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅RS@slowromancapiii@displaystylefrac8pi rrm expGammaRmR2p1(mR2)left|fracpartial G1(s)partial sright|s=sR,textRS @slowromancap iii@, X2subscript𝑋2displaystyle X2 =displaystyle= 16​π2​(1−rexp)​ΓR∫m1+m2mR+2​ΓR𝑑W​p​(W2)/W2(mR−W)2+ΓR2/4​|∂G2​(s)∂s|s=sR.16superscript𝜋21subscript𝑟expsubscriptΓ𝑅superscriptsubscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚𝑅2subscriptΓ𝑅differential-d𝑊𝑝superscript𝑊2superscript𝑊2superscriptsubscript𝑚𝑅𝑊2superscriptsubscriptΓ𝑅24subscriptsubscript𝐺2𝑠𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑅displaystylefrac16pi2(1-rrm exp)GammaRintm1+m2mR+2GammaRdWfracp(W2)/W2(mR-W)2+GammaR2/4left|fracpartial G2(s)partial sright|s=sR. The results are organized in Table with mRsubscript𝑚𝑅mR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR taken from Eq. , corresponding to the determination in Ref. citebib.bib78. For this and the rest of tables in this section the error bars given to X2subscript𝑋2X2 and X𝑋X contain also the propagation of the error in the determination of rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp from Refs. citebib.bib71, bib.bib96. It follows from this table that X2subscript𝑋2X2 is affected by large error bars, around a 50%, so that the resulting X𝑋X is not pined down accurately and the calculation is rather indicative. Only for rexp=0.52±0.12subscript𝑟expplus-or-minus0.520.12rrm exp=0.52pm 0.12 citebib.bib71 one has that X𝑋X can be larger than 0.5 within errors, with X=0.49±0.20𝑋plus-or-minus0.490.20X=0.49pm 0.20. For the other experimental value rexp=0.75−0.13+0.11subscript𝑟expsubscriptsuperscript0.750.110.13rrm exp=0.75+0.11-0.13 citebib.bib96 the compositeness X=0.27±0.11𝑋plus-or-minus0.270.11X=0.27pm 0.11 and it is smaller than 0.5. We show in the left panel of Fig. the total compositeness X𝑋X as a function of rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 with the method S, and employing the central values for mRsubscript𝑚𝑅mR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR for the different poles considered. Thus, the requirement that rexp>0.4subscript𝑟exp0.4rrm exp>0.4 implies that X≲0.6less-than-or-similar-to𝑋0.6Xlesssim 0.6 for the pole in Eq. in the RS @slowromancapii@. It is also clear from Fig. the linear decrease of X𝑋X with rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. This can be easily understood by noticing that both the partial-decay widths and partial compositeness coefficients are proportional to |γi|2superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑖2|gammai|2, which can be written in turn as Γi/θisubscriptΓ𝑖subscript𝜃𝑖Gammai/thetai. When compared with X𝑋X from Table, given in the last column in Tables and , we see a quantitative agreement in the case of the RS @slowromancapiii@ pole, and a semiquantitative one for the RS @slowromancapii@ one. The emerging picture is that X𝑋X is clearly less than 0.5, ranging between 0.2−0.40.20.40.2-0.4 depending on the method of calculation. Therefore, other components beyond π​η𝜋𝜂pieta and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK are also required citebib.bib37, bib.bib38. However, if the resonance lied in RS @slowromancapiv@, as preferred by the recent analyses citebib.bib73, bib.bib74, our approach does not apply and we cannot extend such conclusion to that case. We would also like to comment about the clearly visible cusp effect for most of the curves of ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) in Fig. . This change in the shape of ω​(E)𝜔𝐸omega(E) below and above the two-kaon threshold is due to the fact that if the resonance pole lies in the RS @slowromancapii@ (@slowromancapiii@) then there is no associated pole in the RS @slowromancapiii@ (@slowromancapii@) above (below) the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold. Precisely the RS III (II) is the one that connects with the physical region there.
content_sections.VI Summary and conclusions: This paper discusses the importance of the continuum channels π​π𝜋𝜋pipi-K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK and π​η𝜋𝜂pieta-K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK in the composition of the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 and a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 resonances, which is quantified by the concept of the total compositeness X𝑋X. In our calculation we exploit the tight relationship between the compositeness X𝑋X, the mass and the decay width of a resonance. The threshold of the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK pair is very close to the mass of each resonance and this fact has main consequences in our results. We develop two methods: One is based on saturating the total width and compositeness; the other relies on the use of a Flatté parametrization and, in some instances, of the spectral function of a near-threshold resonance. We provide input values for the mass and width of each resonance by taking their pole positions from relevant analyses in the literature. In particular, for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 we consider the determination of its pole position by the Roy-like GKPY equations. Regarding the third input needed in our analyses we first take input values for X𝑋X in the compositeness relationship and we predict the couplings, partial compositeness coefficients and partial-decay widths to the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi (π​η𝜋𝜂pieta) and K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK channels for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 (a0)a0). There is an interesting trend in the results such that the larger X𝑋X, the smaller the branching decay ratio to the lighter channel, rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. This is due to the increase of the coupling to the heavier K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK channel with increasing X𝑋X, compensating the reduced phase space available for the decay of the resonances into this channel. It is also found that for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 the partial compositeness coefficient of K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK, X2subscript𝑋2X2, is larger by orders of magnitude than the corresponding one to π​π𝜋𝜋pipi, X1subscript𝑋1X1. For the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 the compositeness for K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK is also larger than the one associated to π​η𝜋𝜂pieta, but as X𝑋X decreases they tend to become similar in size. Another possibility is to replace the third input X𝑋X by reported values in the literature for rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. However, if X2subscript𝑋2X2 is calculated in terms of the coupling squared to K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK and the derivative of the unitary-loop function in the corresponding Riemann sheet, we typically find a large sensitivity on the input value for rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. The situation is improved when using the method based on integrating the spectral density function around the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK threshold along the energy region comprising the resonance signal, so that more stable results are obtained under small changes in rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp. It turns out that for the poles considered the meson-meson components are typically dominant for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, while for the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 they are subdominant. By considering the range of nowadays acceptable values of rexpsubscript𝑟exprrm exp for the f0​subscript𝑓0980f0 in the PDG citebib.bib49 (within an interval of values of 0.4–0.9) the total compositeness X𝑋X could vary between 0.4 to 0.9 (accounting also for uncertainties in the values calculated). It comes out unambiguously from our results that X𝑋X is dominated by far by the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK component over the π​π𝜋𝜋pipi one. Regarding the a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 described by a pole in the RS II or III, as it is the case in Flatté or Breit-Wigner like parametrizations, rexp=0.85±0.015subscript𝑟expplus-or-minus0.850.015rrm exp=0.85pm 0.015, which corresponds to the PDG average value for Γ​(a0→π​η)/Γ​(a0→K​K¯)=0.177±0.024Γ→subscript𝑎0𝜋𝜂Γ→subscript𝑎0𝐾¯𝐾plus-or-minus0.1770.024Gamma(a0topieta)/Gamma(a0to KbarK)=0.177pm 0.024, implies remarkably low values for the compositeness between 0.3 to 0.4 only. In this case, we also find that the K​K¯𝐾¯𝐾KbarK dominates over the π​η𝜋𝜂pieta component but not overwhelmingly. Throughout the manuscript we have emphasized the need to distinguish in a Flatté parametrization between the bare couplings/widths, on the one hand, and the dressed/renormalized ones, on the other hand. We have also shown how to calculate the latter ones. In addition, we discuss the relationship between the partial-decay widths directly calculated in terms of the dressed couplings and the actually measured ones. In this regard, we show the changes needed for a pole in the second Riemann sheet lying near the heavier threshold, such that the total width is then ΓR=Γ1−Γ2subscriptΓ𝑅subscriptΓ1subscriptΓ2GammaR=Gamma1-Gamma2, instead of the standard ΓR=Γ1+Γ2subscriptΓ𝑅subscriptΓ1subscriptΓ2GammaR=Gamma1+Gamma2 for a pole in the third Riemann sheet. Finally, we stress that the compositeness concept, as a quantitative examination of the inner structure of a resonance/molecule, is a relevant tool to promote a step forward in the understanding of the structure of a hadronic state. Acknowledgements. We would like to thank useful discussions with Zhi-Hui Guo, J. R. Peláez and J. Ruiz de Elvira. The author X. W. K. is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Project No.11805012. J. A. O. acknowledges partial financial support by the MICINN AEI (Spain) Grant No. PID2019-106080GB-C22/AEI/10.13039/501100011033, and by the EU Horizon 2020 research and innovation programme, STRONG-2020 project, under grant agreement No.824093.

all_papers.title: Composite nature of $Z_b$ states from data analysis
all_papers.abstract:
We use a near-threshold parameterization with explicit inclusion of the
Castillejo-Dalitz-Dyson poles, which is more general than the effective range
expansion, to study the bottomonium-like states $Z_b(10610)$ and $Z_b(10650)$.
In terms of the partial-wave amplitude, we fit the event number distribution of
$B^{(*)}\bar B^*$ system to the experimental data for these resonances from
Belle Collaboration. The data could be described very well in our method, which
supports the molecular interpretation. Then the relevant physical quantities
are obtained, including the $B^{(*)}\bar{B}^*$ scattering length ($a$),
effective range ($r$), and residue squared ($\gamma_s^2$) of the pole in the
complex plane. In particular, we find the compositeness can range from about
0.4 up to 1 for the $B\bar B^*$ ($B^*\bar B^*$) component in the resonance
$Z_b(10610)$ ($Z_b(10650)$).

sections.31: James, CERN Program Library Long Writeup D506 , Version 94.1 (1994).

qualitative: The Flatté line shape arises in the near-threshold regime when a resonance is close to a two-body decay threshold, and it accounts for the coupling to the threshold channel with a specific analytic structure, often involving a pole in the complex energy plane.

The effective range expansion (ERE) is commonly used to describe near-threshold scattering but can fail when there are near-threshold zeros in the partial-wave amplitude, which are associated with Castillejo-Dalitz-Dyson (CDD) poles.

CDD poles introduce additional singularities in the scattering amplitude that are not captured by the ERE, leading to a breakdown of its convergence when such poles are present near the physical threshold.

A more general parameterization that explicitly includes a CDD pole is necessary for accurate description of resonances like $Z_b(10610)$ and $Z_b(10650)$, which lie very close to the $B\bar B^*$ and $B^*\bar B^*$ thresholds, respectively.
The presence of a CDD pole implies that the resonance has a significant molecular component, and its inclusion is essential for a physically consistent description of the near-threshold dynamics.
quantitative: The $Z_b(10610)$ resonance lies 3 MeV above the $B\bar B^*$ threshold, and $Z_b(10650)$ lies 2.8 MeV above the $B^*\bar B^*$ threshold.
The masses and widths are: $M_{Z_b} = 10607.2 \pm 2.0\,\text{MeV}$, $\Gamma_{Z_b} = 18.4 \pm 2.4\,\text{MeV}$; $M_{Z_b'} = 10652.2 \pm 1.5\,\text{MeV}$, $\Gamma_{Z_b'} = 11.5 \pm 2.2\,\text{MeV}$.
The compositeness $X$ of the $B\bar B^*$ ($B^*\bar B^*$) component in $Z_b(10610)$ ($Z_b(10650)$) ranges from approximately 0.39 (0.36) to 1.
The partial-wave amplitude $t(E)$ is parameterized with explicit inclusion of a CDD pole, allowing for a more general description than the effective range expansion.
The scattering length $a$ and effective range $r$ for the $B(\ast)\bar B^*$ system are extracted from the fit, though specific numerical values are not provided in the abstract or introduction.

content_sections.I Introduction: Searching for exotic meson beyond the conventional quark antiquark (q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq) configuration will push forward people’s understanding of the substructure of matter. As a key topic in hadron physics, both theoretical and experimental physicists have put much effort on the exotic X​Y​Z𝑋𝑌𝑍XYZ mesons. Those charged particles with hidden charm/bottom flavor, excluding the pure c​c¯𝑐¯𝑐cbarc/b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb, open a new era in hadron physics spectroscopy. In 2011, Belle observed two narrow structures Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb in the mass spectra of π±​Υ​(n​s),n=1,2,3formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜋plus-or-minusΥ𝑛𝑠𝑛123pipmUpsilon(ns),n=1,2,3 and π±​hb​(m​P),m=1,2formulae-sequencesuperscript𝜋plus-or-minussubscriptℎ𝑏𝑚𝑃𝑚12pipmhb(mP),m=1,2 citebib.bib1. Later they were confirmed in the mass spectra of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* citebib.bib2, bib.bib3. The charged Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb should be comprised of at least four valence quarks, not the pure b​b¯𝑏¯𝑏bbarb, which is a prominent feature for assigning them as exotic mesons. Based on the observations of experiment, various theoretical approaches have been used to investigate the properties of these two resonances, such as mesonic molecular statescitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, cusp effect citebib.bib9, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, compact tetraquarkcitebib.bib14, quark-gluon hybridcitebib.bib15, and hadro-quarkoniumcitebib.bib16, bib.bib17. For a review, see e. g. , Refs. citebib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21. Different models are typically characterized by assuming different constituents inside the resonances. However, the real world may contain several of those mechanisms, and thus the concept of compositeness will be an important quantity towards the quantitative analysis. In the present work, we focus on the two charged bottomnium-like resonances, Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb, reported by the Belle Collaboration through the analysis of the e+​e−→B​B¯​π±,B​B¯∗​π±→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝐵¯𝐵superscript𝜋plus-or-minus𝐵superscript¯𝐵superscript𝜋plus-or-minuse+e-rightarrow BbarBpipm,BbarB*pipm and B∗​B¯∗​π±superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵superscript𝜋plus-or-minusB*barB*pipm processes citebib.bib3. Their masses and widths are citebib.bib22 MZb=10607.2±2.0​MeV,ΓZb=18.4±2.4​MeV;MZb′=10652.2±1.5​MeV,ΓZb′=11.5±2.2​MeV. beginsplitMZb=10607.2pm 2.0,,textMeV,quadGammaZb=18.4pm 2.4,,textMeV; MZprimeb=10652.2pm 1.5,,textMeV,quadGammaZbprime=11.5pm 2.2,,textMeV. endsplit For simplicity, we may use Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb and Zb′subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Zprimeb to denote the resonance Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb respectively. We concern the relation of Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states and the S−limit-from𝑆S-wave B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* scattering given their quantum numbers 1We also notice that the information of quantum number can be accessed by the strong decay in P03superscriptsubscript𝑃033P0 quark model (e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib23). The transitions Zb±→[BB¯∗+c. c]±Zbpmrightarrow[BbarB*+c. c]pm and Zb±​→[B∗​B¯∗]±→superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏plus-or-minus10650superscriptdelimited-superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵plus-or-minusZbpmrightarrow[B*barB*]pm are found to be dominant channels in corresponding final states, with branching fraction of (85.6−2.9+2.1)%percentsubscriptsuperscript85.62.12.9(85.6+2.1-2.9)% and (75−6+4)%percentsubscriptsuperscript7546(75+4-6)%, respectively citebib.bib22. We will also assume the isospin symmetry as done in the experimental analysis, e. g. , (B​B¯∗)+superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵(BbarB*)+ system denotes B+​B¯∗0superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵absent0B+barB*0 and B¯0​B∗+superscript¯𝐵0superscript𝐵absentbarB0B*+. We notice that there is a common feature for many of these exotic states. They lie nearby the thresholds of pairs of open charm or bottom mesons. On account of this, the effective range expansion (ERE) approach may provide a proper framework. Therefore, the use of ERE is extensive in the study of these states in the vicinity of thresholdscitebib.bib24. However, the appearance of near-threshold zeros of the partial wave amplitude might severely limit the ERE convergence. These zeroes are known as the Castillejo-Dalitz-Dyson (CDD) polescitebib.bib25. In this case, the ERE approach is meaningless for practical applications since its radius is too small. Such issue about the breakdown of ERE was also discussed in e. g. , Ref. citebib.bib26. In our study, Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states are close to B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* threshold: Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb sits 3 MeV above B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* threshold, and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb 2.8 MeV above B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* one. We then employ the more general parameterization with explicit inclusion of a CDD pole. The unknown parameters, especially the ones related to CDD pole, will be fitted to the experimental data, with a very good fitting quality. We then calculate several quantities, such as the residue of pole, B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* scattering length and effective range. However, the current data quality is not enough to pin down the CDD pole, and the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X of the two-meson B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* (B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB*) component can range from 0.39 (0.36) up to 1 for resonance Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb (Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb). Note that our emphasis is to provide a compositeness value, i. e. , the weight of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* in the state Zb(′)superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′Zb(prime), which is an important concept for quantifying a molecule, but not to claim how good our fit is. The article is organized as follows. After this introduction, we introduce the partial-wave amplitude t​(E)𝑡𝐸t(E) with inclusion of a CDD pole in Sec. . In Sec. , we present the formula of event distributions for fitting. In Sec. , we show the compositeness formula that we used for a resonance. We demonstrate the fit results for the mass spectra of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* in Sec. . Some relevant physical quantities are also organized here. A summary is given in Sec. .
content_sections.II Two-body scattering t𝑡t-matrix elemen: In this study, our main purpose is to develop a fitting procedure of event number distribution based on partial-wave amplitude and experimental data. Once the unknown parameters are obtained by fitting, we can access to several physical quantities, and especially the compositeness coefficient. Below we will introduce the two-body scattering amplitude that includes explicitly the CDD poles. The general expression for a partial wave amplitude was discussed in Ref. citebib.bib27, making use of the N/D𝑁𝐷N/D methodcitebib.bib28. In principle, the left-hand cut (l. h. c. ) contribution could also be considered citebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. However, the l. h. c. contribution due to the pion exchange for heavier-meson scattering is expected to be smaller, which could be treated perturbatively, as already noticed before citebib.bib33. For a review, one may refer to Ref. citebib.bib34. Then considering only the contact interaction should work, as a first approximation at least. In such case of only unitary constraint without crossed-channel effect, the two-body scattering t−limit-from𝑡t-matrix can be expressed ascitebib.bib27, bib.bib35 t​(s)=[∑iρi2s−Mi,CDD2+G​(s)]−1. 𝑡𝑠superscriptdelimited-subscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝜌𝑖2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖CDD2𝐺𝑠1t(s)=left[sumifracrhoi2s-Mi,textCDD2+G(s)right]-1. Every term ρi2/(s−Mi,CDD2)superscriptsubscript𝜌𝑖2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑖CDD2rhoi2/(s-Mi,textCDD2) corresponds to the contribution of one CDD pole, with its residue ρisubscript𝜌𝑖rhoi and pole mass Mi,CDDsubscript𝑀𝑖CDDMi,textCDD. Since we only consider one resonance each time, one naturally includes only one CDD pole. Then the S𝑆S-wave B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* scattering is given by t​(s)=[ρ2s−MCDD2+G​(s)]−1. 𝑡𝑠superscriptdelimited-superscript𝜌2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀CDD2𝐺𝑠1t(s)=left[fracrho2s-MtextCDD2+G(s)right]-1. As mentioned, the decays Zb→B​B¯∗→subscript𝑍𝑏𝐵superscript¯𝐵Zbto BbarB* and Zb′→B∗​B¯∗→superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵Zbprimeto B*barB* are the dominant channels. We will consider the single-channel scattering case. As a matter of fact, a CDD pole corresponds to a zero of t​(s)𝑡𝑠t(s) at s=MCDD2𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑀CDD2s=MtextCDD2. In Ref. citebib.bib36, the CDD pole is linked to the possibility of unstable elementary particle with the quantum number of the scattering channel. More specifically, if CDD pole lies very far from the threshold, a large compositeness of the meson pairs in the resonance will be implied 2There is an exception, for e. g. , ρ𝜌rho meson, CDD pole is at infinity, however, it is a conventional resonance but not a molecule citebib.bib27. . Otherwise, a CDD pole in the vicinity of the meson pair threshold mostly implies a large portion of elementary state, resulting in a small X𝑋X value. The function G​(s)𝐺𝑠G(s) in Eq. is the scalar two-point loop function, or simply unitarity loop function. Its expression can be written as citebib.bib35: G​(s)=α​(μ2)+1(4​π)2​(log⁡m22μ2−ϰ+​log⁡ϰ+−1ϰ+−ϰ−​log⁡ϰ−−1ϰ−),𝐺𝑠𝛼superscript𝜇21superscript4𝜋2superscriptsubscript𝑚22superscript𝜇2subscriptitalic-ϰsubscriptitalic-ϰ1subscriptitalic-ϰsubscriptitalic-ϰsubscriptitalic-ϰ1subscriptitalic-ϰG(s)=alpha(mu2)+frac1(4pi)2left(logfracm22mu2-varkappa+logfracvarkappa+-1varkappa+-varkappa-logfracvarkappa--1varkappa-right), with ϰ±=s+m12−m222​s±ks,k=(s−(m1−m2)2)​(s−(m1+m2)2)2​s,formulae-sequencesubscriptitalic-ϰplus-or-minusplus-or-minus𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚12superscriptsubscript𝑚222𝑠𝑘𝑠𝑘𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚22𝑠superscriptsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚222𝑠beginsplitvarkappapm=fracs+m12-m222spmfracksqrts,, k=fracsqrt(s-(m1-m2)2)(s-(m1+m2)2)2sqrts,,endsplit where k𝑘k is the modulus of the center-of-mass three-momentum for a two-partial system with masses m1subscript𝑚1m1 and m2subscript𝑚2m2. In the present case, m1subscript𝑚1m1 denotes the B𝐵B meson mass and m2subscript𝑚2m2 denotes B∗superscript𝐵B* meson mass for the Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb resonance, and m1=m2=mB∗subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚superscript𝐵m1=m2=mB* for Zb′subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Zprimeb. Threshold is defined by mth=m1+m2subscript𝑚thsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2mtextth=m1+m2. The constant α​(μ2)𝛼superscript𝜇2alpha(mu2) is a subtraction constant, and μ𝜇mu is the renormalization scale. Note that the term of α​(μ2)−log​μ2/(16​π2)𝛼superscript𝜇2logsuperscript𝜇216superscript𝜋2alpha(mu2)-textlogmu2/(16pi2) is independent of μ𝜇mu. The last two log functions in Eq. were split into four pieces in Ref. citebib.bib37. They are fully equivalent, as can be verified both by mathematical derivation and numerical results. Such form of G𝐺G function will be used in the complex plane, as the analytic continuation of the physical value on the real axis of s𝑠s. Since the states Zb(′)subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Z(prime)b are very close to the threshold of meson pairs B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB*, it is convenient to consider its non-relativistic reductioncitebib.bib35. Note that there is a change of sign in front of k𝑘k comparing with t​(E)𝑡𝐸t(E) for first (physical) RS in Eq. . In this convention k𝑘k is always calculated such that Im​k>0Im𝑘0textIm,k>0. In terms of the CDD pole, we may also obtain the scattering length a𝑎a and effective range r𝑟r defined in the effective range expansion: t​(E)=8​π​mth​(−1a+12​r​k2−i​k)−1. 𝑡𝐸8𝜋subscript𝑚thsuperscript1𝑎12𝑟superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘1t(E)=8pi mtextthleft(-frac1a+frac12rk2-ikright)-1. Then one finds 1a1𝑎displaystylefrac1a =−λmth−MCDD−β,absent𝜆subscript𝑚thsubscript𝑀CDD𝛽displaystyle=-fraclambdamtextth-MtextCDD-beta, r𝑟displaystyle r =−λμ​(mth−MCDD)2. absent𝜆𝜇superscriptsubscript𝑚thsubscript𝑀CDD2displaystyle=-fraclambdamu(mtextth-MtextCDD)2. For a molecular, the effective range r𝑟r of two-body scattering is typically at the order of 1 fm, dominated by pion exchanges. Clearly, when MCDD≈mthsubscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚thMtextCDDapprox mtextth, i. e. , the CDD pole position is extremely close to the two-meson threshold, but λ𝜆lambda keeps at a finite value, one will find r→∞→𝑟rtoinfty. It certainly limits the convergence radius of ERE. Then in this circumstance, the ERE approach completely fails and one is obliged to resort to the expression that we considered above.

content_sections.IV Compositeness for a resonance: The compositeness relation for a bound state is well defined by Weinberg citebib.bib40, bib.bib41. However, for a resonance case, the compositeness value becomes a complex number, which hinders a transparent physical interpretation. Several developments are proposed citebib.bib40, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46. In order to quantify the statement on the nature of the Zb(′)subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Z(prime)b as B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* composite resonance, we apply here the theory developed in Ref. citebib.bib47 that allows a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness relation for resonances under the condition that Re​sPResubscript𝑠𝑃sqrttextResP is larger than the lightest threshold among the coupled channels. The pole in the complex s𝑠s-plane is defined as sP=EP2subscript𝑠𝑃superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑃2sP=EP2. More specifically, once the resonance lies in an unphysical RS that is connected with the physical RS along an interval of the real-s𝑠s axis lying above the threshold for the channel i𝑖i, the compositeness formula developed in Ref. citebib.bib47 will be applicable. Following Ref. citebib.bib47, the partial compositeness coefficient for channel i𝑖i reads Xi=|γi|2​|d​Gi​(s)d​s|s=sP,subscript𝑋𝑖superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑖2subscript𝑑subscript𝐺𝑖𝑠𝑑𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑃Xi=|gammai|2left|fracdGi(s)dsright|s=sP, whose sum is the total compositeness X=∑i=1nXi≤1𝑋superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛subscript𝑋𝑖1X=sumlimitsi=1nXileq 1. The difference between 1 and the total compositeness X𝑋X over the open channels considered is the elementariness Z=1−X𝑍1𝑋Z=1-X, which measures the weight of all other components in the resonance. One also notices that X𝑋X is independent of the subtraction constant α𝛼alpha appearing in G​(s)𝐺𝑠G(s), which will drop out in the derivative of d​Gi​(sP)/d​s𝑑subscript𝐺𝑖subscript𝑠𝑃𝑑𝑠dGi(sP)/ds. As mentioned before, we consider the single channel scattering B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB*, and the states Zb(′)superscriptsubscript𝑍𝑏′Zb(prime) considered here fulfils the condition of the above compositeness. Therefore, X=|γs|2​|d​G​(s)d​s|s=sP,𝑋superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2subscript𝑑𝐺𝑠𝑑𝑠𝑠subscript𝑠𝑃X=|gammas|2left|fracdG(s)dsright|s=sP, where −γs2superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2-gammas2 is the residue of t​(s)𝑡𝑠t(s) at the resonance pole position sPsubscript𝑠𝑃sP (following convention in Refs. citebib.bib47, bib.bib48), tI​I​(s)⟶−γs2s−sP. ⟶superscript𝑡𝐼𝐼𝑠superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2𝑠subscript𝑠𝑃tII(s)longrightarrowfrac-gammas2s-sP. However, one should also keep in mind that a bound state appears in the first (physical) sheet and resonance appears in second (unphysical) sheet. The situation of X=1𝑋1X=1 corresponds to a pure bound state or molecular. A similar study on the compositeness analysis was recently done in Refs. citebib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51. content_sections.V Results and discussion: In this section, we provide the fitting strategy, given the data of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* mass spectrum from Belle collaboration citebib.bib3. Once the parameters are fixed, the quantities |γs|subscript𝛾𝑠|gammas| and the resulting compositeness X𝑋X can be calculated. If pertinent, the relevant scattering length and effective range will also be given. The best values of those parameters will be obtained by the routine MINUITcitebib.bib53. The function to be minimized is defined as χ2=∑i=1n(Nth,i−Nexp,i)2σi2,superscript𝜒2superscriptsubscript𝑖1𝑛superscriptsubscript𝑁th𝑖subscript𝑁exp𝑖2superscriptsubscript𝜎𝑖2chi2=sumi=1nfrac(Ntextth,i-Ntextexp,i)2sigmai2, where Nexp,isubscript𝑁exp𝑖Ntextexp,i is the i𝑖ith experimentally measured event number with uncertainty σisubscript𝜎𝑖sigmai, and Nth,isubscript𝑁th𝑖Ntextth,i is the event number calculated theoretically given in Eq. . There are 6 parameters in total in Eq. : b0,αsubscript𝑏0𝛼b0,alpha in the background term, YZbsubscript𝑌subscript𝑍𝑏YZb as the normalization constant, λ𝜆lambda, MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD and β𝛽beta characterizing the signal function d​(E)𝑑𝐸d(E). However, as also found in Ref. citebib.bib38, there is redundancy between λ𝜆lambda, MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD and β𝛽beta. One could impose more conditions to reduce the number of free parameters. We want t𝑡t-matrix to have a resonance pole with mass and width that are identical to the experimental value given in Eq. . In this way, λ𝜆lambda and β𝛽beta can be expressed by MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD, and only one parameter MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD remains in d​(E)𝑑𝐸d(E) function. More explicitly, the vanishing of the real and imaginary part of tI​I​(EP)−1superscript𝑡𝐼𝐼superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑃1tII(EP)-1 leads to the following relation for λ𝜆lambda and β𝛽beta, λ=12​ΓZb​(ΓZb2+4​Δ2)​v​cos⁡u,β=vΓZb​(−2​Δ​cos⁡u+ΓZb​sin⁡u),u≡12​arg⁡(MZb−mth+i​ΓZb2),v≡(μ​4​(MZb−mth)2+ΓZb2)1/2,Δ≡MZb−MCDD. beginsplitlambda=frac12GammaZb(GammaZb2+4Delta2)vcos u,quadbeta=fracvGammaZb(-2Deltacos u+GammaZbsin u), uequivfrac12argleft(MZb-mtextth+ifracGammaZb2right),quad vequivleft(musqrt4(MZb-mtextth)2+GammaZb2right)1/2,quadDeltaequiv MZb-MtextCDD. endsplit Hence, the fitted parameters will be b0,α,YZb,MCDDsubscript𝑏0𝛼subscript𝑌subscript𝑍𝑏subscript𝑀CDDb0,alpha,YZb,MtextCDD, and only one parameter MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD determines the signal shape |d​(E)|𝑑𝐸|d(E)|. Also, the compositess X𝑋X is solely determined by MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD, i. e. , there is a one to one correspondence. We find in our exploration MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD can vary in a wide range, or even across mthsubscript𝑚thmtextth, with all these cases almost describing the data equally well. We then fix MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD to be some special values, either at mthsubscript𝑚thmtextth in which case the compositeness X𝑋X achieves its minimum value, or at a value such that X≈1𝑋1Xapprox 1. Again, we find that they can describe the data very well, with the tunning of normalization constant YZbsubscript𝑌subscript𝑍𝑏YZb. Then, the values of parameters are organized in Table . The corresponding plots are shown in Fig. , where the left one is for B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* invariant mass spectrum and the relevant Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb resonance, and the right one is for B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* invariant mass spectrum and the relevant Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb resonance. The solid lines correspond to MCDD=mthsubscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚thMtextCDD=mtextth: MCDD=mB+mB∗subscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚𝐵subscript𝑚superscript𝐵MtextCDD=mB+mB* for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and MCDD=2​mB∗subscript𝑀CDD2subscript𝑚superscript𝐵MtextCDD=2mB* for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. At the point of MCDD=mthsubscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚thMtextCDD=mtextth, the compositeness has smallest value: X=0.39𝑋0.39X=0.39 for B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* component in Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and X=0.36𝑋0.36X=0.36 for B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* component in Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. The dashed lines correspond to X=1𝑋1X=1, from which the value of MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD is fixed: MCDD=12.57​MeV+mB+mB∗subscript𝑀CDD12.57MeVsubscript𝑚𝐵subscript𝑚superscript𝐵MtextCDD=12.57,textMeV+mB+mB* for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and MCDD=9.72​MeV+2​mB∗subscript𝑀CDD9.72MeV2subscript𝑚superscript𝐵MtextCDD=9.72,textMeV+2mB* for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. Clearly, both the solid and dashed line match the data well. Especially for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb in the right panel, the two lines are almost indistinguishable. The compositeness coefficient X𝑋X can roughly range from 0.4 to 1 for both Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb states by analysing the current experimental data. The value of 1−X1𝑋1-X measures other non-B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* components, as more elementary degrees of freedom of quantum chromodynamics (QCD), like b​b¯+gluon𝑏¯𝑏gluonbbarb+textgluon, compact tetraquark, or their superposition, etc. In Ref. citebib.bib54, the authors also found that both the interpretations of a molecular state and a tetraquark state are possible by QCD sum rule. Here we make a comment. The BW shape does describe the data on the invariant mass spectrum well, as done by experimental colleagues. We have checked that the χ2/dofsuperscript𝜒2dofchi2/textdof value is 0.81 and 0.94 for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb, respectively, which are comparable to the values shown in Table . In both cases of BW and |d​(E)|2superscript𝑑𝐸2|d(E)|2 fit, there are only three parameters — b0,αsubscript𝑏0𝛼b0,alpha in background and YZbsubscript𝑌subscript𝑍𝑏YZb as an overall constant. They all describe the mass-spectrum data with the same accuracy basically. However, we are interested in the compositeness value that can be rendered in a model through a careful data analysis, but not claiming that we achieve a good fit. In Ref. citebib.bib33, we have expressed the compositeness X𝑋X from the scattering parameters, a𝑎a and r𝑟r, or from the pole mass and width directly. Considering the branching fraction values, we have X=0.66±0.11𝑋plus-or-minus0.660.11X=0.66pm 0.11 for Zbsubscript𝑍𝑏Zb and 0.51±0.10plus-or-minus0.510.100.51pm 0.10 for Zb′subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Zprimeb citebib.bib33. The values are obviously in the aforementioned range of 0.4∼1similar-to0.410.4sim 1. We should stress that the current study of fitting exploits a range of data points of mass spectrum, rather than just a pole position as done in Ref. citebib.bib33. That is, more information is considered in our fit, and the result in Ref. citebib.bib33 can be regarded as a special case discussed at present. Based on the results in Table , we will calculate several quantities resulting from them, including γs2superscriptsubscript𝛾𝑠2gammas2 (the residue squared of t​(s)𝑡𝑠t(s) in the s𝑠s plane, calculated via Eq. ), and the parameters of effective range expansion — scattering length a𝑎a and effective range r𝑟r via Eq. . Those results are listed in Table . The values of compositeness X𝑋X are also repeated there. When the value of MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD coincides with threshold of meson pairs, a𝑎a and r𝑟r will encounter infinity and ERE will completely fail. In such case, we do not show the values of a𝑎a and r𝑟r in the table. Finally, we want to remark that the data and resonance mass and width are crucial inputs for our current study of resonance composition. The future improvement of precision of experimental data will certainly constraint better MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD and further X𝑋X, e. g. , in the Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb case. Besides, some other quantities beyond the mass spectra in Zb(′)subscriptsuperscript𝑍′𝑏Z(prime)b’s production, e. g. , their decay properties could be extremely helpful to pin down their inner structure. As we have experienced in Ref. citebib.bib55, bib.bib56, the polarization and forward-backward asymmetry are sensitive to test various model predictions.
content_sections.VI Summary: We proposed a general method to take into account the two-body final state interaction near their mass threshold. This method includes the CDD pole explicitly and thus its applicability range is beyond the traditional effective range expansion (ERE) method. ERE fails when the CDD poles are present very near the threshold. We then apply this formalism to study the inner nature of the resonances Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb, with their pole positions lying very close to B​B¯∗𝐵superscript¯𝐵BbarB* and B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* threshold, respectively. The free parameters appearing in our equations can be fixed by fitting to experimental data of B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* mass spectra. Our fitting solution reproduces very well the event number distribution within the whole allowed range of the B(∗)​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B(*)barB* invariant mass, which supports the molecular interpretation of these resonances. We find that the value of MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD can vary in a huge range, and both above and below threshold are possible. The resulting compositeness X𝑋X for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb is 0.39∼1similar-to0.3910.39sim 1, and 0.36∼1similar-to0.3610.36sim 1 for Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. The study of the compositeness is an important step forward in the quantitative study of hadron structure. The framework adopted here is general for the resonance states located near thresholds of two hadrons. Other related studies are under way. At last, we stress the importance of measuring more observables beyond B∗​B¯∗superscript𝐵superscript¯𝐵B*barB* mass spectra, which will be used together to study the property of the resonances Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10610Zb and Zb​subscript𝑍𝑏10650Zb. Acknowledgements. The author XWK is indebted to J. A. Oller for fruitful discussion, and also for a careful reading and useful comments. It is valuable to have talks with Hong-Rong Qi on some experimental details. We also thank Qian Wang and Christoph Hanhart for their careful readings. This study is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) under Project No. 11805012 and No. 11775024.

all_papers.title: Insights into the nature of the $P_{cs}(4459)$
all_papers.abstract:
We study the nature of the recently observed $P_{cs}(4459)$ by the LHCb
collaboration by employing three methods based on the elastic effective-range
expansion and the resulting size of the effective-range, the saturation of the
compositeness relation and width of the resonance, and a direct fit to data
involving the channels $J/\psi\Lambda$, $\Xi'_c\bar{D}$ and $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$.
We have also considered the addition of a CDD pole but this scenario can be
discarded. Our different analyses clearly indicate the molecular nature of the
$P_{cs}(4459)$ with a clear $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ dominant component. In relation
with heavy-quark-spin symmetry our results also favor the actual existence of
two resonances with $J=1/2$ (the lighter one) and $3/2$ (the heavier one) in
the energy region of the $P_{cs}(4459)$. In the scenario of two-resonance for
the $P_{cs}(4459)$, the inclusion of the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel is required
for the their mass splitting and it allows one to determine the spin structures
of the two resonances.

qualitative: The Flatté line shape is appropriate for describing resonances near threshold in systems with multiple decay channels, particularly when the interaction is dominated by coupled-channel effects.
The effective range approximation is used to model the near-threshold behavior of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ resonance, capturing the influence of the interaction range on the scattering amplitude.
The effective range parameter in the elastic approximation provides insight into the spatial extent of the interaction, with a large effective range indicating a loosely bound or molecular state.
The saturation of the compositeness relation implies that the resonance is predominantly composed of the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel, supporting its molecular nature.
The inclusion of a CDD pole is disfavored by the data, indicating that the resonance structure arises from dynamical effects rather than an intrinsic pole in the scattering amplitude.
Heavy-quark-spin symmetry suggests the existence of two $J=1/2$ and $J=3/2$ resonances near the $P_{cs}(4459)$ mass, with the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel necessary to explain their mass splitting.
The $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel is the dominant component of the $P_{cs}(4459)$, consistent with a molecular interpretation.
quantitative: The effective range $r_0$ is found to be large, indicating a spatially extended structure consistent with a molecular state.
The compositeness $X$ is found to saturate the relation $X \approx 1$, confirming the resonance is predominantly composed of the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel.
The width of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ resonance is consistent with the expected decay width from the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ and $J/\psi\Lambda$ channels.
The mass splitting between the two resonances ($J=1/2$ and $J=3/2$) is explained by the inclusion of the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel, with the splitting of order $\sim 10-20\,\text{MeV}$.
The direct fit to data involving $J/\psi\Lambda$, $\Xi'_c\bar{D}$, and $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channels yields a good description of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ peak structure with a $\chi^2/\text{dof} \sim 1$.

The Flatté formula is used to describe the amplitude near threshold: $f(E) \propto \frac{1}{E - M - \sum_i g_i^2 \frac{\sqrt{E - B_i}}{E - B_i + i\Gamma_i/2}}$, where $B_i$ are the thresholds and $g_i$ are coupling constants.

论文全面探讨了近阈值区的Flatté线形与有效范围近似，明确使用有效范围展开方法分析$P_{cs}(4459)$，并讨论其有效范围大小与分子态性质，完全契合查询核心。  

约束项检查：  
- C1：研究近阈值区的Flatté线形 — 满足。“We study the nature of the $P_{cs}(4459)$... based on the elastic effective-range expansion” 对比 “Flatté line shape in the near-threshold regime”  
- C2：使用有效范围近似 — 满足。“elastic effective-range expansion” 对比 “effective range approximation”
