authors: Meng-Lin Du, Zhi-Hui Guo and J. A. Oller

content_sections.1 Introduction: The study of exotic hadrons beyond the conventional quark-antiquark (q​q¯𝑞¯𝑞qbarq) configurations for mesons and the three-quark (3​q3𝑞3q) ones for baryons has been an interesting topic in strong interactions since long time ago, e. g. one could mention the discussions on the lightest scalar resonances f0​subscript𝑓0500f0, f0​subscript𝑓0980f0, a0​subscript𝑎0980a0 and κ​𝜅900kappa citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, the case of the Λ​Λ1405Lambda resonances citebib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, the Roper citebib.bib9, etc. The interest on this topic has received a great boost since the discoveries in 2003 of the Ds​0∗​superscriptsubscript𝐷𝑠02317Ds0* citebib.bib10 and the χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1 citebib.bib11, aka X​𝑋3872X, whose properties cannot be accommodated within the conventional quark model expectations. As a result a new era has developed in hadron spectroscopy based on the detection of numerous multiquark states with heavy quarks, particularly the charm quark, and more recently the bottom quark as well. An important outcome in this fruitful field of research was the discovery by the LHCb in 2015 of the charmonium pentaquarks Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4450Pc and Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc decaying into J/ψ​p𝐽𝜓𝑝J/psi p citebib.bib12. With higher statistics in 2019 citebib.bib13 the same collaboration in the same decay process determined the existence of the pentaquarks Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4457Pc, Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4440Pc (instead of just one resonance Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4450Pc) and the lighter and narrow Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4312Pc. In Refs. citebib.bib14, bib.bib15 a through analysis of the experimental data employing coupled-channel dynamics, heavy-quark-spin symmetry (HQSS) and one-pion exchange provided a clear hint for the existence of a narrow Pc​subscript𝑃𝑐4380Pc. Very recently the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 has reported the first evidence of a charmonium pentaquark resonance with strangeness, the so called Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs appearing as a peak in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution from an amplitude analysis of the Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xib-to J/psiLambda K- decay. Ref. citebib.bib16 also concludes that this structure is consistent with two resonances. Indeed strange pentaquark resonances were already predicted theoretically in several references citebib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, being also suggested to look for them in the decay process Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xib-to J/psiLambda K- by the Refs. citebib.bib25, bib.bib19. The discovery of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs by the LHCb collaboration has prompted numerous studies of this resonance citebib.bib26, bib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31. These studies typically favor a molecular-type picture with a dominant component of the channel Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, whose threshold lies only about 19MeV above the nominal mass of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. However, there is no such a general consensus regarding the spin of the resonance between the two values J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2323/2 and, indeed, some studies point out towards the existence of two resonances around 4.45GeV in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distributions. In this work we intent to scrutinize the nature of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs without ascribing to a specific dynamical model, by making use of general arguments based on unitarity and analyticity in S𝑆S-matrix theory. We exploit three general methods, recently applied in a similar way by two of us in the study of the X​𝑋6900X citebib.bib32, all of which come to the conclusion of the molecular nature of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs with a clearly dominant Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* component. The first method is based on the fact that a CDD pole citebib.bib33 in the near-threshold region gives rise to a strong impact citebib.bib34 in the values of the scattering length a𝑎a and effective range r𝑟r. Particularly relevant is to focus on r𝑟r because a priori its absolute value is given by the typical range of strong interactions around 1fm citebib.bib35, while the presence of a nearby CDD pole would typically imply much larger values citebib.bib34. This signature could then be taken as favoring the presence in the state of interest of a large bare component, or of large components from other channels, to which the CDD pole would be associated citebib.bib36. However, we find here that r𝑟r has a natural value around −11-1fm by applying the elastic effective-range-expansion (ERE) study to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, suggesting the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* molecular nature of this resonance. We also observe that when this method is applied to the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel (whose threshold is around 13MeV below the resonance mass) the resulting r𝑟r has instead a much larger modulus indicating that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is not dynamically generated by this channel. The second method is a coupled-channel generalization that relies on the saturation of the width ΓΓGamma and compositeness X𝑋X of the resonance in terms of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*(Ξc′D¯)XicprimebarD) channels. The latter concept refers to the weight of the baryon-meson components in the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. For its calculation we follow Ref. citebib.bib37 that provides a probabilistic interpretation for the compositeness of a resonance. The optimal way to calculate the compositeness is still under debate citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib41, bib.bib41. By following this method we have at our disposal two equations, though the outcome depends on the value assumed for the total compositeness X𝑋X, while ΓΓGamma is taken from the LHCb collaboration Ref. citebib.bib16. Nonetheless, given a value of X𝑋X the method provides us with the partial decay widths and the partial compositeness coefficients for each channel. The third method allows one to also include coupled channels and avoid having to take for granted a value for X𝑋X. We proceed with a direct fit to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution around the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance energy region explicitly taking into account the three coupled channels J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, together with constraints from HQSS. The lightest channel accounts in an effective way of those channels whose thresholds are relatively far from the resonance mass, in particular this is the case for the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda which is degenerate to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda in the heavy-quark limit. Our analysis also favors the presence of two resonances (with J=12𝐽12J=frac12 and J=32𝐽32J=frac32 respectively) in the peak of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distribution associated to the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. Since the S𝑆S-wave Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD only couples to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* in J=12𝐽12J=frac12, it could affect the dynamics of this channel and leave significant impact on the line shape. As a result, it leads to the mass split of the two resonances and allows one to determine their spins. We stress that the HQSS breaking of the potential, which is of order 𝒪​(ΛQCDmc)𝒪subscriptΛQCDsubscript𝑚𝑐O(fracLambdatextQCDmc), cannot account for the mass splitting of the two resonances, cf. Sec. . In other words, the inclusion of the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel provides an extra mechanism which is responsible for the mass splitting of the two resonances while keeping the HQSS constraints on the interacting potential. In addition, we have also allowed the presence of a CDD pole in the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel but no acceptable fits result. We find that the methods 1 and 3 agree in the basic fact that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is mainly a Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* molecular resonance, while the methods 2 and 3 provide also very compatible values for the couplings of the resonance to the different channels when the same value for X𝑋X is used in the second method. The contents of the manuscript are structured as follows. After this introduction we describe and apply the methods 1, 2 and 3 to the study the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs in the Secs. , and , respectively. In the latter section we also compare between several types of fits with different number of channels and partial waves involved, as well as with different degree of imposition of the HQSS constraints. The conclusions are gathered in Sec. .
content_sections.2 Effective range expansion for the elas: First we rely on the ERE method to perform an exploratory study of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs near the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD thresholds. The elastic S𝑆S-wave ERE formula reads T​(E)=1−1a+12​r​k2−i​k,𝑇𝐸11𝑎12𝑟superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘displaystyle T(E)=frac1-frac1a+frac12r,k2-i,k,, where a𝑎a and r𝑟r are the scattering length and the effective range in order, and the non-relativistic relation between the three-momentum k𝑘k and the energy E𝐸E in the center of mass (CM) frame is k=2​μm​(E−mth),𝑘2subscript𝜇𝑚𝐸subscript𝑚thdisplaystyle k=sqrt2mum(E-mrm th),, being μm=m1​m2/(m1+m2)subscript𝜇𝑚subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2subscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2mum=m1m2/(m1+m2) the reduced mass and mth=m1+m2subscript𝑚thsubscript𝑚1subscript𝑚2mrm th=m1+m2 the threshold of the two states in the scattering. Above the threshold, the unitarity relation Im​T​(E)−1=−k,(E>mth),Im𝑇superscript𝐸1𝑘𝐸subscript𝑚thrm Im,T(E)-1=-k,,quad(E>mrm th),, is automatically satisfied by the amplitude in Eq. . There are two caveats when applying the ERE formalism. First the left hand cuts originated from the particle exchanges in the crossed channels are neglected. This assumption is consistent with the pionless effective filed theory description, which turns out to be able to explain the various pentaquark candidatescitebib.bib42, bib.bib43. Nevertheless, further studies on the inclusion of the light-meson exchanges reveal possibly noticeable effects on the assignments of the spin for the different pentaquark statescitebib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib15, bib.bib14. The other caveat is about the possible underlying CDD pole near the threshold. It is rather challenging to infer the existence of the near-threshold CDD pole a priori. In Refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib34, a practicable criteria is provided to discern the appearance of a near-threshold CDD pole based on the large magnitude of the effective range r𝑟r. This is because the effective range is found to scale as r∝1(MCDD−mth)2,proportional-to𝑟1superscriptsubscript𝑀CDDsubscript𝑚th2displaystyle rproptofrac1(MtextCDD-mrm th)2,, when the CDD position in energy, MCDDsubscript𝑀CDDMtextCDD, approaches to the threshold. To be more specific, if the resulting magnitude of r𝑟r in the ERE study is large, as compared to the typical range around 1fm of strong interactions, it would be a clear hint for the existence of a CDD pole near threshold, indicating as well that the ERE expansion is not suitable to handle such systems. On the contrary, if the resulting |r|𝑟|r| has a value less than 1∼2similar-to121sim 2fm, a near-threshold CDD pole is not necessary and the ERE would not be spoiled by its presence, and it could be used to describe such scattering processes.4Note that a large value of |r|𝑟|r| can also be caused by a near coupled-channel effect. However, this effect could also be related to the presence of a CDD pole in the elastic scattering of the open channel. Regarding the previous discussion, by identifying the observed pentaquark candidate Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs as the resonance pole in the elastic scattering amplitude of Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, one can then fix in every case the corresponding scattering length and effective range via the mass and width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. The size obtained for the effective range would be an indication of whether the resonance can be considered as “molecular” in terms of the channel considered or it better corresponds to a bare state (preexisting resonance). The resonance pole lies in the second Riemann sheet (RS) in the elastic scattering case and the amplitude TI​I​(E)subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸TII(E) in this RS has the form TI​I​(E)=1−1a+12​r​k2+i​k. subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸11𝑎12𝑟superscript𝑘2𝑖𝑘displaystyle TII(E)=frac1-frac1a+frac12r,k2,+i,k,. In this convention, the imaginary part of k𝑘k is understood to be positively taken, namely Im​k>0Im𝑘0rm Imk>0, in Eqs. and . The solution of TI​I​(ER)−1=0subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼superscriptsubscript𝐸𝑅10TII(ER)-1=0 gives the resonance pole position ER=MR−i​ΓR/2subscript𝐸𝑅subscript𝑀𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ𝑅2ER=MR-iGammaR/2, being MRsubscript𝑀𝑅MR and ΓRsubscriptΓ𝑅GammaR the resonance mass and width, respectively. Alternatively, one could also determine a𝑎a and r𝑟r in terms of the resonance pole position ERsubscript𝐸𝑅ER and their explicit relations turn out to becitebib.bib46 a=−2​ki|kr|2+|ki|2,r=−1ki,formulae-sequence𝑎2subscript𝑘𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑟2superscriptsubscript𝑘𝑖2𝑟1subscript𝑘𝑖displaystyle a=-frac2ki|kr|2+|ki|2,,quad r=-frac1ki,, where krsubscript𝑘𝑟kr and kisubscript𝑘𝑖ki stand for the real and imaginary parts of the three-momentum kRsubscript𝑘𝑅kR evaluated at the pole, namely, kR=2​μm​(ER−mth)subscript𝑘𝑅2subscript𝜇𝑚subscript𝐸𝑅subscript𝑚thkR=sqrt2mum(ER-mrm th). For clarity, let us point out again that the convention ki>0subscript𝑘𝑖0ki>0 is taken. The ERE scattering amplitude is completely fixed after a𝑎a and r𝑟r are known, and with it an important quantity, i. e. the residue, which can then be inferred by taking the Laurent expansion of TI​I​(E​(k))subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝐸𝑘TII(E(k)) around the pole position TI​I​(k)=−ki/krk−kR+𝒪​[(k−kR)0]. subscript𝑇𝐼𝐼𝑘subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟𝑘subscript𝑘𝑅𝒪delimited-superscript𝑘subscript𝑘𝑅0displaystyle TII(k)=frac-ki/krk-kR+cal O[(k-kR)0],. In our previous studiescitebib.bib37, bib.bib34, this residue −ki/krsubscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟-ki/kr has been demonstrated to be equal to the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X citebib.bib37, which corresponds to the probability of the two-body component in the resonance, i. e. , X=−kikr. 𝑋subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟displaystyle X=-frackikr,. As proven in Ref. citebib.bib34 when MR−mth>0subscript𝑀𝑅subscript𝑚th0MR-mrm th>0 it follows that the compositeness X=−ki/kr𝑋subscript𝑘𝑖subscript𝑘𝑟X=-ki/kr lies within the range [0,1]01[0,1], a result that is also consistent with the working condition of the probabilistic interpretation revealed in Ref. citebib.bib37. It should be stressed that the expression of the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X in Eq. is only valid in the context of the single-channel ERE when there is a resonance pole in the amplitude. General discussions on the compositeness relations for resonances will be given in the next section. The mass and width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs from the single-resonance fit of the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 read MR=4458.8±2.9−1.1+4.7​MeV,ΓR=17.3±6.5−5.7+8.0​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscript𝑀𝑅plus-or-minus4458.8subscriptsuperscript2.94.71.1MeVsubscriptΓ𝑅plus-or-minus17.3subscriptsuperscript6.58.05.7MeVdisplaystyle MR=4458.8pm 2.9+4.7-1.1textMeV,,qquadGammaR=17.3pm 6.5+8.0-5.7textMeV,, where the first errors are statistics and the second ones are systematic. We will take a conservative estimation and combine the two types of uncertainties by adding in quadrature the statistic error and the largest of the two systematic errors. The explicit values of the mass and width used in our analyses are given in the second and third columns of Table, respectively, together with the thresholds of Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* in the next column. As a tentative study, let us now focus on the two elastic scattering cases to address the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs, i. e. those stemming from taking separately the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, whose thresholds lie near the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs peak observed by the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. Therefore, we separately consider in the ERE analysis that the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs corresponds to the poles in the elastic Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* scattering processes. The coupled-channel studies will be postponed until the next section. The determination of the scattering length and effective range for each case is summarized in the columns fifth and sixth of Table. In the elastic Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD scattering, the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs pole meets the working condition raised in Ref. citebib.bib37, i. e. , its mass is above the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD threshold, so that the prescription in Eq. is eligible for the probabilistic interpretation. The small value of the compositeness X𝑋X of the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD component, shown in the last column, indicates that other components than the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, play more important roles in the constitution of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. The small value of X𝑋X, whose central value is 31%percent3131%, is also consistent with the rather large magnitude of r𝑟r, as the latter hints towards the appearance (within this simplistic elastic picture of Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD scattering) of an underlying CDD pole near threshold, see Eq. . For the case of the elastic Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* scattering, the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs pole does not meet the requirement of the working condition in Ref. citebib.bib37, since the mass of the pole is below threshold. As a result, the prescription of Eq. loses the probabilistic interpretation in the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* case. Nevertheless, the magnitudes of r𝑟r and a𝑎a all correspond to the conventional strong interactions, which hint the natural explanation of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs as a molecular of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* system. This conclusion is further corroborated when discussing the coupled-channel case. Note that the total width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs is assumed to be saturated by only one channel, either the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, in the elastic ERE framework. This assumption should be revisited since the partial decay width to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel is likely non-negligible, which is indeed the channel that is used to measure the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs in experimentcitebib.bib16. As a result, a more realistic study would require the information of the partial decay width to Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD, which will be investigated in the next section within a coupled-channel analysis. Later on various coupled-channel fits to the experimental event distributions of J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda will be also considered.
content_sections.3 Coupled-channel study of J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/\\p: There are intensive discussions in the literature to extend the Weinberg’s compositeness relation for the bound states citebib.bib47, bib.bib48 to the resonance casescitebib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib51, bib.bib41. The key obstacle in the latter situation is that the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X usually becomes complex citebib.bib52. Different proposals have been given in Refs. citebib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib37, bib.bib41 to cure this problem. In Ref. citebib.bib37 the Weinberg’s compositeness relation for bound sates is generalized to resonances by performing a phase transformation of the S𝑆S matrix, so that the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X corresponding to the resonance state turns to be a positive number between 0 and 1, which can be interpreted as the probability to find the two-particle component in a resonance. It is demonstrated in Ref. citebib.bib37 that the compositeness coefficient Xjsubscript𝑋𝑗Xj of a specific channel j𝑗j reduces to taking the absolute value of the product of the complex residue/coupling squared gj2superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑗2gj2 and the derivative of the two-point Green function at the resonance pole position sR=(MR−i​ΓR/2)2subscript𝑠𝑅superscriptsubscript𝑀𝑅𝑖subscriptΓ𝑅22sR=(MR-iGammaR/2)2citebib.bib37, i. e. Therefore we conclude that the values presented in Tables and are robust under the mild changes of n𝑛n. The equations that represent the saturation of the compositeness coefficient X𝑋X and the decay width ΓΓGamma can be used to determine the couplings g1subscript𝑔1g1 and g2subscript𝑔2g2, which in turn allow us to further analyze the partial compositeness factors and the partial decay widths. This approach based on saturating given values of X𝑋X and ΓΓGamma is not suitable to distinguish between the different total angular momenta J𝐽J. The point is that the coupling strengths |gj|2superscriptsubscript𝑔𝑗2|gj|2 would appear in the same way for any total angular momentum J𝐽J. Possible contributions from lighter channels, such as the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda channel, which threshold is rather distant from the interested energy region around 4.46GeV, is effectively reabsorbed in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. To solve Eqs. and , one must first provide the value of the total compositeness X𝑋X, which is however typically unknown a priori. Here we carry out exploring studies by giving some tentative values to X𝑋X, and the results are summarized in Table for the situation in which the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* coupled channels are included, and in Table for the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD case. Three different values of X𝑋X, namely X=0.1,0.5𝑋0.10.5X=0.1,0.5 and 1.0, are used to analyze the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* system. It turns out that the coupling strengths of |g1|subscript𝑔1|g1| and |g2|subscript𝑔2|g2| tends to decrease and increase, respectively, when increasing the values of X𝑋X, so do the partial decay widths of Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 and Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2. This trend is typical in this type of studies as previously observed citebib.bib56, bib.bib32. In all the cases, the partial compositeness factor X2subscript𝑋2X2 of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* is always much larger than the compositeness coefficient X1subscript𝑋1X1 contributed by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. Even when taking X=1.0𝑋1.0X=1.0, the magnitudes of the two partial widths of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channels are similar because |g2|≫|g1|much-greater-thansubscript𝑔2subscript𝑔1|g2|gg|g1|. While, when taking smaller values for the total compositeness coefficient X𝑋X, the widths of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs are found to be dominated by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel. For the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD coupled-channel system the maximum value of X𝑋X that results by solving Eqs. and turns out to be around 0.3. According to the results shown in Table, the partial compositeness factor X2subscript𝑋2X2 is always much larger than the value of X1subscript𝑋1X1, regardless of the total compositeness coefficient X𝑋X taken in the study. In contrast, the magnitudes of the two partial widths are rather sensitive to the values of X𝑋X. Larger values of X𝑋X lead to bigger partial widths Γ2subscriptΓ2Gamma2 of the Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel, and smaller Γ1subscriptΓ1Gamma1 for J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda. It is clear that definite values of X𝑋X will be obviously helpful to give more concrete conclusions of the properties of Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. In the following section, we perform the fits within dynamical unitarized models to the experimental event distributions of the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda from the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. In this way, we are able to obtain more precise values of the coupling strengths gisubscript𝑔𝑖gi, which in turn allow us to perform predictions for the partial decay widths and compositeness coefficients contributed from different channels.
content_sections.4 Fits to the experimental event distrib: In the following, we perform fits to the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda invariant-mass distributions from the LHCbcitebib.bib16 data on the decay Ξb−→J/ψ​Λ​K−→subscriptsuperscriptΞ𝑏𝐽𝜓Λsuperscript𝐾Xi-bto J/psiLambda K-, in order to obtain the couplings and the resonance pole position. In this way one can further constrain the coupling strengths which will in turn give more definite predictions for X𝑋X. We consider first the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel, and later also include the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD one, as these are the two-body channels whose thresholds, lying around 4478 and 4446MeV, respectively, are very close to the resonance region of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. Because of this fact we study their interaction in S𝑆S wave only. In addition, we also include the much lighter channel J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, whose event distribution will be fitted. In principle one could also include the ηc​Λsubscript𝜂𝑐ΛetacLambda since it is related to J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda by the HQSS, in which limit the mass of the J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi and ηcsubscript𝜂𝑐etac are equal. As we are interested only in the energy region around the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance it is then legitimate to characterize both in terms of just one effective channel, namely the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda (more detailed discussions on this point are given below). However, if we fix g′=0superscript𝑔′0gprime=0 and release C12′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12Cprimefrac12 the resulting fit 2subscript32022 in Tables and , gives rise to a very uncertain coupling g𝑔g. Though, it is still compatible within errors with the determinations from the other three-channel fits, its central value is very different, while for the other fits 320 and 1subscript32011 their central values are quite similar. Notice also that again the χ2=2.81superscript𝜒22.81chi2=2.81 has improved only by a basically negligible amount compared to 320. The pole content for these fits is not shown because it is very similar to the one already given in Table. In summary, our reference fit for the three-channel case is the 320 one, in which the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel is introduced perturbatively (g′=C12′=d12′=0superscript𝑔′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12subscriptsuperscript𝑑′120gprime=Cprimefrac12=dprimefrac12=0) and we also take into account both J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2. Releasing either g′superscript𝑔′gprime or C12′subscriptsuperscript𝐶′12Cprimefrac12 does not really provide better fits and the values for the fit parameters are compatible with those from 320. This indicates a perturbative role of the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel, which is also in agreement with our previous results in Sec. when considering only the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channels in J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2. Let us recall that in that case it was not possible to account for the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs resonance signal.

With the determined pole parameters in Table for the fit involving the three-coupled channels J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda, Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, we can revise the discussions of the compositeness and partial decay widths, and make comparisons with the studies based on the saturation of the compositeness coefficient and decay width in the Sec. . For the partial decay width of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs into the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda channel, one can safely use the standard decay width formula in Eq. . For the partial widths into the near-threshold channels Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, we will rely on the Lorentzian spectral integration formula in Eq. . By taking n=8𝑛8n=8, the partial decay widths for the lighter resonance pole with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 in Table are found to be Γ1=0.5−0.5+1.9​MeV,Γ2=4.3−1.4+1.2​MeV,Γ3=0.9−0.6+1.2​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript0.50.51.9MeVformulae-sequencesubscriptΓ2superscriptsubscript4.31.41.2MeVsubscriptΓ3superscriptsubscript0.90.61.2MeVdisplaystyleGamma1=0.5-0.5+1.9rm MeV,,quadGamma2=4.3-1.4+1.2rm MeV,,quadGamma3=0.9-0.6+1.2rm MeV,, and the sum of the three widths agrees nicely with the width of the corresponding resonance pole, i. e. minus twice the imaginary part of the pole. This is also the main reason to take n=8𝑛8n=8 here as well as in Sec. . The partial compositeness coefficients contributed by the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξ′c​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD based on the pole parameters in Table are X1=0.0±0.0,X2=0.15±0.05. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑋1plus-or-minus0.00.0subscript𝑋2plus-or-minus0.150.05displaystyle X1=0.0pm 0.0,,quad X2=0.15pm 0.05,. It should be noted that for the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel the pole position of the lighter resonance state with J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 does not meet the working condition of Ref. citebib.bib37, and as a result it is not very meaningful to interpret the partial X2subscript𝑋2X2 (corresponding to X3subscript𝑋3X3 in the three-channel case) calculated in Eq. as the probability of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* component in that resonance pole. In fact, had we blindly used Eq. to calculate X2subscript𝑋2X2 its value found would be 0.94, so that the sum of compositeness for the three channels would turn out to be slightly larger than 1. This is a manifestation that one should follow the working conditions argued in Ref. citebib.bib37 for giving a probabilistic interpretation of the compositeness X𝑋X of a resonance. For the heavier resonance pole with J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 in Table , the partial widths calculated for the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* are Γ1=2.6−2.6+8.2​MeV,Γ3=0.4−0.4+2.5​MeV,formulae-sequencesubscriptΓ1superscriptsubscript2.62.68.2MeVsubscriptΓ3superscriptsubscript0.40.42.5MeVdisplaystyleGamma1=2.6-2.6+8.2rm MeV,,quadGamma3=0.4-0.4+2.5rm MeV,, and the resulting partial compositeness coefficients turn out to be X1=0.0±0.0,X3=1.0−0.2+0.2. formulae-sequencesubscript𝑋1plus-or-minus0.00.0subscript𝑋3superscriptsubscript1.00.20.2displaystyle X1=0.0pm 0.0,,quad X3=1.0-0.2+0.2,. Despite that the mass of the J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 pole lies clearly below the threshold of Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* we have given X3subscript𝑋3X3 calculated from Eq. as an indication that this resonance is clearly dominated by the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel component. A similar comment could be also made in relation to X3subscript𝑋3X3 for the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 pole. Indeed, we also find for both poles that the coupling |g3|subscript𝑔3|g3| is huge in comparison with |g1|subscript𝑔1|g1| and much larger, by around a factor 7/2, than |g2|subscript𝑔2|g2| for J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2. We point out that a direct comparison of the results for the partial widths and compositeness coefficients in this section and the ones in Sec. is not straightforward. The point is that in Sec. the discussions are based on the masses and widths of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs from the single-state determination of the LHCb collaborationcitebib.bib16. However, in the present section our reference fit prefers the two-resonance solutions and the widths of the two resonance poles taking separately are found to be much smaller than the single-state determinations from the LHCb, though the combination of the two nearby peaks, convoluted with the experimental resolution in energy, gives rise to a total signal as a double bump with a width in agreement with the one pole determination for the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs citebib.bib16. Nonetheless, both the results here and in Sec. are essentially compatible, as one concludes by comparing the couplings |gi|subscript𝑔𝑖|gi| in the last and first columns of Tables and , respectively, with those determined by fitting directly the experimental data in Table.
content_sections.5 Conclusions: In this work we focus on the narrow peak around 4459MeV observed in the J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda event distributions from the LHCb measurementscitebib.bib16, which is the first discovered charmonium pentaquark with strangeness. We have considered three different methods, based on the elastic effective-range expansion, the saturation of the width and the compositeness of the resonance, and a fit to data employing unitarized phenomenological amplitudes with constraints from heavy-quark-spin symmetry. It is important to notice that the different methods provide compatible results for those magnitudes to which they can be applied for their calculation. In this sense, our analyses clearly favor a dominant role of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* state in the composition of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs. For instance, the elastic effective-range analysis gives an effective range of −0.94±0.13plus-or-minus0.940.13-0.94pm 0.13fm when considering only the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD*, while it is much larger in absolute value, around −44-4fm, when taking only the Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD channel. When considering the fits to data we then obtain that the compositeness factor X𝑋X of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel is around one, implying that it is saturated almost entirely by the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* channel alone. Finally, even if allowing the presence of a CDD pole in the two-channel case, with J/ψ​Λ𝐽𝜓ΛJ/psiLambda and either Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* or Ξc′​D¯superscriptsubscriptΞ𝑐′¯𝐷XicprimebarD, no acceptable fits result, which is also a clear indication of the molecular scenario. Another important outcome of our fits is that when requiring heavy-quark-spin symmetry, such that the J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 and 3/2 couplings of the Ξc​D¯∗subscriptΞ𝑐superscript¯𝐷XicbarD* are equal, our analysis requires to include also the Ξc′​D¯subscriptsuperscriptΞ′𝑐¯𝐷XiprimecbarD channel to fit data properly. Furthermore, in such circumstances, the resulting fit contains two poles in the energy region of interest around the resonant peak of the Pc​s​subscript𝑃𝑐𝑠4459Pcs, a J=1/2𝐽12J=1/2 pole with mass 4453.8−3.3+2.4subscriptsuperscript4453.82.43.34453.8+2.4-3.3MeV, and a J=3/2𝐽32J=3/2 one with mass 4466.6−2.7+1.9subscriptsuperscript4466.61.92.74466.6+1.9-2.7MeV. These two poles are narrow, the half-width obtained for the lighter one is 2.8−0.8+0.9subscriptsuperscript2.80.90.82.8+0.9-0.8MeV, and that for the heavier one is determined much less precisely as 1.3−3.7+1.3subscriptsuperscript1.31.33.71.3+1.3-3.7MeV. With the present energy resolution and statistics of the LHCb collaboration citebib.bib16 there is only a small hint that there are actually two resonances involved, though this could be disentangled in the future by experiments with improved statistics.

all_papers.title:
Study of the lineshape of $X(3872)$ using $B$ decays to
  $D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}K$
all_papers.abstract:
We present a study of the $X(3872)$ lineshape in the decay $B \to X(3872)K\to
D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}K$ using a data sample of $772\times 10^6$
$B\overline{B}$ pairs collected at the $\Upsilon(4S)$ resonance with the Belle
detector at the KEKB asymmetric-energy $e^+e^-$ collider. The peak near the
threshold in the $D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}$ invariant mass spectrum is fitted
using a relativistic Breit-Wigner lineshape. We determine the mass and width
parameters to be $m = 3873.71 ^{+0.56}_{-0.50} ({\rm stat}) \pm0.13 ({\rm
syst}) ~{\rm MeV}/c^2$ and $\Gamma_0 = 5.2 ^{+2.2}_{-1.5} ({\rm stat}) \pm 0.4
({\rm syst})~{\rm MeV}$, respectively. The branching fraction is found to be
${\cal B} (B^+\to X(3872)K^+) \times {\cal{B}}(X(3872) \to
D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}) = (0.97 ^{+0.21}_{-0.18} ({\rm stat}) \pm 0.10 ({\rm
syst})) \times 10^{-4}$. The signal from $B^0$ decays is observed for the first
time with $5.2\sigma$ significance, and the ratio of branching fractions
between charged and neutral $B$ decays is measured to be ${\cal B}(B^0\to
X(3872)K^0)/{\cal B}(B^+ \to X(3872)K^+) = 1.34^{+0.47}_{-0.40} ({\rm stat})
^{+0.10}_{-0.12} ({\rm syst})$. The peak is also studied using a Flatt\'{e}
lineshape. We determine the lower limit on the $D\overline{D}{}^{*}$ coupling
constant $g$ to be $0.075$ at 95% credibility in the parameter region where the
ratio of $g$ to the mass difference from the $D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}$ threshold
is equal to $-15.11~{\rm GeV}^{-1}$, as measured by LHCb.

qualitative: The Flatté lineshape is used to model the X(3872) resonance near the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold, accounting for coupled-channel effects through an energy-dependent partial width.

In the near-threshold regime, the Flatté parametrization captures the influence of the D⁰D̄*⁰ channel on the resonance structure, particularly when the resonance mass is close to the threshold and the state can couple to this channel in S-wave.

The Flatté model is especially suited for systems near threshold where the effective range approximation becomes relevant, as it incorporates the scattering amplitude's pole structure and threshold behavior.

The lineshape in the Flatté model reflects the underlying dynamics of the scattering amplitude, including the presence of poles in the complex energy plane, which can provide insight into the nature of the X(3872) as a molecular or resonance state.

The effective range approximation is implicitly embedded in the Flatté formalism, where the coupling strength and threshold parameters determine the shape of the resonance near threshold, particularly when the mass difference from threshold is small.
quantitative: The lower limit on the D⁰D̄*⁰ coupling constant g is determined to be 0.075 at 95% credibility, in the parameter region where the ratio of g to the mass difference from the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold is equal to -15.11 GeV⁻¹, as measured by LHCb.
The D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold mass is 3871.69 ± 0.10 MeV/c².
The X(3872) mass is measured as 3873.71⁺⁰·⁵⁶₋₀·⁵⁰ (stat) ± 0.13 (syst) MeV/c², which lies just above the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold.
The Flatté model allows for the determination of the coupling strength g, which is a key parameter in the effective range approximation for near-threshold resonances.

The Flatté parametrization includes an energy-dependent width that modifies the standard Breit-Wigner form, with the threshold behavior governed by the relation involving g and the mass difference from threshold.

content_sections.I introduction: The charmonium-like X​𝑋3872X state, also known as χc​1​subscript𝜒𝑐13872chic1citebib.bib1, was discovered by the Belle experiment as a narrow peak in the vicinity of the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold in the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- invariant mass distribution in exclusive B+→J/ψ​π+​π−​K+→superscript𝐵𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝐾B+to J/psipi+pi-K+ decayscitebib.bib2. Its existence has been confirmed by multiple experiments: D0citebib.bib3, BABARcitebib.bib4, CDFcitebib.bib5, LHCbcitebib.bib6, and BESIIIcitebib.bib7. In addition to the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- decay, other decays such as J/ψ​ω𝐽𝜓𝜔J/psiomegacitebib.bib8, J/ψ​γ𝐽𝜓𝛾J/psigamma, ψ​(2​S)​γ𝜓2𝑆𝛾psi(2S)gammacitebib.bib9, D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0citebib.bib10, bib.bib11, D0​D¯​π00superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00D0overlineD0pi0citebib.bib12, and π0​χc​0superscript𝜋0subscript𝜒𝑐0pi0chic0citebib.bib13 have been observed. The X​𝑋3872X quantum numbers JP​Csuperscript𝐽𝑃𝐶JPC have been determined to be 1++superscript1absent1++citebib.bib14, bib.bib15. Various interpretations such as a loosely bound statecitebib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, an admixture of a molecular state and a pure charmonium resonancecitebib.bib20, a tetraquarkcitebib.bib21, and a cusp at the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 thresholdcitebib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24 have been proposed, and the structure of the state remains uncertain. Measurement of the lineshape in various decay modes can help to discriminate among different choices for the structure. In this paper, we examine two models for the lineshape in the decay to D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0: a Breit-Wigner, and a Flatté-inspired parametrization. The X​𝑋3872X peak has already been analyzed with the Breit-Wigner lineshape commonly used for resonance states. Based on the analyses of the decays including J/ψ𝐽𝜓J/psi, the mass is 3871.65±0.06​MeV/c2plus-or-minus3871.650.06MeVsuperscript𝑐23871.65pm 0.06rm MeV/c2 and the width is 1.19±0.21​MeVplus-or-minus1.190.21MeV1.19pm 0.21rm MeVcitebib.bib1, with two measurements at the LHCb experimentcitebib.bib25, bib.bib26 contributing significantly to these averages. Analyses of the decay to D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 based on the Breit-Wigner lineshape tend to yield a higher mass and a larger width, with the width measurement subject to large uncertaintiescitebib.bib10, bib.bib11. Discrepancies in the lineshape between the decays to the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- and D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 final states can arise near the threshold due to coupled-channel effectscitebib.bib22. This may be significant for the X​𝑋3872X, as the observed mass coincides with the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold of 3871.69±0.10​MeV/c2plus-or-minus3871.690.10MeVsuperscript𝑐23871.69pm 0.10rm MeV/c2, and a 1++superscript1absent1++ state can couple to the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 channel in S-wave. One model to account for coupled-channel effects is the Flatté-inspired parametrizationcitebib.bib22, bib.bib27, a Breit-Wigner model with an explicit expression for the energy-dependent partial width. At LHCb, an analysis of the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- invariant mass distribution was performed using this Flatté-inspired modelcitebib.bib26. It is difficult to determine all of the parameters using only this distribution, due to a scaling behavior in which the lineshape near the threshold does not change under a linear transformation of four of the five parameterscitebib.bib26, bib.bib28. To determine all the parameters, it is important to analyze not only the J/ψ​π+​π−𝐽𝜓superscript𝜋superscript𝜋J/psipi+pi- decay but also the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decay, as proposed in the theoretical analysiscitebib.bib27. By analyzing the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decay, we aim to provide more information on the lineshape, and in particular on the coupling strength of X​→D0​D¯∗0→𝑋3872superscript𝐷0superscript¯𝐷absent0Xto D0barD*0. In this paper, we present a study of the X​𝑋3872X lineshape using a sample of X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 candidates produced in the exclusive decay B→D0​D¯​K∗0→𝐵superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscript𝐾absent0Bto D0overlineD*0K using the full Belle dataset. There have been three previous studiescitebib.bib12, bib.bib10, bib.bib11. Referencecitebib.bib12 is an analysis of the B→D0​D¯​π00​K→𝐵superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00𝐾Bto D0overlineD0pi0K decay at Belle, and Refscitebib.bib10, bib.bib11 are analyses of the B→D0​D¯​K∗0→𝐵superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscript𝐾absent0Bto D0overlineD*0K decays at BABAR and Belle, respectively. The latter two analyses apply a D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 selection and a mass-constrained fit to the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 candidates. While this has the advantage of improving the signal-to-noise ratio, it has the disadvantage of disallowing entries below the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold, which is important for studying the structure. Given the limited size of our data sample, we adopt a similar technique to the latter analyses, i. e. subtracting the reconstructed D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 mass and adding the nominal mass. The disadvantage of requiring the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 is partially compensated for by analyzing the Flatté model, in which we can obtain a lineshape reflecting poles of the scattering amplitude. Compared to Refscitebib.bib10, bib.bib11, additional D0superscript𝐷0D0 decay modes are included, increasing the efficiency to reconstruct D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 decays. Throughout this paper, charge conjugation is always included. We do not distinguish D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 from D¯​D∗00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝐷absent00overlineD0D*0 unless otherwise indicated. The paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , the Belle detector and data set are described. In Sections and, the event selection and the fitted model are presented. In Sec. , the results of fitting the data with the relativistic Breit-Wigner model and the Flatté model are presented. Section contains a discussion of the results, and the conclusions of the paper.
content_sections.II Detector and Data Set: We use a data sample of 772×106772superscript106772times 106 B​B¯𝐵¯𝐵BoverlineB pairs, collected at a center-of-mass energy of s=10.58​GeV𝑠10.58GeVsqrts=10.58rm GeV, corresponding to the Υ​(4​S)Υ4𝑆Upsilon(4S) resonance, with the Belle detector at the KEKB asymmetric-energy e+​e−superscript𝑒superscript𝑒e+e- collidercitebib.bib29, bib.bib30. The Belle detector is a large-solid-angle magnetic spectrometer that consists of a silicon vertex detector (SVD), a 50-layer central drift chamber (CDC), an array of aerogel threshold Cherenkov counters (ACC), a barrel-like arrangement of time-of-flight scintillation counters (TOF), and an electromagnetic calorimeter comprised of CsI(Tl) crystals (ECL) located inside a super-conducting solenoid coil that provides a 1.5T magnetic field. An iron flux-return located outside of the coil is installed to detect KL0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝐿0KL0 mesons and to identify muons (KLM). The detector is described in detail elsewherecitebib.bib31, bib.bib32. To determine the event selection and the detector response, we use a sample of Monte Carlo (MC) simulated events generated using the EvtGen event generatorcitebib.bib33. The detector response is simulated using the GEANT3 packagecitebib.bib34.
content_sections.III Event Selection: The event selection is determined using the MC samples in two steps. First, the selection criteria for the final-state particles are determined based on our previous studiescitebib.bib12, bib.bib11. Second, the selection criteria for the intermediate-state particles are optimized by maximizing the figure-of-merit S/S+B𝑆𝑆𝐵S/sqrtS+B, where S𝑆S and B𝐵B are the estimated numbers of signal and background events, respectively. The resulting selection is described below. Tracks are selected using vertex information measured by the tracking system. A track candidate is accepted if its distance along the detector axis from the point of closest approach to the interaction point is less than 4.0cm, and its distance transverse to the detector axis is less than 1.0cm. These requirements are not imposed for tracks in KS0→π+​π−→superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋KS0topi+pi- candidates. In addition, pion and kaon candidates are selected using likelihoods ℒπsubscriptℒ𝜋cal Lpi and ℒKsubscriptℒ𝐾cal LK based on the time-of-flight measured by the TOF, the number of Cherenkov photons detected by the ACC, and the ionization loss in the CDC. Tracks with a likelihood ratio ℒπ/(ℒπ+ℒK)>0.1subscriptℒ𝜋subscriptℒ𝜋subscriptℒ𝐾0.1cal Lpi/(cal Lpi+cal LK)>0.1 are used as charged pion candidates, and tracks with ℒπ/(ℒπ+ℒK)0.95subscriptℒ𝑒subscriptℒ𝑒subscriptℒ𝑒0.95cal Le/(cal Le+cal Ltildee)>0.95 are identified as electrons and eliminated. Here, ℒesubscriptℒ𝑒cal Le and ℒesubscriptℒ𝑒cal Ltildee are distinct likelihoods for the electron and non-electron hypotheses, based on ECL, tracking, and other information. The particle identification is described in detail elsewherecitebib.bib35. KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 candidates are reconstructed from charged pion pairs with opposite charges. The π+​π−superscript𝜋superscript𝜋pi+pi- invariant mass is required to agree with the known KS0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0KS0 masscitebib.bib1 within 7​MeV/c27MeVsuperscript𝑐27rm MeV/c2 (≈3.6​σabsent3.6𝜎approx 3.6sigma of the resolution). Candidates are selected using a neural network classifiercitebib.bib36 with various kinematic variables as input. To improve the four-momentum resolution, a mass- and vertex-constrained fit is applied. Photon candidates are reconstructed from ECL clusters with no matching charged tracks. Candidates are selected based on the ratio, E9/E25subscript𝐸9subscript𝐸25E9/E25, of the energy deposited in the 3×3333times 3 array of crystals centered on the crystal with the highest energy deposition to that in the 5×5555times 5 array: we require E9/E25>0.8subscript𝐸9subscript𝐸250.8E9/E25>0.8. Neutral pions are reconstructed from photon pairs. The photons are required to have energy greater than 30​MeV30MeV30rm MeV in the barrel region or 50​MeV50MeV50rm MeV in the endcaps. The γ​γ𝛾𝛾gammagamma invariant mass is required to agree with the π0superscript𝜋0pi0 nominal masscitebib.bib1 within 12​MeV/c212MeVsuperscript𝑐212rm MeV/c2. This mass window corresponds to 92% signal efficiency. A mass-constrained fit is applied to improve the momentum resolution. D0superscript𝐷0D0 candidates are reconstructed in six decay modes: K−​π+superscript𝐾superscript𝜋K-pi+, K−​π+​π0superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0K-pi+pi0, K−​π+​π−​π+superscript𝐾superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋K-pi+pi-pi+, KS0​π+​π−superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋KS0pi+pi-, KS0​π+​π−​π0superscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋superscript𝜋0KS0pi+pi-pi0, and K+​K−superscript𝐾superscript𝐾K+K-. The π0superscript𝜋0pi0 candidates used in this reconstruction are required to have momentum in the center-of-mass system greater than 100​MeV/c100MeV𝑐100rm MeV/c, and energy in the laboratory system greater than 150​MeV150MeV150rm MeV. If a π0superscript𝜋0pi0 is included, the reconstructed D0superscript𝐷0D0 invariant mass is required to be within 16​MeV/c216MeVsuperscript𝑐216rm MeV/c2 of the nominal masscitebib.bib1 corresponding to 85% signal efficiency; otherwise, it is required to be within 8.5​MeV/c28.5MeVsuperscript𝑐28.5rm MeV/c2 corresponding to 91% efficiency. To improve the momentum resolution, a mass- and vertex-constrained fit is applied. Candidates where the χ2superscript𝜒2chi2 probability of the fit is less than 0.0001 are eliminated. D¯∗0overlineD*0 candidates are reconstructed in two decay modes: D¯​γ0¯𝐷superscript𝛾0overlineD0gamma and D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0. For the D¯​γ0¯𝐷superscript𝛾0overlineD0gamma mode, only γ𝛾gamma candidates with an energy greater than 90​MeV90MeV90rm MeV in the laboratory system are used. For the D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0 mode, only π0superscript𝜋0pi0 candidates with a momentum in the center-of-mass system of less than 100​MeV/c100MeV𝑐100rm MeV/c and an energy in the laboratory system of less than 200​MeV200MeV200rm MeV are used. The difference in the reconstructed mass between D¯∗0overlineD*0 and D¯0overlineD0 is required to agree with the nominal valuecitebib.bib1 within 9.0​MeV/c29.0MeVsuperscript𝑐29.0rm MeV/c2 and 2.0​MeV/c22.0MeVsuperscript𝑐22.0rm MeV/c2 for D¯​γ0¯𝐷superscript𝛾0overlineD0gamma and D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0, respectively, corresponding to 90% signal efficiency in each case. B𝐵B meson candidates are then reconstructed in the decay modes D0​D¯​K+∗0superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝐾absent0D0overlineD*0K+ and D0​D¯​KS0∗0superscript𝐷0¯𝐷superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐾𝑆0absent0D0overlineD*0KS0. To reduce wrong combinations, the daughter K+superscript𝐾K+ is required to have ℒK/(ℒπ+ℒK)>0.6subscriptℒ𝐾subscriptℒ𝜋subscriptℒ𝐾0.6cal LK/(cal Lpi+cal LK)>0.6, corresponding to an identification efficiency of 89%. The B𝐵B candidates are selected based on the beam-energy constrained mass, Mbc≡(Ebeamcms)2−(pBcms)2subscript𝑀bcsuperscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝐸beamcms2superscriptsuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝐵cms2Mrm bcequivsqrt(Erm beamrm cms)2-(pBrm cms)2 and the difference of the energy in the center-of-mass system between the B𝐵B candidate and the beam, Δ​E≡EBcms−EbeamcmsΔ𝐸superscriptsubscript𝐸𝐵cmssuperscriptsubscript𝐸beamcmsDelta Eequiv EBrm cms-Erm beamrm cms, where Ebeamcmssuperscriptsubscript𝐸beamcmsErm beamrm cms is the beam energy in the center-of-mass system corresponding to half of s𝑠sqrts, and pBcmssuperscriptsubscript𝑝𝐵cmspBrm cms and EBcmssuperscriptsubscript𝐸𝐵cmsEBrm cms are the energy and momentum of B𝐵B candidates in the center-of-mass system, respectively. We retain events with Mbc>5.2​GeV/c2subscript𝑀bc5.2GeVsuperscript𝑐2Mrm bc>5.2rm GeV/c2 and |Δ​E|<50​MeVΔ𝐸50MeV|Delta E|<50rm MeV for later analysis. The Mbcsubscript𝑀bcMrm bc signal region is defined as |Mbc−mB|<4.5​MeV/c2subscript𝑀bcsubscript𝑚𝐵4.5MeVsuperscript𝑐2|Mrm bc-mB|<4.5rm MeV/c2 (≈2​σabsent2𝜎approx 2sigma) for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma and |Mbc−mB|<6.0​MeV/c2subscript𝑀bcsubscript𝑚𝐵6.0MeVsuperscript𝑐2|Mrm bc-mB|<6.0rm MeV/c2 (≈2.5​σabsent2.5𝜎approx 2.5sigma) for D¯→∗0D¯π00overlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0, where mBsubscript𝑚𝐵mB denotes the nominal B𝐵B masscitebib.bib1. The Δ​EΔ𝐸Delta E signal region is defined as |Δ​E|<12​MeVΔ𝐸12MeV|Delta E|<12rm MeV (≈2​σabsent2𝜎approx 2sigma). For suppression of continuum events, we use a FastBDT classifiercitebib.bib37 trained on the simulation sample with the following event-shape information as input: modified Fox-Wolfram momentscitebib.bib38, the momentum flow in concentric cones around the thrust axiscitebib.bib39, and thrust-related quantities. Events for which the classifier output is less than 0.15 are eliminated. This requirement retains 96% of the signal candidates and rejects 49% of the candidates of continuum events. After this selection, the average number of B𝐵B candidates per event is 1.8, because D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 and D∗0D¯0D*0overlineD0 are often indistinguishable and double-counted. To avoid multiple counting of signal events, we select the candidate that has the highest value of the product of the following likelihood ℒℒcal L and prior probability 𝒫𝒫cal P ℒ=ℒM​(D0)×ℒM(D¯)0×ℒM(D¯)∗0−M(D¯)0×ℒΔ​E[×ℒM​(π0)],𝒫=εi​j​kζi​j​k×ℬ(D0→i)×ℬ(D¯→0j)×ℬ(D¯→∗0k),beginsplitcal L=cal LM(D0)timescal LM(overlineD0)timescal LM(overlineD*0)-M(overlineD0) qquadqquadqquadqquadqquadtimescal LDelta E[timescal LM(pi0)], cal P=fracvarepsilonijkzetaijktimescal B(D0to i)timescal B(overlineD0to j)timescal B(overlineD*0to k),endsplit where ℒℒcal L is the product of the likelihoods of the measured D0superscript𝐷0D0, D¯0overlineD0, and D¯∗0overlineD*0 masses, and Δ​EΔ𝐸Delta E; and, for the D¯→∗0D¯π00overlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0 mode, the likelihood of the measured π0superscript𝜋0pi0 mass. Each likelihood is obtained using probability density functions (PDFs) determined using the MC samples. The probability 𝒫𝒫cal P is obtained from the probability that a signal event can be reconstructed εi​j​ksubscript𝜀𝑖𝑗𝑘varepsilonijk, the average number of B𝐵B candidates per event ζi​j​ksubscript𝜁𝑖𝑗𝑘zetaijk, and the decay branching fraction, when D0superscript𝐷0D0, D¯0overlineD0, and D¯∗0overlineD*0 are reconstructed in the i𝑖i, j𝑗j, and k𝑘k modes, respectively. The values of εi​j​ksubscript𝜀𝑖𝑗𝑘varepsilonijk and ζi​j​ksubscript𝜁𝑖𝑗𝑘zetaijk are determined using the MC samples. The (Mbc,Δ​E)subscript𝑀bcΔ𝐸(Mrm bc,Delta E) distribution of the selected B𝐵B candidates is shown in Fig. . The red solid (blue dashed) rectangle shows the (Mbc,Δ​E)subscript𝑀bcΔ𝐸(Mrm bc,Delta E) signal region for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma (D¯​π00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00overlineD0pi0): B𝐵B candidates used in the lineshape study are selected from this region. For all events remaining in the selection, the following D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 invariant mass is calculated instead of applying a mass-constrained fit to improve the mass resolution, M(D0D¯)∗0=M​(D0​D¯​γ0)−M​(D¯​γ0)+mD¯∗0forD¯→∗0D¯γ0,M​(D0​D¯​π00)−M​(D¯​π00)+mD¯∗0forD¯→∗0D¯π00,beginsplitM(D0overlineD*0) =leftbeginarrayllM(D0overlineD0gamma)-M(overlineD0gamma)+moverlineD*0 rm foroverlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma, M(D0overlineD0pi0)-M(overlineD0pi0)+moverlineD*0 rm foroverlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0, endarrayright. endsplit where the reconstructed D¯∗0overlineD*0 invariant mass, M​(D¯​γ0)𝑀¯𝐷superscript𝛾0M(overlineD0gamma) or M​(D¯​π00)𝑀¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋00M(overlineD0pi0), is subtracted, and the D¯∗0overlineD*0 nominal mass, mD¯∗0moverlineD*0, is added. The lineshape and signal yield are determined by fitting the distribution in the region below 4.0​GeV/c24.0GeVsuperscript𝑐24.0rm GeV/c2.
content_sections.IV Fit Strategy and Detector Response: In this work, the obtained M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) distributions are fitted with two lineshape models: the relativistic Breit-Wigner, and a Flatté-inspired model. The fits with these two models, shown in the next section, are performed with the following procedure. When signal events are reconstructed correctly, the invariant mass distribution has a peak consisting of the natural lineshape convolved with the mass-dependent detector response. This response, i. e. the mass dependence of the signal efficiency and the mass resolution, is studied and parameterized using a set of X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 MC samples generated with zero width, and a range of mass values from the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold to 4.0​GeV/c24.0GeVsuperscript𝑐24.0rm GeV/c2. Here, the X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 decays are generated using a uniform phase space model; the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 width is assumed to be around 60keVcitebib.bib23. Since the signal-to-noise ratio depends on the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 decay mode, fits are performed separately for D∗0→D0​γ→superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷0𝛾D*0to D0gamma and D∗0→D0​π0→superscript𝐷absent0superscript𝐷0superscript𝜋0D*0to D0pi0. In addition, fits are performed separately for B0superscript𝐵0B0 and B+superscript𝐵B+ candidates to determine the ratio of branching fractions between B0→X​​K0→superscript𝐵0𝑋3872superscript𝐾0B0to XK0 and B+→X​​K+→superscript𝐵𝑋3872superscript𝐾B+to XK+. The fit function for correctly reconstructed X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 decays, which we refer to as “signal”, is constructed as follows. The signal efficiency varies depending on the mass by a few tens of percent, especially around the threshold, as shown in Fig. (a). It is parameterized by the threshold function p0​1−p1​ep2​(M−mD0−mD∗0)+p3​(M−mD0−mD∗0)subscript𝑝01subscript𝑝1superscript𝑒subscript𝑝2𝑀subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0subscript𝑝3𝑀subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0p01-p1ep2(M-mD0-mD*0)+p3(M-mD0-mD*0) with parameters p0subscript𝑝0p0–p3subscript𝑝3p3 in the low-mass region, which is continuously connected to a constant value in the high-mass region. The mass resolution for the signal is modeled as the sum of a Gaussian and a reversed Crystal Ball functioncitebib.bib40 with a common mean. Figure(b) shows the M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) spread due to the resolution, and the resolution function used in this work, for several choices of the X​𝑋3872X mass. As noted in the previous Belle study, the resolution degrades with the square root of the difference between the mass and the thresholdcitebib.bib11. The convolution with the mass-dependent resolution function entails longer computation times. The effect of smearing due to the resolution is small at masses away from the peak, since the natural lineshape is broadcitebib.bib10, bib.bib11. For example, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the natural lineshape is a few MeV, while the FWHM of the mass resolution near the peak is only about 220 keV. Therefore, instead of convolution with the mass-dependent resolution function, convolution with the specific resolution function at the mass of 3871.9​MeV/c23871.9MeVsuperscript𝑐23871.9rm MeV/c2 (near the peak) is adopted as an approximation. To reproduce the behavior near threshold, the signal function is multiplied by a soft threshold function that rises from zero to one at the threshold using an error function. The procedure is validated on X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 MC samples generated with a broad lineshape. The effect of the approximation is negligible. The ratios of signal yields among the decay modes are fixed in the fit using the product of the expected total signal efficiency and the branching fraction of each decay mode. Here the expected signal efficiency depends on the lineshape because of the mass dependence of the signal efficiency. The total signal efficiency is obtained by averaging the signal efficiencies as a function of mass weighted by the values of the lineshape function. It is then corrected by taking account of the signal which may leak out of the fit range, depending on the lineshape, and by taking the ratio of the area of the signal function in the fit range to that from the threshold to mB−mKsubscript𝑚𝐵subscript𝑚𝐾mB-mK. Here, a mass-dependent resolution function is convolved with the signal function, because smearing due to the resolution is important at higher masses. The calculation of the total signal efficiency is validated on MC samples for a broad range of lineshape parameters. A separate fit function is used for “broken signal”: cases where the D0superscript𝐷0D0 is reconstructed incorrectly, a wrong π0superscript𝜋0pi0 or γ𝛾gamma is combined in the D¯∗0overlineD*0 reconstruction, or a D∗0D¯0D*0overlineD0 signal event is misinterpreted as D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 by combining π0superscript𝜋0pi0 or γ𝛾gamma from D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 incorrectly with the D¯0overlineD0 to make a fake D¯∗0overlineD*0. For the D¯→∗0D¯π00overlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0 mode, such events produce a broad peak in the M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) signal region and possibly distort the lineshape of the signal. The fit function for the broken signal therefore takes account of the mass dependencies of the resolution and the efficiency as in the case of correctly reconstructed signal events. The mass dependence of the efficiency is parameterized using the same threshold function used for the signal. The resolution is reproduced by a triple Gaussian multiplied by a soft threshold function at the D0D¯∗0D0overlineD*0 threshold, and its mass dependence is studied and parameterized using zero-width MC samples. Since the resolution for the broken signal is several times worse than that for the signal, we do not use approximated convolution: we instead use a discrete convolution with the mass-dependent resolution function. In the fit, the yield of the broken signal relative to the signal is fixed to the value expected from the lineshape and the ratio of the total efficiency of the broken signal to that of the signal. The broken signal peak due to the D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma mode has little sensitivity to the natural lineshape. To reduce the systematic uncertainty due to the shape, we use a histogram PDF depending on the lineshape. This PDF is obtained by plotting the broken-signal histogram for each of the zero-width MC samples, scaling it by the value of the assumed lineshape at the generated mass, and summing up all of the scaled histograms. Here, the bin widths are adjusted to increase as the mass increases to suppress statistical fluctuations. The background from e+​e−→q​q¯→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒𝑞¯𝑞e+e-to qbarq (q=u,d,s,c𝑞𝑢𝑑𝑠𝑐q=u,d,s,c) continuum events, and e+​e−→Υ​(4​S)→B​B¯→superscript𝑒superscript𝑒Υ4𝑆→𝐵¯𝐵e+e-toUpsilon(4S)to BoverlineB events other than signal, is studied using the background MC sample. The shape of the invariant mass distribution is reproduced using a threshold function, M−(mD0+mD∗0)𝑀subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0sqrtM-(mD0+mD*0), where mD0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0mD0 and mD∗0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0mD*0 are the nominal masses of D0superscript𝐷0D0 and D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0citebib.bib1, respectively.
content_sections.V Fit to Data V.1 Relativistic Breit-Wig: The relativistic Breit-Wigner lineshape function is defined ascitebib.bib1 fBW​(M)=mBW​M​Γ​(M)(M2−mBW2)2+mBW2​Γ​(M)2,subscript𝑓BW𝑀subscript𝑚BW𝑀Γ𝑀superscriptsuperscript𝑀2superscriptsubscript𝑚BW22superscriptsubscript𝑚BW2Γsuperscript𝑀2frm BW(M)=fracmrm BWMGamma(M)(M2-mrm BW2)2+mrm BW2Gamma(M)2, where M𝑀M is the observed invariant mass, and mBWsubscript𝑚BWmrm BW is the mass of the resonance. The mass-dependent width Γ​(M)Γ𝑀Gamma(M) is defined as Γ(M)=ΓBWmBWM(p​(M)p​(mBW))2​L+1,Gamma(M)=Gammarm BWfracmrm BWMbiggr(fracp(M)p(mrm BW)biggl)2L+1, where ΓBWsubscriptΓBWGammarm BW and L𝐿L are the width of the resonance and the orbital angular momentum, respectively. Taking account of the closeness to the threshold, the decay is assumed to be pure S-wave (L=0𝐿0L=0) with no D-wave (L=2𝐿2L=2) admixture. The momentum of one of the daughters in the rest frame of X​𝑋3872X, p​(M)𝑝𝑀p(M), can be calculated as p​(M)=12​M​(M2−(mD0+mD∗0)2)×(M2−(mD0−mD∗0)2). 𝑝𝑀12𝑀superscript𝑀2superscriptsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent02superscript𝑀2superscriptsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent02beginsplitp(M)=frac12Msqrt(M2-(mD0+mD*0)2) timessqrt(M2-(mD0-mD*0)2). endsplit Figure presents the M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) distributions obtained from the data. Here, unbinned maximum likelihood fits are performed simultaneously to the distributions for the D¯∗0overlineD*0 decay modes, D¯→∗0D¯π0overlineD*0tooverlineDpi0 and D¯​γ¯𝐷𝛾overlineDgamma, and for the B+superscript𝐵B+ and B0superscript𝐵0B0 samples, with common fit parameters m𝑚m and Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0. The PDFs are convolved with the detector response as described in the previous section. Table summarizes the parameters obtained from the fit. The significance is determined from the log-likelihood ratio −2​ln⁡(ℒ0/ℒ)2subscriptℒ0ℒ-2ln(cal L0/cal L) accounting for the difference in the number of degrees of freedom, where ℒ0subscriptℒ0cal L0 and ℒℒcal L are the fit likelihood without and with the peak component; i. e. , the yield is constrained to be zero for the significance of each B𝐵B mode, and the parameters m𝑚m and Γ0subscriptΓ0Gamma0 are additionally dropped for the combined significance. We consider the following nine sources of uncertainty on the mass, the width, and the signal yield: (i) The uncertainty due to the assumed shape of the generic background is estimated by performing a fit after changing the PDF from the threshold function with a square root to an inverted ARGUS functioncitebib.bib41. (ii) The mass resolution is validated by comparing the data and MC Δ​EΔ𝐸Delta E resolution in the B+→D¯​π+∗0​π−​π+→superscript𝐵¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝜋absent0superscript𝜋superscript𝜋B+tooverlineD*0pi+pi-pi+ control sample, which has a similar decay topology to B→X(→D0D¯)∗0KBto X(to D0overlineD*0)K. The ratios of the mass resolution obtained for MC and data are 1.01±0.10plus-or-minus1.010.101.01pm 0.10 for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma and 1.08±0.13plus-or-minus1.080.131.08pm 0.13 for D¯→∗0D¯π00overlineD*0tooverlineD0pi0. This resolution is consistent in data and MC, so no correction is applied, and the associated uncertainty is assigned by performing fits with the resolution varied by the precision, ±1​σ≡±13%plus-or-minus1𝜎plus-or-minuspercent13pm 1sigmaequivpm 13%. (iii, iv) The uncertainties arising from the mass dependence of the efficiency and the ratio of the broken-signal to the signal are evaluated by summing in quadrature the changes induced by ±1​σplus-or-minus1𝜎pm 1sigma variations of the relevant parameters. (v) The uncertainty due to possible bias in the fit is evaluated by performing pseudo experiments. The input value of a parameter subtracted from the median of the parameter distribution is regarded as the corresponding uncertainty. (vi) For the mBWsubscript𝑚BWmrm BW measurement only, the uncertainty arising from the finite precision of the D0superscript𝐷0D0 mass and the Δ​(mD∗0−mD0)Δsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0Delta(mD*0-mD0) mass difference is taken as the ±1​σplus-or-minus1𝜎pm 1sigma uncertainty of 2​mD0+Δ​(mD∗0−mD0)=3871.69±0.10​MeV/c22subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0Δsubscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷0plus-or-minus3871.690.10MeVsuperscript𝑐22mD0+Delta(mD*0-mD0)=3871.69pm 0.10rm MeV/c2 following Ref. citebib.bib1. (vii) The nonzero D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 width (ΓD∗0subscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absent0GammaD*0) leads to three potential sources of bias: a bias arising from the mass difference technique, a bias arising from the consideration of the D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 width in the lineshape model, and a bias due to the interference between X→D0D¯∗0Xto D0overlineD*0 and D¯​D∗00¯𝐷superscriptsuperscript𝐷absent00overlineD0D*0. Biases from these three sources are evaluated as follows. For the first bias, two M(D0D¯)∗0M(D0overlineD*0) distributions are formed in MC with a broad lineshape: one where mD∗0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0mD*0 in Eq. is fixed to the nominal value (as in our analysis), and the other where mD∗0subscript𝑚superscript𝐷absent0mD*0 is replaced by the true D∗0superscript𝐷absent0D*0 mass generated by EvtGen, where ΓD∗0=65.5​keVsubscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absent065.5keVGammaD*0=65.5rm keVcitebib.bib23 is assumed. Each distribution is fitted with the PDF of the signal component, and the largest difference is regarded as the associated uncertainty. For the second bias, the distribution for data is fitted after smearing the assumed lineshape with a Breit-Wigner function of ΓD∗0=65.5​keVsubscriptΓsuperscript𝐷absent065.5keVGammaD*0=65.5rm keV, and the change from the original result is regarded as the associated uncertainty. The third bias is ignored since the interference effect is negligible above the thresholdcitebib.bib42. The uncertainties associated with the first and second biases are added in quadrature. (viii) Limited MC statistics lead to uncertainty on the shape of the broken-signal for D¯→∗0D¯γ0overlineD*0tooverlineD0gamma. This is evaluated by repeating the fit while varying each bin entry of the MC PDF histogram assuming Poisson distributions. The 68% interval of the distributions of the resulting fit values is used to assign the uncertainty.
