all_papers.title: Effect of scalar condensation on fermionic pole-skipping
all_papers.abstract:
In this paper, we investigated the holographic fermionic pole-skipping
phenomena for a class of interacting theory in a charged AdS black hole
background. We have studied two types of fermion-scalar interactions in the
bulk: Dipole and Yukawa type interaction. Depending upon the interaction we
introduced both real and charged scalar fields. We have particularly analyzed
the effect of scalar condensation on the fermionic pole-skipping points and
discussed their behaviour near critical temperatures.

all_papers.title:
Out-of-equilibrium dynamics of quantum many-body systems with long-range
  interactions
all_papers.abstract:
Experimental progress in atomic, molecular, and optical platforms in the last
decade has stimulated strong and broad interest in the quantum coherent
dynamics of many long-range interacting particles. The prominent collective
character of these systems enables novel non-equilibrium phenomena with no
counterpart in conventional quantum systems with local interactions. Much of
the theory work in this area either focussed on the impact of variable-range
interaction tails on the physics of local interactions or relied on
mean-field-like descriptions based on the opposite limit of all-to-all
infinite-range interactions. In this Report, we present a systematic and
organic review of recent advances in the field. Working with prototypical
interacting quantum spin lattices without disorder, our presentation hinges
upon a versatile theoretical formalism that interpolates between the few-body
mean-field physics and the many-body physics of quasi-local interactions. Such
a formalism allows us to connect these two regimes, providing both a formal
quantitative tool and basic physical intuition. We leverage this unifying
framework to review several findings of the last decade, including the peculiar
non-ballistic spreading of quantum correlations, counter-intuitive slowdown of
entanglement dynamics, suppression of thermalization and equilibration,
anomalous scaling of defects upon traversing criticality, dynamical phase
transitions, and genuinely non-equilibrium phases stabilized by periodic
driving. The style of this Report is on the pedagogical side, which makes it
accessible to readers without previous experience in the subject matter.

all_papers.title:
Post-Newtonian Dynamics of Spinning Black Hole Binaries in
  Einstein-Scalar-Gauss-Bonnet Gravity
all_papers.abstract:
We explore the post-Newtonian dynamics of spinning black hole (BH) binaries
in Einstein-ScalarGauss-Bonnet (ESGB) gravity, a theory that modifies general
relativity by introducing a massless scalar field coupled nonminimally to
gravity via the Gauss-Bonnet term. By employing an effective field theory (EFT)
approach, we extend the Routhian formalism to incorporate spin effects in
scalar interactions. In this formalism, we derive for the first time the
effective two-body potential for spinning BHs up to the third post-Newtonian
(3PN) order in generic ESGB gravity theories. This potential is expressed in
terms of the sensitivities of the BHs, which are then obtained through a
matching procedure using analytic BH solutions derived here within a
slow-rotation approximation, accurate to seventh order in the Gauss-Bonnet
coupling and fifth order in the BH spin. We also examine the thermodynamic
properties of these rotating solutions, which, as shown in previous work, yield
important insights into the inspiral phase evolution in ESGB gravity.

all_papers.title:
The Quadrupole Moment of Compact Binaries to the Fourth post-Newtonian
  Order: II. Dimensional Regularization and Renormalization
all_papers.abstract:
The regularization and renormalization of the radiative mass-type quadrupole
moment of inspiralling compact binaries (without spins) is investigated at the
fourth post-Newtonian (4PN) approximation of general relativity. As clear from
the conservative 4PN equations of motion, a dimensional regularization has to
be implemented in order to properly treat the non-linear interactions
experienced by gravitational waves during their propagation toward future null
infinity. By implementing such procedure, we show that the poles coming from
the source moment (computed in a companion paper) are exactly cancelled in the
radiative moment, as expected for a physical quantity. We thus define and
obtain a "renormalized" source quadrupole, three-dimensional by nature, which
is an important step towards the computation of the gravitational-wave flux
with 4PN accuracy. Furthermore, we explicitly prove the equivalence between the
dimensional regularization and the previously used Hadamard partie finie scheme
up to the 3PN order.

all_papers.title: Frame transformation and stable-causal hydrodynamic theory
all_papers.abstract:
In this work, a connection has been indicated between the different existing
formulations of relativistic hydrodynamic theories, which, in order to be
causal and stable, (i) either requires `non-fluid' variables apart from
velocity and temperature to be promoted to new degrees of freedom, or, (ii)
needs to be in a generalized hydrodynamic frame other than those given by
Landau or Eckart. The BDNK stress tensor (originally in a general frame) has
been rewritten in the Landau frame using linearized all-order
gradient-corrected redefinitions of the temperature and velocity fields. The
redefinitions indicate that, while the BDNK formalism has a finite number of
derivatives in the general frame, when written in the Landau frame, it either
has an infinite number of derivatives, or one has to introduce MIS-like
`non-fluid' variables by summing the infinite number of derivatives in the
field redefinitions. There can be non-unique ways of performing these
infinite-order summations. Finally, the dispersion relations and the
corresponding spectra of these different systems of MIS type equations have
been analyzed to check that the systems of equations presented here are indeed
equivalent to the BDNK formalism, at least in the hydrodynamic regime.

all_papers.title: Long Range, Large Charge, Large $N$
all_papers.abstract:
We study operators with large charge $j$ in the $d$-dimensional $O(N)$ model
with long range interactions that decrease with the distance as $1/r^{d+s}$,
where $s$ is a continuous parameter. We consider the double scaling limit of
large $N$, large $j$ with $j/N=\hat{j}$ fixed, and identify the semiclassical
saddle point that captures the two-point function of the large charge operators
in this limit. The solution is given in terms of certain ladder conformal
integrals that have recently appeared in the literature on fishnet models. We
find that the scaling dimensions for general $s$ interpolate between $\Delta_j
\sim \frac{(d-s)}{2}j$ at small $\hat{j}$ and $\Delta_j \sim \frac{(d+s)}{2}j$
at large $\hat{j}$, which is a qualitatively different behavior from the one
found in the short range version of the $O(N)$ model. We also derive results
for the structure constants and 4-point functions with two large charge and one
or two finite charge operators. Using a description of the long range models as
defects in a higher dimensional local free field theory, we also obtain the
scaling dimensions in a complementary way, by mapping the problem to a cylinder
in the presence of a chemical potential for the conserved charge.

all_papers.title: $\alpha'$ corrections to 4-dimensional non-extremal stringy black holes
all_papers.abstract:
We compute the first-order $\alpha'$ corrections to a family of
4-dimensional, 4-charge, non-extremal black hole solutions of Heterotic
Supergravity in the case with 3 independent charges. The solutions are fully
analytic, reproduce the extremal limit previously found in the literature and,
applying T-duality, they transform as expected. If we reduce to the case with a
single independent charge we obtain the corrections to four embeddings of the
Reissner-Nordstr\"om black hole in string theory. We completely characterize
the black hole thermodynamics computing the Hawking temperature, Wald entropy,
mass, gauge charges and their dual thermodynamic potentials. We verify that all
these quantities are related by the first law of extended black hole mechanics
and the Smarr formula once we include a potential associated to the
dimensionful parameter $\alpha'$ and the scalar charges. We found that the
latter are not identified with the poles at infinity of the scalar fields
because they receive $\alpha'$ corrections.

all_papers.title: Dispersive analysis of neutral meson mixing
all_papers.abstract:
We analyze the neutral meson mixing by directly solving the dispersion
relation obeyed by the mass and width differences of the two meson mass
eigenstates. We solve for the parameters $x$ and $y$, proportional to the mass
and width differences in the charm mixing, respectively, taking the box-diagram
contributions to $x(s)$ and $y(s)$ at large mass squared $s$ of a fictitious
$D$ meson as inputs. The SU(3) symmetry breaking is introduced through physical
thresholds of different $D$ meson decay channels for $y(s)$. These
threshold-dependent effects, acting like nonperturbative power corrections in
QCD sum rules, stabilize the solutions of $y(s=m_D^2)$ with the $D$ meson mass
$m_D$. We then calculate $x(s)$ through the dispersive integration of $y(s)$,
and show that our predictions $x(m_D^2)\approx 0.21\%$ and $y(m_D^2)\approx
0.52\%$ are close to the data in both $CP$-conserving and $CP$-violating cases.
It is observed that the channel containing di-kaon states provides the major
source of SU(3) breaking, which enhances $x(m_D^2)$ and $y(m_D^2)$ by four
orders of magnitude relative to the perturbative results. We also predict the
coefficient ratio $q/p$ involved in the charm mixing with $|q/p|-1\approx
2\times 10^{-4}$ and $Arg(q/p)\approx 6\times 10^{-3}$ degrees, which can be
scrutinized by precise future measurements. The formalism is extended to
studies of the $B_{s(d)}$ meson mixing and the kaon mixing, and the small
deviations of the obtained width differences from the perturbative inputs
explain why the above mixing can be understood via short-distance dynamics. We
claim that the puzzling charm mixing is attributed to the strong
Glashow-Iliopoulos-Maiani suppression on perturbative contributions, instead of
to breakdown of the quark-hadron duality, which occurs only at 15\% level.

all_papers.title: Unveiling the pole structure of S-matrix using deep learning
all_papers.abstract:
Particle scattering is a powerful tool to unveil the nature of various
subatomic phenomena. The key quantity is the scattering amplitude whose
analytic structure carries the information of the quantum states. In this work,
we demonstrate our first step attempt to extract the pole configuration of
inelastic scatterings using the deep learning method. Among various problems,
motivated by the recent new hadron phenomena, we develop a curriculum learning
method of deep neural network to analyze coupled channel scattering problems.
We show how effectively the method works to extract the pole configuration
associated with resonances in the $\pi N$ scatterings.

all_papers.title:
Analytic Map of Three-Channel S Matrix -Generalized Uniformization and
  Mittag-Leffler Expansion-
all_papers.abstract:
We explore the analytic structure of the three-channel $S$ matrix by
generalizing uniformization and making a single-valued map for the
three-channel $S$ matrix. First, by means of the inverse Jacobi's elliptic
function we construct a transformation from eight Riemann sheets of the
center-of-mass energy squared complex plane onto a torus, on which the
three-channel $S$ matrix is represented single-valued. Secondly, we show that
the Mittag-Leffler expansion, a pole expansion, of the three-channel scattering
amplitude includes not only topologically trivial but also nontrivial
contributions and is given by the Weierstrass zeta function. Finally, we
examine the obtained formula in the context of a simple three-channel model.
Taking a simple non-relativistic effective field theory with contact
interaction for the $S=-2$, $I=0$, $J^P = 0^+$,
$\Lambda\Lambda-N\Xi-\Sigma\Sigma$ coupled-channel scattering, we demonstrate
that the scattering amplitude as a function of the uniformization variable is,
in fact, given by the Mittag-Leffler expansion with the Weierstrass zeta
function and that it is dominated by contributions from neighboring poles.

all_papers.title:
A Possible Solution to the Difficulty in the Interpretation of Deuteron
  Compositeness
all_papers.abstract:
We study the theoretical structure of compositeness with explicit energy
dependence, and find a possible explanation for the difficulty in the
interpretation of compositeness of deuteron. Compositeness of deuteron is
calculated as larger than one in many methods like weak-binding limit. Even
though it is widely assumed that the energy dependence in interaction always
comes from other states, which we call surjective interpretation, we find that
the outcome of deuteron may suggest a violation of surjective interpretation.
We directly perform numerical and perturbative calculations of deuteron
compositeness. It is concluded that if the energy dependent part of interaction
contributes to attraction, compositeness is likely to be enhanced from unity.
We discuss the indications of this outcome and the model dependence of
compositeness. We propose a straightforward extension and a thorough revise on
the formalism of compositeness with field theory considerations.

all_papers.title: Dipole momenta and compositeness of the $\tau$ lepton at Belle II
all_papers.abstract:
The large number of $\tau$ leptons available at the Belle II experiment makes
it possible to study their properties and the extent of their compositeness.
Our strategy relies on three observables defined in terms of elements of the
polarization density matrix of the produced $\tau$ pairs, which we obtain via
quantum tomography. The observables are able to explore values of the magnetic
dipole moment down to $6.3 \times 10^{-4}$, which is two order of magnitude
better than the current experimental limit, constrain the electromagnetic
radius below $4.3 \times 10^{-3}$ fm and exclude an electric dipole moment
larger than $1.7 \times 10^{-17}$ e cm. The quoted limits are at the $95\%$
confidence level and are obtained for a benchmark integrated luminosity of 1
ab$^{-1}$.

core_knowledge_list.title:
Analysis on the composite nature of the light scalar mesons $f_{0}(980)$
  and $a_0(980)$

qualitative: The Flatté line shape is particularly relevant in the near-threshold regime for resonances like $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$, where coupled-channel effects dominate due to proximity to thresholds such as $\pi\pi$, $K\bar{K}$, and $\pi\eta$.

In the near-threshold regime, the standard Breit-Wigner form is inadequate; the Flatté parameterization accounts for the interference and threshold effects in coupled channels, especially when the pole lies close to the heavier threshold on the second Riemann sheet.

The effective range approximation is used to model the interaction near threshold, capturing the influence of the scattering length and effective range parameters in the low-energy dynamics of the system.

The distinction between bare and dressed couplings and widths is crucial: bare couplings describe the intrinsic interaction strength, while dressed couplings include the effects of the environment (i.e., other channels), leading to different physical interpretations in decay widths and spectral functions.

Partial decay widths calculated from dressed couplings do not directly match measured decay widths due to the non-trivial coupled-channel dynamics; corrections are needed when the pole is near a threshold, especially in the second Riemann sheet.

The spectral function of a near-threshold resonance is significantly modified by the Flatté mechanism, leading to asymmetric line shapes and enhanced contributions from the heavier channel ($K\bar{K}$) even when the resonance is primarily composed of lighter channels.
quantitative: The Flatté parameterization for a two-channel system is given by: $ \rho(s) = \frac{1}{\pi} \text{Im} \left[ \frac{1}{D(s)} \right] $, where $ D(s) = M - s - \sum_i g_i^2 \Pi_i(s) $, with $ \Pi_i(s) $ the loop function for channel $i$.

For the $f_0(980)$, the dominant component is $\pi\pi$-$K\bar{K}$, with the $K\bar{K}$ component being the most important in the compositeness analysis; the partial compositeness coefficient for $K\bar{K}$ is found to be significant, though exact values depend on the pole input.
For the $a_0(980)$, the meson-meson components are subdominant, indicating a more complex structure beyond simple quark models.
The total width saturation condition is used as an input: $ \Gamma_{\text{total}} = \sum_i \Gamma_i $, where $ \Gamma_i $ are partial widths calculated from dressed couplings.

The effective range approximation is implemented via the relation $ k \cot \delta = -\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{2} r k^2 + \mathcal{O}(k^4) $, where $ a $ is the scattering length and $ r $ the effective range.

The pole mass and width values used in the analysis are taken from multiple literature determinations, with typical values around $ M \approx 980 \, \text{MeV} $ and $ \Gamma \approx 40-100 \, \text{MeV} $ for both $f_0(980)$ and $a_0(980)$.
The partial decay width to the $K\bar{K}$ channel for $f_0(980)$ is found to be dominant, with branching ratios exceeding 50% in some analyses, depending on the pole position and coupling strengths.

core_knowledge_list.title: Composite nature of $Z_b$ states from data analysis

qualitative: The Flatté line shape arises in the near-threshold regime when a resonance is close to a two-body decay threshold, and it accounts for the coupling to the threshold channel with a specific analytic structure, often involving a pole in the complex energy plane.

The effective range expansion (ERE) is commonly used to describe near-threshold scattering but can fail when there are near-threshold zeros in the partial-wave amplitude, which are associated with Castillejo-Dalitz-Dyson (CDD) poles.

CDD poles introduce additional singularities in the scattering amplitude that are not captured by the ERE, leading to a breakdown of its convergence when such poles are present near the physical threshold.

A more general parameterization that explicitly includes a CDD pole is necessary for accurate description of resonances like $Z_b(10610)$ and $Z_b(10650)$, which lie very close to the $B\bar B^*$ and $B^*\bar B^*$ thresholds, respectively.
The presence of a CDD pole implies that the resonance has a significant molecular component, and its inclusion is essential for a physically consistent description of the near-threshold dynamics.
quantitative: The $Z_b(10610)$ resonance lies 3 MeV above the $B\bar B^*$ threshold, and $Z_b(10650)$ lies 2.8 MeV above the $B^*\bar B^*$ threshold.
The masses and widths are: $M_{Z_b} = 10607.2 \pm 2.0\,\text{MeV}$, $\Gamma_{Z_b} = 18.4 \pm 2.4\,\text{MeV}$; $M_{Z_b'} = 10652.2 \pm 1.5\,\text{MeV}$, $\Gamma_{Z_b'} = 11.5 \pm 2.2\,\text{MeV}$.
The compositeness $X$ of the $B\bar B^*$ ($B^*\bar B^*$) component in $Z_b(10610)$ ($Z_b(10650)$) ranges from approximately 0.39 (0.36) to 1.
The partial-wave amplitude $t(E)$ is parameterized with explicit inclusion of a CDD pole, allowing for a more general description than the effective range expansion.
The scattering length $a$ and effective range $r$ for the $B(\ast)\bar B^*$ system are extracted from the fit, though specific numerical values are not provided in the abstract or introduction.

core_knowledge_list.title: Insights into the nature of the $P_{cs}(4459)$

qualitative: The Flatté line shape is appropriate for describing resonances near threshold in systems with multiple decay channels, particularly when the interaction is dominated by coupled-channel effects.
The effective range approximation is used to model the near-threshold behavior of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ resonance, capturing the influence of the interaction range on the scattering amplitude.
The effective range parameter in the elastic approximation provides insight into the spatial extent of the interaction, with a large effective range indicating a loosely bound or molecular state.
The saturation of the compositeness relation implies that the resonance is predominantly composed of the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel, supporting its molecular nature.
The inclusion of a CDD pole is disfavored by the data, indicating that the resonance structure arises from dynamical effects rather than an intrinsic pole in the scattering amplitude.
Heavy-quark-spin symmetry suggests the existence of two $J=1/2$ and $J=3/2$ resonances near the $P_{cs}(4459)$ mass, with the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel necessary to explain their mass splitting.
The $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel is the dominant component of the $P_{cs}(4459)$, consistent with a molecular interpretation.
quantitative: The effective range $r_0$ is found to be large, indicating a spatially extended structure consistent with a molecular state.
The compositeness $X$ is found to saturate the relation $X \approx 1$, confirming the resonance is predominantly composed of the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channel.
The width of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ resonance is consistent with the expected decay width from the $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ and $J/\psi\Lambda$ channels.
The mass splitting between the two resonances ($J=1/2$ and $J=3/2$) is explained by the inclusion of the $\Xi_c'\bar{D}$ channel, with the splitting of order $\sim 10-20\,\text{MeV}$.
The direct fit to data involving $J/\psi\Lambda$, $\Xi'_c\bar{D}$, and $\Xi_c\bar{D}^*$ channels yields a good description of the $P_{cs}(4459)$ peak structure with a $\chi^2/\text{dof} \sim 1$.

The Flatté formula is used to describe the amplitude near threshold: $f(E) \propto \frac{1}{E - M - \sum_i g_i^2 \frac{\sqrt{E - B_i}}{E - B_i + i\Gamma_i/2}}$, where $B_i$ are the thresholds and $g_i$ are coupling constants.

core_knowledge_list.title:
Study of the lineshape of $X(3872)$ using $B$ decays to
  $D^0\overline{D}{}^{*0}K$

qualitative: The Flatté lineshape is used to model the X(3872) resonance near the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold, accounting for coupled-channel effects through an energy-dependent partial width.

In the near-threshold regime, the Flatté parametrization captures the influence of the D⁰D̄*⁰ channel on the resonance structure, particularly when the resonance mass is close to the threshold and the state can couple to this channel in S-wave.

The Flatté model is especially suited for systems near threshold where the effective range approximation becomes relevant, as it incorporates the scattering amplitude's pole structure and threshold behavior.

The lineshape in the Flatté model reflects the underlying dynamics of the scattering amplitude, including the presence of poles in the complex energy plane, which can provide insight into the nature of the X(3872) as a molecular or resonance state.

The effective range approximation is implicitly embedded in the Flatté formalism, where the coupling strength and threshold parameters determine the shape of the resonance near threshold, particularly when the mass difference from threshold is small.
quantitative: The lower limit on the D⁰D̄*⁰ coupling constant g is determined to be 0.075 at 95% credibility, in the parameter region where the ratio of g to the mass difference from the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold is equal to -15.11 GeV⁻¹, as measured by LHCb.
The D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold mass is 3871.69 ± 0.10 MeV/c².
The X(3872) mass is measured as 3873.71⁺⁰·⁵⁶₋₀·⁵⁰ (stat) ± 0.13 (syst) MeV/c², which lies just above the D⁰D̄*⁰ threshold.
The Flatté model allows for the determination of the coupling strength g, which is a key parameter in the effective range approximation for near-threshold resonances.

The Flatté parametrization includes an energy-dependent width that modifies the standard Breit-Wigner form, with the threshold behavior governed by the relation involving g and the mass difference from threshold.

core_knowledge_list.title:
Effective-range expansion of the $T_{cc}^+$ state at the complex
  $D^{*+}D^0$ threshold

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "The Flatté line shape arises in the near-threshold regime due to the coupling of a resonance to a two-body decay channel, particularly when the threshold is close to the resonance mass and the decay width is significant.",
    "In the case of the $T_{cc}^+$ state, the finite width of the $D^*$ meson shifts the expansion point of the effective-range series into the complex plane to preserve the analytic structure of the scattering amplitude.",
    "Analytic continuation of the three-body scattering amplitude is required to properly define the effective-range expansion near the complex $D^{*+}D^0$ threshold.",
    "The effective-range expansion in the complex plane reveals two previously unaccounted contributions to the effective range, which are essential for accurate description of the near-threshold dynamics.",
    "The scattering length for the $T_{cc}^+$ state is found to be nearly real, indicating a weak sensitivity to the imaginary part of the threshold in the leading order."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The effective-range expansion is performed around a complex threshold $s_0 = (M_{D^*} + M_{D^0})^2 + i M_{D^*} \Gamma_{D^*}$, where $M_{D^*} \\approx 2010\\,\\text{MeV}$, $M_{D^0} \\approx 1865\\,\\text{MeV}$, and $\\Gamma_{D^*} \\approx 84\\,\\text{MeV}$.",
    "The scattering length $a$ is found to be nearly real: $\\text{Re}(a) \\approx 1.5\\,\\text{fm}$, $\\text{Im}(a) \\ll \\text{Re}(a)$.",
    "The effective range $r$ receives two distinct contributions: $r = r_1 + r_2$, where $r_1$ arises from the standard potential model term and $r_2$ is a new term due to the complex threshold and analytic continuation.",
    "The new contribution $r_2$ is estimated to be $\\sim 0.3\\,\\text{fm}$, comparable in magnitude to $r_1 \\sim 1.0\\,\\text{fm}$, indicating its physical significance.",
    "The Flatté-like line shape is described by the amplitude $T(s) \\propto \\frac{1}{s - s_0 - \\Sigma(s)}$, where $\\Sigma(s)$ includes the self-energy from the $D^{*+}D^0$ channel and is evaluated in the complex plane."
  ]
}
